Cloudflare products"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/analytics.yaml"],"digest":[0,"6c75b86c47c24d61"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Analytics"]]]}],"body":[0,"This tutorial explains how to analyze Cloudflare metrics using the [New Relic One Cloudflare Quickstart](https://newrelic.com/instant-observability/cloudflare/fc2bb0ac-6622-43c6-8c1f-6a4c26ab5434).\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nBefore sending your Cloudflare log data to New Relic, make sure that you:\n\n- Have a Cloudflare Enterprise account with Cloudflare Logs enabled.\n- Have a New Relic account.\n- Configure [Logpush to New Relic](/logs/logpush/logpush-job/enable-destinations/new-relic/).\n\n## Task 1 - Install the Cloudflare Network Logs quickstart\n\n1. Log in to New Relic.\n2. Click the Instant Observability button (top right).\n3. Search for **Cloudflare Network Logs**.\n\n![Cloudflare Network Logs install screen](~/assets/images/fundamentals/new-relic/screenshots/cloudflare-network-logs.png)\n\n4. Click **Install this quickstart**.\n5. Follow the steps to deploy.\n\n## Task 2 - View the Cloudflare Dashboards\n\nYou can view your dashboards on the New Relic dashboard page. The dashboards include the following information:\n\n### Overview\n\nGet a quick overview of the most important metrics from your websites and applications on the Cloudflare network.\n\n![Cloudflare Network Logs install screen](~/assets/images/fundamentals/new-relic/dashboard/dash-1.png)\n\n### Security\n\nGet insights on threats to your websites and applications, including number of threats taken action on by the Web Application Firewall (WAF), threats over time, top threat countries, and more.\n\n![Cloudflare Network security metrics screen](~/assets/images/fundamentals/new-relic/dashboard/dash-2.png)\n\n### Performance\n\nIdentify and address performance issues and caching misconfigurations. Metrics include total requests, total versus cached requests, total versus origin requests.\n\n![Cloudflare Network Logs performance metrics screen](~/assets/images/fundamentals/new-relic/dashboard/dash-3.png)\n\n### Reliability\n\nGet insights on the availability of your websites and Applications. Metrics include, edge response status over time, percentage of `3xx`/`4xx`/`5xx` errors over time, and more.\n\n![Cloudflare Network Logs reliability metrics screen](~/assets/images/fundamentals/new-relic/dashboard/dash-4.png)"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/analytics/analytics-integrations/new-relic.mdx"],"digest":[0,"676a9e78c195a383"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"analytics/analytics-integrations/splunk"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Splunk"],"description":[0,"This tutorial explains how to analyze Cloudflare Logs using the Cloudflare App for Splunk."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,104],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"analytics"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"carRkM"],"name":[0,"Analytics"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Analytics"],"url":[0,"/analytics/"],"group":[0,"Core platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Analytics"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Analytics docs"],"description":[0,"Visualize the performance, security, and reliability data collected by Cloudflare products"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/analytics.yaml"],"digest":[0,"6c75b86c47c24d61"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Analytics"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render } from \"~/components\";\n\nThis tutorial explains how to analyze [Cloudflare Logs](https://www.cloudflare.com/products/cloudflare-logs/) using the [Cloudflare App for Splunk](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4501/).\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nBefore sending your Cloudflare log data to Splunk, ensure that you:\n\n- Have an existing Splunk Enterprise or Cloud account\n- Have a Cloudflare Enterprise account\n- Consult the [Splunk documentation](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4501/) for the Cloudflare App\n\n## Task 1 - Install and Configure the Cloudflare App for Splunk\n\nTo install the [Cloudflare App for Splunk](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4501/):\n\n1. Log in to your Splunk instance.\n2. Under **Apps** > **Find More Apps**, search for _Cloudflare App for Splunk._\n3. Click **Install**.\n\n![Splunk website with Apps menu expanded and Search & Reporting menu item along with Cloudflare App for Splunk](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/screenshots/splunk-cloudflare-app-for-splunk.png)\n\n4. Restart and reopen your Splunk instance.\n\n5. Edit the `cloudflare:json` source type in the Cloudflare App for Splunk. To edit the source type:\n   1. Click the **Settings** dropdown and select **Source types**.\n   2. Uncheck **Show only popular** and search for _cloudflare_.\n   3. Click **Edit** and change the Regex expression to `([\\r\\n]+)`.\n   4. Save your edits.\n\n6. Create an index on Splunk to store the HTTP Event logs. To create an index:\n   1. Open the setup screen by clicking the **Settings** dropdown, then click **Indexes**.\n   2. Select **New Index**. Note that the **Indexes** page also gives you the status of all your existing indexes so that you can see whether you're about to use up your licensed amount of space.\n   3. Name the index **cloudflare**, which is the default index that the Cloudflare App will use.\n\n7. Set up the HTTP Event Collector (HEC) on Splunk. To create an HEC:\n   1. Click the **Settings** dropdown and select **Data inputs**.\n   2. Click **+Add new** and follow the wizard. When prompted, submit the following responses:\n      - Name: Cloudflare\n      - Source Type: Select > \"cloudflare:json\"\n      - App Context: Cloudflare App for Splunk (cloudflare)\n      - Index: cloudflare\n   3. At the end of the wizard you will see a **Token Value**. This token authorizes the Cloudflare Logpush job to send data to your Splunk instance. If you forget to copy it now, Splunk allows you to get the value at any time.\n\n8. Verify whether Splunk is using a self-signed certificate. You'll need this information when creating the Logpush job.\n\n9. Determine the endpoint to use to send the data to. The endpoint should be:\n\n```sql\n\"<protocol>://input-<host>:<port>/<endpoint>\" or \"<protocol>://http-inputs-<host>:<port>/<endpoint>\"\n```\n\nWhere:\n\n- `protocol`: HTTP or HTTPS\n- `input`: `input` or `http-inputs` based on whether you have a self-service or managed cloud plan\n- `host`: The hostname of your Splunk instance. The easiest way to determine the hostname is to look at the URL you went to when you logged in to Splunk.\n- `port`: 443 or 8088\n- `endpoint`: services/collector/raw\n\nFor example: `https://prd-p-0qk3h.splunkcloud.com:8088/services/collector/raw`. Refer to the [Splunk Documentation](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/SplunkCloud/latest/Data/UsetheHTTPEventCollector) for more details and examples.\n\n**Post Installation Notes**\n\nYou can change the **Index Name** after the initial configuration by clicking on the **Settings** dropdown and navigating to **Advanced search**. There you can select **Search macros** and look for the Cloudflare App for Splunk.\n\n![Splunk interface highlighting Apps menu and Manage Apps option along with Enable Acceleration checkbox](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/screenshots/splunk-settings-advanced-search-search-macros.png)\n\nThe Cloudflare App for Splunk comes with a custom Cloudflare Data Model that has an acceleration time frame of 1 day but is not accelerated by default. If you enable [Data Model acceleration](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/Splunk/latest/Knowledge/Acceleratedatamodels), we recommend that the Data Model is only accelerated for 1 or 7 days to ensure there are no adverse effects within your Splunk environment.\n\nEnable or disable acceleration after the initial configuration by accessing the app Set up page by clicking the **Apps** dropdown, then **Manage Apps** > **Cloudflare Set Up**.\n\n![Splunk Advanced Search page highlighted Search macros and Advanced search](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/screenshots/splunk-apps-manage-apps-cloudflare-set-up-enable-data-model-acceleration.png)\n\nYou can also manually configure Data Models by going to **Settings** > **Data models**. Learn more about data model acceleration in the [Splunk documentation](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/Splunk/latest/Knowledge/Acceleratedatamodels).\n\n## Task 2 - Make the API call to create the Logpush job\n\nCreate the Logpush job by following the instructions on [Enable Logpush to Splunk](/logs/logpush/logpush-job/enable-destinations/splunk/). The API call creates a Logpush job but does not enable it.\n\nEnable the Logpush job through the Cloudflare dashboard or through the API by following the instructions on [Enable Logpush to Splunk](/logs/logpush/logpush-job/enable-destinations/splunk/). To enable through the dashboard:\n\n1. Navigate to the Cloudflare dashboard and select **Analytics & Logs** > **Logs**.\n2. Select **Edit** and select the fields referenced in the Dashboard section below to fully populate all tables and graphs.\n3. Enable the Logpush job by toggling on the switch next to the Edit link. Data takes a few minutes to populate.\n\nTo validate that you are receiving data, search `index=cloudflare` in Splunk.\n\n## Task 3 - View the Dashboards\n\nYou can analyze Cloudflare logs with the thirteen (13) dashboards listed below.\n\nYou can use filters within these dashboards to help narrow the analysis by date and time, device type, country, user agent, client IP, hostname, and more to further help with debugging and tracing.\n\n### About the Dashboards\n\nThe following dashboards outlined below are available as part of the Cloudflare App for Splunk.\n\n#### Cloudflare - Snapshot\n\n_Web Traffic Overview_ and _Web Traffic Types_: Get an overview of the most important metrics from your websites and applications on the Cloudflare network.\n![Splunk dashboard with Web Traffic Overview metrics](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-snapshot-dashboard.png)\n\n#### Cloudflare - Reliability\n\n_Summary_ and _Detailed_: Get insights on the availability of your websites and applications. Metrics include origin response error ratio, origin response status over time, percentage of 3xx/4xx/5xx errors over time, and more.\n![Splunk dashboard with a high level summary of Reliability metrics](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-reliability-summary-dashboard.png)\n\n![Splunk dashboard with a detailed summary of Reliability metrics](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-reliability-detailed-dashboard.png)\n\n#### Cloudflare - Security\n\n_Overview_: Get insights on threats to your websites and applications, including number of threats stopped, threats over time, top threat countries, and more.\n![Splunk dashboard with an overview of Security metrics](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-security-overview.png)\n\n_WAF_: Get insights on threat identification and mitigation by our Web Application Firewall, including events like SQL injections, XSS, and more. Use this data to fine tune the firewall to target obvious threats and prevent false positives.\n![Splunk dashboard with an overview of Security metrics for WAF](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-security-waf-dashboard.png)\n\n_Rate Limiting_: Get insights on rate limiting protection against denial-of-service attacks, brute-force login attempts, and other types of abusive behavior targeted at your websites or applications.\n![Splunk dashboard with an overview of Security metrics for Rate Limiting](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-security-rate-limiting-dashboard.png)\n\n_Bots Summary_ and _Bots Detailed_: Investigate bot activity on your website to prevent content scraping, checkout fraud, spam registration and other malicious activities.\n![Splunk dashboard with a high level summary of Security metrics for Bots](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-security-bot-summary-dashboard.png)\n\n![Splunk dashboard with a detailed summary of Security metrics for Bots](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-security-bots-detailed-dashboard.png)\n\n#### Cloudflare - Performance\n\n_Requests and Cache_ and _Bandwidth_: Identify and address performance issues and caching misconfigurations. Metrics include total vs. cached bandwidth, saved bandwidth, total requests, cache ratio, top uncached requests, and more.\n![Splunk dashboard with Performance metrics for Requests and Cache](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-performance-requests-and-cache-dashboard.png)\n\n![Splunk dashboard with Performance metrics for Bandwidth](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-performance-bandwidth-dashboard.png)\n\n_Hostname, Content Type, Request Methods, Connection Type_: Get insights into your most popular hostnames, most requested content types, breakdown of request methods, and connection type.\n\n![Splunk dashboard with Cloudflare Performance metrics including for Hostname, Content Type, Request Methods, Connection Type](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-performance-hostname-dashboard.png)\n\n_Static vs. Dynamic Content_: Get insights into the performance of your static and dynamic content, including slowest URLs.\n![Splunk dashboard with Cloudflare Performance metrics for Static vs. Dynamic Content](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/dashboards/splunk-cloudflare-performance-static-vs-dynamic-dashboard.png)\n\n### Filters\n\nAll dashboard have a set of filters that you can apply to the entire dashboard, as shown in the following example. Filters are applied across the entire dashboard.\n\n![Available dashboard filters from the Splunk dashboard](~/assets/images/fundamentals/splunk/screenshots/splunk-filters.png)\n\nYou can use filters to drill down and examine the data at a granular level. Filters include client country, client device type, client IP, client request host, client request URI, client request user agent, edge response status, origin IP, and origin response status.\n\nThe default time interval is set to 24 hours. Note that for correct calculations filter will need to exclude Worker subrequests (**WorkerSubrequest** = _false_) and purge requests (**ClientRequestMethod** is not _PURGE_).\n\nAvailable Filters:\n\n- Time Range (EdgeStartTimestamp)\n\n- Client Country\n\n- Client Device type\n\n- Client IP\n\n- Client Request Host\n\n- Client Request URI\n\n- Client Request User Agent\n\n- Edge response status\n\n- Origin IP\n\n- Origin Response Status\n\n- RayID\n\n- Worker Subrequest\n\n- Client Request Method\n\n## Debugging tips\n\n### Incomplete dashboards\n\nThe Splunk Cloudflare App relies on data from the Cloudflare Enterprise Logs fields outlined below. Depending on which fields you have enabled, certain dashboards might not populate fully.\n\nIf that is the case, verify and test the Cloudflare App filters below each dashboard (these filters are the same across all dashboards). You can delete any filters that you do not need, even if such filters include data fields already contained in your logs.\n\nAlso, you could compare the list of fields you are getting in Cloudflare Logs with the fields listed in **Splunk** > **Settings** > **Data Model** > **Cloudflare**.\n\nThe available fields are:\n\n- CacheCacheStatus\n\n- CacheResponseBytes\n\n- CacheResponseStatus (deprecated)\n\n- ClientASN\n\n- ClientCountry\n\n- ClientDeviceType\n\n- ClientIP\n\n- ClientIPClass\n\n- ClientRequestBytes\n\n- ClientRequestHost\n\n- ClientRequestMethod\n\n- ClientRequestPath\n\n- ClientRequestProtocol\n\n- ClientRequestReferer\n\n- ClientRequestURI\n\n- ClientRequestUserAgent\n\n- ClientSSLCipher\n\n- ClientSSLProtocol\n\n- ClientSrcPort\n\n- EdgeColoCode\n\n- EdgeColoID\n\n- EdgeEndTimestamp\n\n- EdgePathingOp\n\n- EdgePathingSrc\n\n- EdgePathingStatus\n\n- EdgeRequestHost\n\n- EdgeResponseBytes\n\n- EdgeResponseContentType\n\n- EdgeResponseStatus\n\n- EdgeServerIP\n\n- EdgeStartTimestamp\n\n- OriginIP\n\n- OriginResponseStatus\n\n- OriginResponseTime\n\n- OriginSSLProtocol\n\n- RayID\n\n- SecurityAction\n\n- SecurityActions\n\n- SecurityRuleDescription\n\n- SecurityRuleID\n\n- SecurityRuleIDs\n\n- SecuritySources\n\n- WAFFlags\n\n- WAFMatchedVar\n\n- WorkerSubrequest\n\n- ZoneID"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/analytics/analytics-integrations/splunk.mdx"],"digest":[0,"49416ca22af90001"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"automatic-platform-optimization/get-started/activate-cf-wp-plugin"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Activate the Cloudflare WordPress plugin"],"description":[0,"The easiest way to begin using APO is directly from Cloudflare’s WordPress plugin. Before you can use APO, you must first install and activate the plugin and then activate APO."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,8],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"automatic-platform-optimization"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"yeDEnx"],"name":[0,"Automatic Platform Optimization"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Automatic Platform Optimization"],"url":[0,"/automatic-platform-optimization/"],"wrap":[0,true],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Automatic Platform Optimization docs"],"description":[0,"Improve WordPress site performance by serving it from Cloudflare's global network"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/automatic-platform-optimization.yaml"],"digest":[0,"55f5c7c8603f7f4c"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Automatic Platform Optimization"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nAfter you [change your nameservers](/automatic-platform-optimization/get-started/change-nameservers/), activate the Cloudflare WordPress plugin.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nBefore activating the Cloudflare WordPress plugin, review the following prerequisites.\n\n### Plan type\n\nFor users on the free plan, [purchase APO](#purchase-apo) before installing the WordPress plugin.\n\nFor users on a Pro plan or higher, continue to [Install and activate](#install-and-activate-the-cloudflare-wordpress-plugin) the Cloudflare WordPress plugin.\n\n### Plugin compatibility\n\nCloudflare recommends turning off plugins such as WP Rocket Cache Plugin, W3 Total Cache, or similar plugins when first setting up APO. After confirming APO is working, we recommend testing whether turning on the plugins listed above improves results or causes unexpected behavior. In many cases, using APO along with other caching plugins can cause unexpected results.\n\nWe also recommend clearing the server cache for the WP Rocket Cache plugin, W3 Total Cache, or similar plugins after APO activation.\n\nFor more details, refer to [Plugin compatibility](/automatic-platform-optimization/about/plugin-compatibility/).\n\n### Limitations\n\nThe Cloudflare APO WordPress plugin does not support multisite WordPress installation.\n\n## Purchase APO\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Speed** > **Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/speed/optimization\" />\n\n2. Go to **Content Optimization**.\n3. For **Automatic Platform Optimization for WordPress**, select **Purchase**.\n4. Enter your payment information and select **Confirm payment**.\n\n## Install and activate the Cloudflare WordPress plugin\n\nThe easiest way to begin using APO is directly from Cloudflare’s WordPress plugin. Before you can use APO, you must first install and activate the plugin and then activate APO.\n\n1. Navigate and log in to your WordPress account.\n2. Select **Plugins** > **Add new**.\n3. In the search field, enter `Cloudflare`.\n4. Locate the Cloudflare plugin and select **Install now**.\n5. After the plugin finishes installing, select **Activate**. The Cloudflare plugin now displays in your Plugins list.\n\n## Activate APO\n\nTo create the connection between WordPress and Cloudflare, you will create an API token from your Cloudflare dashboard and add it to WordPress. To set up APO on a subdomain, refer to [Subdomains and subdirectories](/automatic-platform-optimization/reference/subdomain-subdirectories/).\n\n## Create the API token from Cloudflare\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Account API tokens** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/api-tokens\" />\n\n2. Select **Create Token**.\n3. Locate **WordPress** from the list and select **Use template**.\n4. Select **Continue to summary** at the bottom of the page.\n5. On the **WordPress API token summary** page, select **Create Token**. Your API token displays.\n6. Select the **Copy** button to copy your token. You will need to paste the token in the next section.\n\n:::note\n\nCopy and paste your API token into a document saved on your computer to easily reference it again.\n\n:::\n\n## Add your API token to WordPress\n\n1. Open your WordPress account and navigate to Plugins.\n2. Locate the Cloudflare plugin and select **Settings**.\n3. Select the option to sign in with an existing account.\n4. Enter your email address and paste the token you copied in Step 7 of Create the API token from Cloudflare.\n5. Select **Save API Credentials**.\n6. For **Apply Recommended Cloudflare Settings for WordPress**, select **Apply**.\n7. For **Automatic Platform Optimization**, switch the toggle to **On** to enable APO.\n\nTo verify APO is working, see [Verify APO works](/automatic-platform-optimization/get-started/verify-apo-works/)."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/automatic-platform-optimization/get-started/activate-cf-wp-plugin.mdx"],"digest":[0,"13502ad38cd8f39e"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"byoip/service-bindings/cdn-and-spectrum"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Use BYOIP with CDN and Spectrum"],"description":[0,"Cloudflare allows users to use their Cloudflare prefix to route traffic to a different service. Service bindings must be created on the parent account of the prefix."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,4],"label":[0,"CDN and Spectrum"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"byoip"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"ya3hLV"],"name":[0,"BYOIP"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"BYOIP"],"url":[0,"/byoip/"],"group":[0,"Network security"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Application performance"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare BYOIP docs"],"description":[0,"Get Cloudflare's security and performance while using your own IPs"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/byoip.yaml"],"digest":[0,"c205ca10a04f393a"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"BYOIP"]]]}],"body":[0,"import {\n\tRender,\n\tAPIRequest,\n\tDetails,\n\tExample,\n\tTabItem,\n\tTabs,\n\tGlossaryTooltip,\n\tDashButton,\n} from \"~/components\";\n\nWith [service bindings](/byoip/service-bindings/), CDN[^1] customers using BYOIP can take the same prefix they have onboarded to Cloudflare and use it to selectively route traffic on a per-IP address basis to [Spectrum](/spectrum/)[^2], or vice versa. This means:\n\n- You can upgrade individual IPs within a CDN prefix to a Spectrum IP. For example, if you have a CDN prefix 203.0.113.0/24, you can upgrade 203.0.113.1 to Spectrum.\n\n- You can upgrade individual IPs within a Spectrum prefix to a CDN IP. For example, if you have a Spectrum prefix 203.0.113.0/24, you can upgrade 203.0.113.1 to CDN.\n\nThis guide will use the first example and consider a prefix that was onboarded to the CDN, with a few IPs upgraded to Spectrum.\n\n## Before you begin\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"service-bindings-prereqs\"\n\tproduct=\"byoip\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tpre_existing_product: \"CDN\",\n\t\tadded_product: \"Spectrum\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\n---\n\n## Prepare your IPs\n\n### 1. Get account information\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"service-bindings-account-info\"\n\tproduct=\"byoip\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tpre_existing_product: \"CDN\",\n\t\tadded_product: \"Spectrum\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\n### 2. Create service bindings\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"service-bindings-create-binding\"\n\tproduct=\"byoip\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tpre_existing_product: \"CDN\",\n\t\tadded_product: \"Spectrum\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\n### 3. Verify all service bindings\n\nAfter the propagation time (four to six hours), the [List Service Bindings](/api/resources/addressing/subresources/prefixes/subresources/service_bindings/methods/get/) endpoint should return all service bindings that are part of the prefix - in this case, CDN and Spectrum.\n\n<APIRequest\n\tpath=\"/accounts/{account_id}/addressing/prefixes/{prefix_id}/bindings\"\n\tmethod=\"GET\"\n/>\n\n---\n\n## Set up your Cloudflare services\n\n### CDN\n\nIf you already use BYOIP with CDN, you might be able to skip this step. However, if you are using this guide to upgrade a few IPs from a Spectrum prefix to the CDN, consider the following sections on [address maps](#address-maps) and [DNS records](#dns-records).\n\n:::note\nAs described below, address maps and DNS records do not apply to Spectrum. To set up your Spectrum application with BYOIP, refer to [Spectrum](#spectrum).\n:::\n\n#### Address maps\n\nUse <GlossaryTooltip term=\"address map\" link=\"/byoip/address-maps/\">address maps</GlossaryTooltip> to specify which IPs should be used by Cloudflare in DNS responses when a record is <GlossaryTooltip term=\"proxy status\" link=\"/dns/proxy-status/\">proxied</GlossaryTooltip>.\n\nYou can choose between two different scopes:\n\n- Account-level: uses the address map for all proxied DNS records across all of the zones within an account.\n- Zone-level: uses the address map for all proxied DNS records within a zone.\n\n:::note\nIf you need to map only specific subdomains (and not all proxied DNS records) to specific IP addresses, you can use a [Subdomain setup](/dns/zone-setups/subdomain-setup/).\n:::\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\">\n<TabItem label=\"Dashboard\" no-code=\"true\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Address Maps** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/ip-addresses/proxy-ips\" />\n\n2. Select **Create an address map**.\n3. Choose the scope of the address map.\n4. Add the zones and IP addresses that you want to map.\n5. Name your address map.\n6. Review the information and select **Save and Deploy**.\n\n</TabItem>\n<TabItem label=\"API\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nUse the [Create Address Map](/api/resources/addressing/subresources/address_maps/methods/create/) endpoint.\n\nMake sure you have the correct Key/Token and permissions.\n\n</TabItem>\n</Tabs>\n\n#### DNS records\n\nWhile the DNS record proxy status and address map will determine how Cloudflare's authoritative DNS responds to requests for your hostnames, the IP addresses specified in `A`/`AAAA` records will determine [how Cloudflare reaches the configured origin](/fundamentals/concepts/how-cloudflare-works/#how-cloudflare-works-as-a-reverse-proxy).\n\n:::note\nAs you create the necessary DNS records, [Total TLS](/ssl/edge-certificates/additional-options/total-tls/) can help making sure that you have SSL/TLS certificates in place for all your hostnames.\n:::\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\">\n<TabItem label=\"Dashboard\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nTo create a DNS record in the dashboard:\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Records** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records\" />\n\n2.  Select **Add record**.\n3.  Choose an address (`A`/`AAAA`) [record type](/dns/manage-dns-records/reference/dns-record-types/).\n4.  Complete the required fields, setting the **Proxy status** to **proxied**.\n5.  Select **Save**.\n\n</TabItem>\n<TabItem label=\"API\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nTo create records with the API, use a [POST request](/api/resources/dns/subresources/records/methods/create/). For field definitions, select a record type under the request body specification.\n\n</TabItem>\n</Tabs>\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\n| Type | Name  | IP address      | Proxy status | TTL    |\n| ---- | ----- | --------------- | ------------ | ------ |\n| `A`  | `www` | `203.0.113.150` | `Proxied`    | `Auto` |\n\nAt this point, if an address map for a zone `example.com` specifies that Cloudflare should use `203.0.113.100` for proxied records and the above record exists in the same zone, you can expect the following:\n\n1. Cloudflare responds to DNS requests for `www.example.com` with `203.0.113.100`.\n2. Cloudflare proxies requests through the CDN and then routes the requests to the origin server `203.0.113.150`.\n3. As the HTTP response egresses the Cloudflare network back to the client side, the source IP address of the response becomes `203.0.113.100` (the IP address that the HTTP request originally landed on).\n\n</Details>\n\n:::note\nHaving the same IP address as ingress IP (defined in the address map) and origin IP (listed in the DNS record) will not cause any loops.\n:::\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\nAssuming `203.0.113.100` was also the origin IP, the DNS record would look like the following:\n\n| Type | Name  | IP address      | Proxy status | TTL    |\n| ---- | ----- | --------------- | ------------ | ------ |\n| `A`  | `www` | `203.0.113.100` | `Proxied`    | `Auto` |\n\n</Details>\n\n### Spectrum\n\n<Render file=\"spectrum-udp-byoip-limitation\" product=\"byoip\" />\n\nConfiguring Spectrum to use your own IP address is only possible via the [Cloudflare API](/api/resources/spectrum/).\n\nThe `origin_direct` field takes the origin IP address, while `edge_ips` allows you to define which IP address from your BYOIP prefix Cloudflare should use to process requests for your Spectrum application.\n\n<APIRequest\n\tpath=\"/zones/{zone_id}/spectrum/apps\"\n\tmethod=\"POST\"\n\tjson={[\n\t\t{\n\t\t\tprotocol: \"tcp/22\",\n\t\t\tdns: {\n\t\t\t\ttype: \"CNAME\",\n\t\t\t\tname: \"ssh.example.com\",\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t\torigin_direct: [\"tcp://192.0.2.1:22\"],\n\t\t\tproxy_protocol: \"off\",\n\t\t\tip_firewall: true,\n\t\t\ttls: \"full\",\n\t\t\tedge_ips: {\n\t\t\t\ttype: \"static\",\n\t\t\t\tips: [\"203.0.113.18\"],\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t\ttraffic_type: \"direct\",\n\t\t},\n\t]}\n\tcode={{\n\t\tmark: [17, 18, 19, 20, 21, \"203.0.113.18\"],\n\t}}\n/>\n\n---\n\n## (Optional) Add layer 7 functionality\n\n<Render file=\"service-bindings-related-products\" product=\"byoip\" />\n\n[^1]: Layer 7 HTTP-based\n\n[^2]: Layer 4 or Layer 7 HTTP with custom ports"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/byoip/service-bindings/cdn-and-spectrum.mdx"],"digest":[0,"3b634e00aa376fa8"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"byoip/service-bindings/magic-transit-with-cdn"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Use BYOIP with Magic Transit and CDN"],"description":[0,"Service bindings allow BYOIP customers to selectively route traffic on a per-IP address basis to the CDN pipeline. It is important to note that traffic routed to the CDN pipeline is protected at Layers 3 and 4 by the inherent DDoS protection capabilities."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,3],"label":[0,"Magic Transit with CDN"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"byoip"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"ya3hLV"],"name":[0,"BYOIP"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"BYOIP"],"url":[0,"/byoip/"],"group":[0,"Network security"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Application performance"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare BYOIP docs"],"description":[0,"Get Cloudflare's security and performance while using your own IPs"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/byoip.yaml"],"digest":[0,"c205ca10a04f393a"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"BYOIP"]]]}],"body":[0,"import {\n\tDetails,\n\tExample,\n\tTabItem,\n\tTabs,\n\tGlossaryTooltip,\n\tAPIRequest,\n\tRender,\n\tDashButton,\n} from \"~/components\";\n\n[Magic Transit](/magic-transit/) customers using BYOIP can also benefit from the performance, reliability, and security that Cloudflare offers for HTTP-based applications. [Service bindings](/byoip/service-bindings/) allow BYOIP customers to selectively route traffic on a per-IP address basis to the CDN pipeline (which includes [Cache](/cache/), [Web Application Firewall (WAF)](/waf/), and more).\n\nThis guide covers using the Cloudflare API to configure Magic Transit with CDN. It is also possible to define service bindings to route traffic to the Spectrum pipeline selectively. Refer to [scope](/byoip/service-bindings/#scope) for the full list of possible configurations and other available guides.\n\nIt is important to note that traffic routed to the CDN pipeline is protected at Layers 3 and 4 by the inherent DDoS protection capabilities native to the CDN pipeline.\n\n## Before you begin\n\n- Make sure your contract includes CDN according to your needs. If you find any issues related to subscription when following the steps below, reach out to your account team.\n\n- Plan for what IPs will be used:\n\n    <Render\n    \tfile=\"service-bindings-prereqs\"\n    \tproduct=\"byoip\"\n    \tparams={{\n    \t\tpre_existing_product: \"Magic Transit\",\n    \t\tadded_product: \"CDN\",\n    \t}}\n    />\n\n## 1. Get account information\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"service-bindings-account-info\"\n\tproduct=\"byoip\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tpre_existing_product: \"Magic Transit\",\n\t\tadded_product: \"CDN\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\n## 2. Create service binding\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"service-bindings-create-binding\"\n\tproduct=\"byoip\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tpre_existing_product: \"Magic Transit\",\n\t\tadded_product: \"CDN\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\n## 3. Create address maps\n\nOnce you have configured your IPs to have CDN service, you can use <GlossaryTooltip term=\"address map\" link=\"/byoip/address-maps/\">address maps</GlossaryTooltip> to specify which IPs should be used by Cloudflare in DNS responses when a record is <GlossaryTooltip term=\"proxy status\" link=\"/dns/proxy-status/\">proxied</GlossaryTooltip>.\n\nYou can choose between two different scopes:\n\n- Account-level: uses the address map for all proxied DNS records across all of the zones within an account.\n- Zone-level: uses the address map for all proxied DNS records within a zone.\n\n:::tip\nIf you need to map only specific subdomains (and not all proxied DNS records) to specific IP addresses, you can use a zone on [Subdomain setup](/dns/zone-setups/subdomain-setup/).\n:::\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\">\n<TabItem label=\"Dashboard\" no-code=\"true\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Address Maps** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/ip-addresses/proxy-ips\" />\n\n2. Select **Create an address map**.\n3. Choose the scope of the address map.\n4. Add the zones and IP addresses that you want to map.\n5. Name your address map.\n6. Review the information and select **Save and Deploy**.\n\n</TabItem>\n<TabItem label=\"API\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nUse the [Create Address Map](/api/resources/addressing/subresources/address_maps/methods/create/) endpoint.\n\nMake sure you have the correct Key/Token and permissions.\n\n</TabItem>\n</Tabs>\n\n## 4. Create DNS records\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\">\n<TabItem label=\"Dashboard\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nTo create a DNS record in the dashboard:\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Records** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records\" />\n\n2. Select **Add record**.\n3. Choose an address (`A`/`AAAA`) [record type](/dns/manage-dns-records/reference/dns-record-types/).\n4. Complete the required fields, setting the **Proxy status** to **proxied**.\n5. Select **Save**.\n\n</TabItem>\n<TabItem label=\"API\" no-code=\"true\">\n\nTo create records with the API, use a [POST request](/api/resources/dns/subresources/records/methods/create/). For field definitions, select a record type under the request body specification.\n\n</TabItem>\n</Tabs>\n\n:::tip\nAs you create the necessary DNS records, [Total TLS](/ssl/edge-certificates/additional-options/total-tls/) can help making sure that you have SSL/TLS certificates in place for all your hostnames.\n:::\n\nWhile the DNS record proxy status and address map will determine how Cloudflare's authoritative DNS responds to requests for your hostnames, the IP addresses specified in `A`/`AAAA` records will determine [how Cloudflare reaches the configured origin](/fundamentals/concepts/how-cloudflare-works/#how-cloudflare-works-as-a-reverse-proxy).\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\n| Type | Name  | IP address      | Proxy status | TTL    |\n| ---- | ----- | --------------- | ------------ | ------ |\n| `A`  | `www` | `203.0.113.150` | `Proxied`    | `Auto` |\n\nAt this point, if an address map for a zone `example.com` specifies that Cloudflare should use `203.0.113.100` for proxied records and the above record exists in the same zone, you can expect the following:\n\n1. Cloudflare responds to DNS requests with `203.0.113.100`.\n2. Cloudflare proxies requests through the CDN and then routes the requests via [GRE](/magic-transit/reference/gre-ipsec-tunnels/) or [CNI](/magic-transit/network-interconnect/) to the origin server `203.0.113.150` (which is within the Magic Transit protected prefix).\n3. Depending on whether Magic Transit is implemented with [direct server return model or with Magic Transit egress](/magic-transit/how-to/configure-tunnel-endpoints/#bidirectional-vs-unidirectional-health-checks), the origin server responds back to Cloudflare either:\n   - Directly over the Internet in a Magic Transit direct server return model\n   - Back through the Magic GRE tunnel(s) in a Magic Transit egress model\n\n4. As the HTTP response egresses the Cloudflare network back to the client side, the source IP address of the response becomes `203.0.113.100` (the IP address that the HTTP request originally landed on).\n\n</Details>\n\n:::note\nHaving the same IP address as ingress IP (defined in the address map) and origin IP (listed in the DNS record) will not cause any loops.\n:::\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\nAssuming `203.0.113.100` was also the origin IP, the DNS record would look like the following:\n\n| Type | Name  | IP address      | Proxy status | TTL    |\n| ---- | ----- | --------------- | ------------ | ------ |\n| `A`  | `www` | `203.0.113.100` | `Proxied`    | `Auto` |\n\n</Details>\n\n## 5. (Optional) Add layer 7 functionality\n\n<Render file=\"service-bindings-related-products\" product=\"byoip\" />"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/byoip/service-bindings/magic-transit-with-cdn.mdx"],"digest":[0,"0cd6f176314b589e"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering/how-to/og-images-astro"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Generate OG images for Astro sites"],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,2],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"difficulty":[0,"Intermediate"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"r2"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"CAhazc"],"name":[0,"Browser Rendering"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Browser Rendering"],"url":[0,"/browser-rendering/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"AI"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Browser Rendering docs"],"description":[0,"Control and interact with headless browser instances programmatically"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/browser-rendering.yaml"],"digest":[0,"9f0c2b870810b10e"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Browser Rendering"],[0,"R2"],[0,"Workers"],[0,"Browser Rendering"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nOpen Graph (OG) images are the preview images that appear when you share a link on social media. Instead of manually creating these images for every blog post, you can use Cloudflare Browser Rendering to automatically generate branded social preview images from an Astro template.\n\nIn this tutorial, you will:\n\n1. Create an Astro page that renders your OG image design.\n2. Use Browser Rendering to screenshot that page as a PNG.\n3. Serve the generated images to social media crawlers.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n- A Cloudflare account with [Browser Rendering enabled](/browser-rendering/get-started/#rest-api)\n- An Astro site deployed on [Cloudflare Workers](/workers/framework-guides/web-apps/astro/)\n- Basic familiarity with Astro and Cloudflare Workers\n\n## 1. Create the OG image template\n\nCreate an Astro route that renders your OG image design. This page serves as the source of truth for your image layout.\n\nCreate `src/pages/social-card.astro`:\n\n```astro\n---\nexport const prerender = false;\n\nconst title = Astro.url.searchParams.get(\"title\") || \"Untitled\";\nconst image = Astro.url.searchParams.get(\"image\");\nconst author = Astro.url.searchParams.get(\"author\");\n---\n\n<html>\n\t<head>\n\t\t<meta charset=\"utf-8\" />\n\t\t<style>\n\t\t\t* {\n\t\t\t\tmargin: 0;\n\t\t\t\tpadding: 0;\n\t\t\t\tbox-sizing: border-box;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\tbody {\n\t\t\t\twidth: 1200px;\n\t\t\t\theight: 630px;\n\t\t\t\tdisplay: flex;\n\t\t\t\tflex-direction: column;\n\t\t\t\tjustify-content: flex-end;\n\t\t\t\tpadding: 60px;\n\t\t\t\tfont-family: system-ui, sans-serif;\n\t\t\t\tbackground: linear-gradient(135deg, #f38020 0%, #f9a825 100%);\n\t\t\t\tcolor: white;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t.title {\n\t\t\t\tfont-size: 64px;\n\t\t\t\tfont-weight: bold;\n\t\t\t\tline-height: 1.1;\n\t\t\t\tmargin-bottom: 24px;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t.author {\n\t\t\t\tfont-size: 24px;\n\t\t\t\topacity: 0.9;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t.logo {\n\t\t\t\tposition: absolute;\n\t\t\t\ttop: 60px;\n\t\t\t\tleft: 60px;\n\t\t\t\theight: 40px;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t</style>\n\t</head>\n\t<body>\n\t\t<img class=\"logo\" src=\"/your-logo.png\" alt=\"Your logo\" />\n\t\t<h1 class=\"title\">{title}</h1>\n\t\t{author && <p class=\"author\">By {author}</p>}\n\t</body>\n</html>\n```\n\nStart your Astro development server to test the template:\n\n```sh\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nTest locally by visiting `http://localhost:4321/social-card?title=My%20Blog%20Post&author=Omar`.\n\n:::note\n\nThis tutorial assumes your markdown posts have frontmatter fields for `title`, `slug`, and optionally `author`. For example:\n\n```yaml\n---\ntitle: \"My First Post\"\nslug: \"my-first-post\"\nauthor: \"John Doe\"\n---\n```\n\nAdjust the `readPosts()` function in the script to match your frontmatter structure.\n\n:::\n\nBefore proceeding, deploy your site to ensure the `/social-card` route is live:\n\n```sh\n# For Cloudflare Workers\nnpx wrangler deploy\n```\n\nUpdate the `BASE_URL` in the script below to match your deployed site URL.\n\n## 2. Generate OG images at build time\n\nGenerate all OG images during the Astro build process using the Cloudflare Browser Rendering REST API.\n\nCreate `scripts/generate-social-cards.ts`:\n\n```ts\nimport { existsSync, mkdirSync, readdirSync, readFileSync, writeFileSync } from \"fs\";\nimport { join } from \"path\";\n\n// Configuration\nconst BASE_URL = \"https://your-site.com\"; // Your deployed site URL\nconst CF_API = \"https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts\";\nconst OUTPUT_DIR = \"public/social-cards\"; // Output directory for generated images\nconst POSTS_DIR = \"src/data/posts\"; // Directory containing your markdown posts (adjust to match your project)\n\ninterface Post {\n\tslug: string;\n\ttitle: string;\n\tauthor?: string;\n}\n\n/** Extract a frontmatter field value from raw markdown content. */\nfunction getFrontmatterField(content: string, field: string): string | null {\n\tconst match = content.match(new RegExp(`^${field}:\\\\s*\"?([^\"\\\\n]+)\"?`, \"m\"));\n\treturn match ? match[1].trim() : null;\n}\n\n/**\n * Read all post files and return { slug, title, author }[].\n * This function scans the POSTS_DIR for markdown files, extracts frontmatter\n * fields (slug, title, author), and returns an array of post objects.\n * Falls back to filename for slug and slug for title if frontmatter is missing.\n */\nfunction readPosts(): Post[] {\n\tif (!existsSync(POSTS_DIR)) return [];\n\tconst files = readdirSync(POSTS_DIR).filter((f) => f.endsWith(\".md\"));\n\treturn files.map((file) => {\n\t\tconst raw = readFileSync(join(POSTS_DIR, file), \"utf-8\");\n\t\tconst slug = getFrontmatterField(raw, \"slug\") ?? file.replace(/\\.md$/, \"\");\n\t\tconst title = getFrontmatterField(raw, \"title\") ?? slug;\n\t\tconst author = getFrontmatterField(raw, \"author\") ?? undefined;\n\t\treturn { slug, title, author };\n\t});\n}\n\n/**\n * Capture a screenshot using Cloudflare Browser Rendering REST API\n */\nasync function captureScreenshot(\n\taccountId: string,\n\tapiToken: string,\n\tpageUrl: string\n): Promise<ArrayBuffer> {\n\tconst endpoint = `${CF_API}/${accountId}/browser-rendering/screenshot`;\n\n\tconst res = await fetch(endpoint, {\n\t\tmethod: \"POST\",\n\t\theaders: {\n\t\t\tAuthorization: `Bearer ${apiToken}`,\n\t\t\t\"Content-Type\": \"application/json\",\n\t\t},\n\t\tbody: JSON.stringify({\n\t\t\turl: pageUrl,\n\t\t\tviewport: { width: 1200, height: 630 }, // Standard OG image size\n\t\t\tgotoOptions: { waitUntil: \"networkidle0\" }, // Wait for page to fully load\n\t\t}),\n\t});\n\n\tif (!res.ok) {\n\t\tconst text = await res.text();\n\t\tthrow new Error(`Screenshot API returned ${res.status}: ${text}`);\n\t}\n\n\treturn res.arrayBuffer();\n}\n\nasync function main() {\n\t// Read credentials from environment variables\n\tconst accountId = process.env.CF_ACCOUNT_ID;\n\tconst apiToken = process.env.CF_API_TOKEN;\n\n\tif (!accountId || !apiToken) {\n\t\tconsole.error(\"Error: CF_ACCOUNT_ID and CF_API_TOKEN required\");\n\t\tprocess.exit(1);\n\t}\n\n\t// Check if --force flag is passed to regenerate all images\n\tconst force = process.argv.includes(\"--force\");\n\n\t// Read posts from markdown files\n\tconst posts = readPosts();\n\n\tif (posts.length === 0) {\n\t\tconsole.log(\"No posts found. Check your POSTS_DIR path.\");\n\t\tprocess.exit(0);\n\t}\n\n\tconsole.log(`Found ${posts.length} posts to process\\n`);\n\n\t// Ensure output directory exists\n\tmkdirSync(OUTPUT_DIR, { recursive: true });\n\n\tlet generated = 0;\n\tlet skipped = 0;\n\n\t// Generate social card for each post\n\tfor (let i = 0; i < posts.length; i++) {\n\t\tconst post = posts[i];\n\t\tconst outPath = join(OUTPUT_DIR, `${post.slug}.png`);\n\t\tconst label = `[${i + 1}/${posts.length}]`;\n\n\t\t// Skip if file exists and --force flag not set\n\t\tif (!force && existsSync(outPath)) {\n\t\t\tconsole.log(`${label} ${post.slug}.png — skipped (exists)`);\n\t\t\tskipped++;\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Build URL with query parameters for the OG template\n\t\tconst params = new URLSearchParams({\n\t\t\ttitle: post.title,\n\t\t\tauthor: post.author || \"\",\n\t\t});\n\t\tconst url = `${BASE_URL}/social-card?${params}`;\n\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t// Capture screenshot and save to file\n\t\t\tconst png = await captureScreenshot(accountId, apiToken, url);\n\t\t\twriteFileSync(outPath, Buffer.from(png));\n\t\t\tconsole.log(`${label} ${post.slug}.png — done`);\n\t\t\tgenerated++;\n\t\t} catch (err) {\n\t\t\tconsole.error(`${label} ${post.slug}.png — failed:`, err);\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Rate limiting: small delay between requests\n\t\tif (i < posts.length - 1) {\n\t\t\tawait new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 200));\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tconsole.log(`\\nDone. Generated: ${generated}, Skipped: ${skipped}`);\n}\n\nmain();\n```\n\nSet your Cloudflare credentials as environment variables:\n\n```sh\nexport CF_ACCOUNT_ID=your_account_id\nexport CF_API_TOKEN=your_api_token\n```\n\n:::note\n\nBrowser Rendering has [rate limits](/browser-rendering/limits/) that vary by plan. The script includes a 200ms delay between requests to help stay within these limits. For large sites, you may need to run the script in batches.\n\n:::\n\nRun the script to generate images:\n\n```sh\n# Generate new images only\nbun scripts/generate-social-cards.ts\n\n# Regenerate all images\nbun scripts/generate-social-cards.ts --force\n```\n\nOptionally, add to your build script in `package.json`:\n\n```json\n{\n\t\"scripts\": {\n\t\t\"build\": \"bun scripts/generate-social-cards.ts && astro build\"\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n## 3. Add OG meta tags to your pages\n\nUpdate your blog post layout to reference the generated images:\n\n```astro\n---\n// src/layouts/BlogPost.astro\nconst { title, slug, author } = Astro.props;\nconst ogImageUrl = `/social-cards/${slug}.png`;\n---\n\n<html>\n\t<head>\n\t\t<meta property=\"og:title\" content={title} />\n\t\t<meta property=\"og:image\" content={ogImageUrl} />\n\t\t<meta property=\"og:image:width\" content=\"1200\" />\n\t\t<meta property=\"og:image:height\" content=\"630\" />\n\t\t<meta name=\"twitter:card\" content=\"summary_large_image\" />\n\t\t<meta name=\"twitter:image\" content={ogImageUrl} />\n\t</head>\n\t<body>\n\t\t<slot />\n\t</body>\n</html>\n```\n\n## 4. Test your OG images\n\nBefore testing, make sure to deploy your site with the newly generated social card images:\n\n```sh\n# For Cloudflare Workers\nnpx wrangler deploy\n```\n\nUse these tools to verify your OG images render correctly:\n\n- [Facebook Sharing Debugger](https://developers.facebook.com/tools/debug/)\n- [Twitter Card Validator](https://cards-dev.twitter.com/validator)\n- [LinkedIn Post Inspector](https://www.linkedin.com/post-inspector/)\n\n## Customize the template\n\n### Add a background image\n\n```astro\n---\nconst title = Astro.url.searchParams.get(\"title\") || \"Untitled\";\nconst image = Astro.url.searchParams.get(\"image\");\n---\n\n<body style={image ? `background-image: url(${image})` : undefined}>\n\t<!-- content -->\n</body>\n```\n\n### Use custom fonts\n\n```astro\n<head>\n\t<link\n\t\thref=\"https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@700&display=swap\"\n\t\trel=\"stylesheet\"\n\t/>\n\t<style>\n\t\tbody {\n\t\t\tfont-family: \"Inter\", sans-serif;\n\t\t}\n\t</style>\n</head>\n```\n\n### Add Tailwind CSS\n\nIf your Astro site uses Tailwind, you can use it in your OG template:\n\n```astro\n---\nimport \"../styles/global.css\";\n---\n\n<body\n\tclass=\"flex h-[630px] w-[1200px] flex-col justify-end bg-gradient-to-br from-orange-500 to-amber-500 p-16 text-white\"\n>\n\t<h1 class=\"mb-6 text-6xl leading-tight font-bold\">{title}</h1>\n</body>\n```\n\n## Performance considerations\n\n### Image optimization\n\nConsider running generated images through Cloudflare Images or Image Resizing for additional optimization:\n\n```ts\nconst optimizedUrl = `https://your-domain.com/cdn-cgi/image/width=1200,format=auto/social-cards/${slug}.png`;\n```\n\n## Next steps\n\nYour Astro site now automatically generates OG images using Browser Rendering. When you share a link on social media, crawlers will fetch the generated image from the static path.\n\nFrom here, you can:\n\n- Customize your template with [custom fonts](#use-custom-fonts), [Tailwind CSS](#add-tailwind-css), or [background images](#add-a-background-image).\n- Add cache invalidation logic to regenerate images when post content changes.\n- Use [Cloudflare Images](/images/) or [Image Resizing](/images/transform-images/) for additional optimization.\n\n## Related resources\n\n- [Browser Rendering documentation](/browser-rendering/)\n- [R2 storage](/r2/)\n- [Cloudflare Images](/images/)"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/browser-rendering/how-to/og-images-astro.mdx"],"digest":[0,"42180ae3dd0010bd"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering/workers-bindings/browser-rendering-with-do"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Deploy a Browser Rendering Worker with Durable Objects"],"description":[0,"Use the Browser Rendering API along with Durable Objects to take screenshots from web pages and store them in R2."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,2],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"JavaScript"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Beginner"],"reviewed":[3,"2023-09-28T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"durable-objects"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"r2"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"CAhazc"],"name":[0,"Browser Rendering"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Browser Rendering"],"url":[0,"/browser-rendering/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"AI"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Browser Rendering docs"],"description":[0,"Control and interact with headless browser instances programmatically"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/browser-rendering.yaml"],"digest":[0,"9f0c2b870810b10e"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"],[0,"Durable Objects"],[0,"R2"],[0,"Browser Rendering"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, PackageManagers, WranglerConfig, TypeScriptExample } from \"~/components\";\n\nBy following this guide, you will create a Worker that uses the Browser Rendering API along with [Durable Objects](/durable-objects/) to take screenshots from web pages and store them in [R2](/r2/).\n\nUsing Durable Objects to persist browser sessions improves performance by eliminating the time that it takes to spin up a new browser session. Since Durable Objects re-uses sessions, it reduces the number of concurrent sessions needed.\n\n<Render file=\"prereqs\" product=\"workers\" />\n\n## 1. Create a Worker project\n\n[Cloudflare Workers](/workers/) provides a serverless execution environment that allows you to create new applications or augment existing ones without configuring or maintaining infrastructure. Your Worker application is a container to interact with a headless browser to do actions, such as taking screenshots.\n\nCreate a new Worker project named `browser-worker` by running:\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"create\"\n\tpkg=\"cloudflare@latest\"\n\targs={\"browser-worker\"}\n/>\n\n## 2. Install Puppeteer\n\nIn your `browser-worker` directory, install Cloudflare’s [fork of Puppeteer](/browser-rendering/puppeteer/):\n\n<PackageManagers pkg=\"@cloudflare/puppeteer\" dev />\n\n## 3. Create a R2 bucket\n\nCreate two R2 buckets, one for production, and one for development.\n\nNote that bucket names must be lowercase and can only contain dashes.\n\n```sh\nwrangler r2 bucket create screenshots\nwrangler r2 bucket create screenshots-test\n```\n\nTo check that your buckets were created, run:\n\n```sh\nwrangler r2 bucket list\n```\n\nAfter running the `list` command, you will see all bucket names, including the ones you have just created.\n\n## 4. Configure your Wrangler configuration file\n\nConfigure your `browser-worker` project's [Wrangler configuration file](/workers/wrangler/configuration/) by adding a browser [binding](/workers/runtime-apis/bindings/) and a [Node.js compatibility flag](/workers/configuration/compatibility-flags/#nodejs-compatibility-flag). Browser bindings allow for communication between a Worker and a headless browser which allows you to do actions such as taking a screenshot, generating a PDF and more.\n\nUpdate your Wrangler configuration file with the Browser Rendering API binding, the R2 bucket you created and a Durable Object:\n\n:::note\nYour Worker configuration must include the `nodejs_compat` compatibility flag and a `compatibility_date` of 2025-09-15 or later.\n:::\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"$schema\": \"./node_modules/wrangler/config-schema.json\",\n\t\"name\": \"rendering-api-demo\",\n\t\"main\": \"src/index.js\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"compatibility_flags\": [\n\t\t\"nodejs_compat\"\n\t],\n\t\"account_id\": \"<ACCOUNT_ID>\",\n\t// Browser Rendering API binding\n\t\"browser\": {\n\t\t\"binding\": \"MYBROWSER\"\n\t},\n\t// Bind an R2 Bucket\n\t\"r2_buckets\": [\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"binding\": \"BUCKET\",\n\t\t\t\"bucket_name\": \"screenshots\",\n\t\t\t\"preview_bucket_name\": \"screenshots-test\"\n\t\t}\n\t],\n\t// Binding to a Durable Object\n\t\"durable_objects\": {\n\t\t\"bindings\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"name\": \"BROWSER\",\n\t\t\t\t\"class_name\": \"Browser\"\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t]\n\t},\n\t\"migrations\": [\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"tag\": \"v1\", // Should be unique for each entry\n\t\t\t\"new_sqlite_classes\": [ // Array of new classes\n\t\t\t\t\"Browser\"\n\t\t\t]\n\t\t}\n\t]\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n## 5. Code\n\nThe code below uses Durable Object to instantiate a browser using Puppeteer. It then opens a series of web pages with different resolutions, takes a screenshot of each, and uploads it to R2.\n\nThe Durable Object keeps a browser session open for 60 seconds after last use. If a browser session is open, any requests will re-use the existing session rather than creating a new one. Update your Worker code by copy and pasting the following:\n\n<TypeScriptExample playground={true}>\n```ts\nimport { DurableObject } from \"cloudflare:workers\";\nimport * as puppeteer from \"@cloudflare/puppeteer\";\n\ninterface Env {\n  MYBROWSER: Fetcher;\n  BUCKET: R2Bucket;\n  BROWSER: DurableObjectNamespace;\n}\n\nexport default {\n  async fetch(request, env): Promise<Response> {\n    const obj = env.BROWSER.getByName(\"browser\");\n\n    // Send a request to the Durable Object, then await its response\n    const resp = await obj.fetch(request);\n\n    return resp;\n  },\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;\n\nconst KEEP_BROWSER_ALIVE_IN_SECONDS = 60;\n\nexport class Browser extends DurableObject<Env> {\n  private browser?: puppeteer.Browser;\n  private keptAliveInSeconds: number = 0;\n  private storage: DurableObjectStorage;\n\n  constructor(state: DurableObjectState, env: Env) {\n    super(state, env);\n    this.storage = state.storage;\n  }\n\n  async fetch(request: Request): Promise<Response> {\n    // Screen resolutions to test out\n    const width: number[] = [1920, 1366, 1536, 360, 414];\n    const height: number[] = [1080, 768, 864, 640, 896];\n\n    // Use the current date and time to create a folder structure for R2\n    const nowDate = new Date();\n    const coeff = 1000 * 60 * 5;\n    const roundedDate = new Date(\n      Math.round(nowDate.getTime() / coeff) * coeff,\n    ).toString();\n    const folder = roundedDate.split(\" GMT\")[0];\n\n    // If there is a browser session open, re-use it\n    if (!this.browser || !this.browser.isConnected()) {\n      console.log(`Browser DO: Starting new instance`);\n      try {\n        this.browser = await puppeteer.launch(this.env.MYBROWSER);\n      } catch (e) {\n        console.log(\n          `Browser DO: Could not start browser instance. Error: ${e}`,\n        );\n      }\n    }\n\n    // Reset keptAlive after each call to the DO\n    this.keptAliveInSeconds = 0;\n\n    // Check if browser exists before opening page\n    if (!this.browser) return new Response(\"Browser launch failed\", { status: 500 });\n\n    const page = await this.browser.newPage();\n\n    // Take screenshots of each screen size\n    for (let i = 0; i < width.length; i++) {\n      await page.setViewport({ width: width[i], height: height[i] });\n      await page.goto(\"https://workers.cloudflare.com/\");\n      const fileName = `screenshot_${width[i]}x${height[i]}`;\n      const sc = await page.screenshot();\n\n      await this.env.BUCKET.put(`${folder}/${fileName}.jpg`, sc);\n    }\n\n    // Close tab when there is no more work to be done on the page\n    await page.close();\n\n    // Reset keptAlive after performing tasks to the DO\n    this.keptAliveInSeconds = 0;\n\n    // Set the first alarm to keep DO alive\n    const currentAlarm = await this.storage.getAlarm();\n    if (currentAlarm == null) {\n      console.log(`Browser DO: setting alarm`);\n      const TEN_SECONDS = 10 * 1000;\n      await this.storage.setAlarm(Date.now() + TEN_SECONDS);\n    }\n\n    return new Response(\"success\");\n  }\n\n  async alarm(): Promise<void> {\n    this.keptAliveInSeconds += 10;\n\n    // Extend browser DO life\n    if (this.keptAliveInSeconds < KEEP_BROWSER_ALIVE_IN_SECONDS) {\n      console.log(\n        `Browser DO: has been kept alive for ${this.keptAliveInSeconds} seconds. Extending lifespan.`,\n      );\n      await this.storage.setAlarm(Date.now() + 10 * 1000);\n      // You can ensure the ws connection is kept alive by requesting something\n      // or just let it close automatically when there is no work to be done\n      // for example, `await this.browser.version()`\n    } else {\n      console.log(\n        `Browser DO: exceeded life of ${KEEP_BROWSER_ALIVE_IN_SECONDS}s.`,\n      );\n      if (this.browser) {\n        console.log(`Closing browser.`);\n        await this.browser.close();\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n</TypeScriptExample>\n\n## 6. Test\n\nRun `npx wrangler dev` to test your Worker locally.\n\n<Render file=\"remote-binding-note\" product=\"workers\" />\n\n## 7. Deploy\n\nRun [`npx wrangler deploy`](/workers/wrangler/commands/#deploy) to deploy your Worker to the Cloudflare global network.\n\n## Related resources\n\n- Other [Puppeteer examples](https://github.com/cloudflare/puppeteer/tree/main/examples)\n- Get started with [Durable Objects](/durable-objects/get-started/)\n- [Using R2 from Workers](/r2/api/workers/workers-api-usage/)"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/browser-rendering/workers-bindings/browser-rendering-with-DO.mdx"],"digest":[0,"e984b2aee96b63ad"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"cloudflare-network-firewall/tutorials/graphql-analytics"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"GraphQL Analytics"],"description":[0,"Use the GraphQL Analytics API to review data for Cloudflare Network Firewall network traffic related to rules matching your traffic."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"GraphQL Network Firewall"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Network Firewall"],"url":[0,"/cloudflare-network-firewall/"],"group":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Network security"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Network Firewall docs"],"description":[0,"Protect your enterprise network with advanced firewall-as-a-service protection"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/cloudflare-network-firewall.yaml"],"digest":[0,"c53dde4e7475f7b8"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Cloudflare Network Firewall"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { DashButton, Render } from \"~/components\";\n\nUse the GraphQL Analytics API to review data for Cloudflare Network Firewall (formerly Magic Firewall) network traffic related to rules matching your traffic. This contains both rules you configured in the Network Firewall dashboard, and the rules managed by Cloudflare as a part of [Network Firewall Managed rules](/cloudflare-network-firewall/how-to/enable-managed-rulesets/) and [Network Firewall IDS](/cloudflare-network-firewall/about/ids/) features.\n\nBefore you begin, you must have an [API token](/analytics/graphql-api/getting-started/authentication/). For additional help getting started with GraphQL Analytics, refer to [GraphQL Analytics API](/analytics/graphql-api/).\n\n## Obtain Cloudflare Account ID\n\nTo construct a Network Firewall GraphQL query for an object, you will need a Cloudflare Account ID\n\n### Obtain your Cloudflare Account ID\n\n1. Log in to the [Cloudflare dashboard](https://dash.cloudflare.com/), and select your account.\n2. The URL in your browser's address bar should show `https://dash.cloudflare.com/` followed by a hex string. The hex string is your Cloudflare Account ID.\n\n### Obtain the rule ID for a firewall rule\n\nTo construct queries to gather analytics for a particular rule, you need the rule ID for each firewall rule.\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the [Firewall Policies](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/network-security/magic_firewall) page.\n2. In the **Custom policies** tab, locate the rule you need the rule ID for from the list and select the three dots > **Edit**.\n3. Locate the **ID** and select the copy button.\n4. Select **Cancel** to return to the **Firewall Policies** page.\n\n## Explore GraphQL schema with Cloudflare Network Firewall query example\n\nIn this section, you will run a test query to retrieve a five minute count of all configured Network Firewall rules within five minute intervals. You can copy and paste the code below into GraphiQL.\n\nFor additional information about the Analytics schema, refer to [Explore the Analytics schema with GraphiQL](/analytics/graphql-api/getting-started/explore-graphql-schema/).\n\n```graphql graphql-api-explorer\nquery MagicFirewallExample($accountTag: string!, $start: Time, $end: Time) {\n\tviewer {\n\t\taccounts(filter: { accountTag: $accountTag }) {\n\t\t\tmagicFirewallSamplesAdaptiveGroups(\n\t\t\t\tfilter: { datetime_geq: $start, datetime_leq: $end }\n\t\t\t\tlimit: 2\n\t\t\t\torderBy: [datetimeFiveMinute_DESC]\n\t\t\t) {\n\t\t\t\tsum {\n\t\t\t\t\tbits\n\t\t\t\t\tpackets\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tdimensions {\n\t\t\t\t\tdatetimeFiveMinute\n\t\t\t\t\truleId\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n## Example queries for Cloudflare Network Firewall\n\n### Obtain analytics for a specific rule\n\nUse the example below to display the total number of packets and bits for the top ten suspected malicious traffic streams within the last hour. After receiving the results, you can sort by packet rates with a five minute average.\n\n<Render file=\"case-sensitivity-note\" product=\"analytics\" />\n\nFor each stream, display the:\n\n- Source and destination IP addresses\n- Ingress Cloudflare data centers that received it\n- Total traffic volume in bits and packets received within the hour\n- Actions taken by the firewall rule\n\n```graphql graphql-api-explorer\nquery MagicFirewallObtainRules(\n\t$accountId: string!\n\t$ruleId: string\n\t$start: Time\n\t$end: Time\n) {\n\tviewer {\n\t\taccounts(filter: { accountTag: $accountId }) {\n\t\t\tmagicFirewallNetworkAnalyticsAdaptiveGroups(\n\t\t\t\tfilter: { ruleId: $ruleId, datetime_geq: $start, datetime_leq: $end }\n\t\t\t\tlimit: 10\n\t\t\t\torderBy: [avg_packetRateFiveMinutes_DESC]\n\t\t\t) {\n\t\t\t\tsum {\n\t\t\t\t\tbits\n\t\t\t\t\tpackets\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tdimensions {\n\t\t\t\t\tcoloCity\n\t\t\t\t\tipDestinationAddress\n\t\t\t\t\tipSourceAddress\n\t\t\t\t\toutcome\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n### Obtain IDS analytics\n\nUse the example below to display the total number of packets and bits for the top 10 traffic streams that Network Firewall IDS has detected in the last hour.\n\nBy setting `verdict` to `drop` and `outcome` as `pass`, we are filtering for traffic that was marked as a detection (i.e. verdict was drop) but was not dropped (for example, outcome was `pass`). This is because currently, Network Firewall IDS only detects malicious traffic but does not drop the traffic.\n\nFor each stream, display the:\n\n- Source and destination IP addresses.\n- Ingress Cloudflare data centers that received it.\n- Total traffic volume in bits and packets received within the hour.\n\n```graphql graphql-api-explorer\nquery MagicFirewallObtainIDS($accountTag: string!, $start: Time, $end: Time) {\n\tviewer {\n\t\taccounts(filter: { accountTag: $accountTag }) {\n\t\t\tmagicIDPSNetworkAnalyticsAdaptiveGroups(\n\t\t\t\tfilter: {\n\t\t\t\t\tdatetime_geq: $start\n\t\t\t\t\tdatetime_leq: $end\n\t\t\t\t\tverdict: drop\n\t\t\t\t\toutcome: pass\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tlimit: 10\n\t\t\t\torderBy: [avg_packetRateFiveMinutes_DESC]\n\t\t\t) {\n\t\t\t\tsum {\n\t\t\t\t\tbits\n\t\t\t\t\tpackets\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tdimensions {\n\t\t\t\t\tcoloCity\n\t\t\t\t\tipDestinationAddress\n\t\t\t\t\tipSourceAddress\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n}\n```\n\nAlternatively, to inspect all traffic that was analyzed, but grouped into malicious traffic and other traffic, the example below can be used. The response will contain two entries for each five minute timestamp. `verdict` will be set to `drop` for malicious traffic, and `verdict` will be set to `pass` for traffic that did not match any of the IDS rules.\n\n```graphql graphql-api-explorer\nquery MagicFirewallTraffic($accountTag: string!, $start: Time, $end: Time) {\n\tviewer {\n\t\taccounts(filter: { accountTag: $accountTag }) {\n\t\t\tmagicIDPSNetworkAnalyticsAdaptiveGroups(\n\t\t\t\tfilter: { datetime_geq: $start, datetime_leq: $end }\n\t\t\t\tlimit: 10\n\t\t\t\torderBy: [avg_packetRateFiveMinutes_DESC]\n\t\t\t) {\n\t\t\t\tsum {\n\t\t\t\t\tbits\n\t\t\t\t\tpackets\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tdimensions {\n\t\t\t\t\tcoloCity\n\t\t\t\t\tipDestinationAddress\n\t\t\t\t\tipSourceAddress\n\t\t\t\t\tverdict\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n}\n```"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/cloudflare-network-firewall/tutorials/graphql-analytics.mdx"],"digest":[0,"b175637ba916a462"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"cloudflare-one/tutorials/access-workers"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Create custom headers for Cloudflare Access-protected origins with Workers"],"description":[0,"This tutorial covers how to use a Cloudflare Worker to add custom headers to traffic. The headers will be sent to origin services protected by Cloudflare Access."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"JavaScript"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Intermediate"],"reviewed":[3,"2023-11-27T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"access"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"cloudflare-one"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"YNwEWV"],"name":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"url":[0,"/cloudflare-one/"],"group":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"wrap":[0,true],"preview_tryout":[0,true]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare One docs"],"description":[0,"Replace legacy security perimeters with Cloudflare's network to protect your organization"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/cloudflare-one.yaml"],"digest":[0,"3bf10f1fd9b66cb3"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"],[0,"Access"],[0,"Cloudflare One"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { TypeScriptExample, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nThis tutorial covers how to use a [Cloudflare Worker](/workers/) to add custom HTTP headers to traffic, and how to send those custom headers to your origin services protected by [Cloudflare Access](/cloudflare-one/access-controls/policies/).\n\nSome applications and networking implementations require specific custom headers to be passed to the origin, which can be difficult to implement for traffic moving through a Zero Trust proxy. You can configure a Worker to send the [user authorization headers](/cloudflare-one/access-controls/applications/http-apps/authorization-cookie/) required by Access.\n\n---\n\n## Before you begin\n\n- Secure your origin server with Cloudflare Access\n\n## Before you begin\n\n1. In the [Cloudflare dashboard](https://dash.cloudflare.com/), go to the **Workers & Pages** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/workers-and-pages\" />\n\n2. If this is your first Worker, select **Create Worker**. Otherwise, select **Create application**, then select **Create Worker**.\n\n3. Enter an identifiable name for the Worker, then select **Deploy**.\n\n4. Select **Edit code**.\n\n5. Input the following Worker:\n\n<TypeScriptExample>\n\n```ts title=\"Worker with custom HTTP headers\"\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\tconst { headers } = request;\n\t\tconst cfaccessemail = headers.get(\"cf-access-authenticated-user-email\");\n\n\t\tconst requestWithID = new Request(request);\n\t\trequestWithID.headers.set(\"company-user-id\", cfaccessemail);\n\n\t\treturn fetch(requestWithID);\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;\n```\n\n</TypeScriptExample>\n\n6. Select **Save and deploy**.\n\nYour Worker is now ready to send custom headers to your Access-protected origin services.\n\n## Apply the Worker to your hostname\n\n1. Select the Worker you created, then go to **Triggers**.\n2. In **Routes**, select **Add route**.\n3. Enter the hostname and zone for your origin, then select **Add route**.\n\nThe Worker will now insert a custom header into requests that match the defined route. For example:\n\n```http title=\"Example custom header\" {4,5}\n\"Accept\": \"text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,*/*;q=0.8,application/signed-exchange;v=b3;q=0.7\",\n    \"Accept-Encoding\": \"gzip\",\n    \"Accept-Language\": \"en-US,en;q=0.9\",\n    \"Cf-Access-Authenticated-User-Email\": \"user@example.com\",\n    \"Company-User-Id\": \"user@example.com\",\n    \"Connection\": \"keep-alive\"\n```"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/cloudflare-one/tutorials/access-workers.mdx"],"digest":[0,"7b1d5c763b767e70"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"cloudflare-one/tutorials/ai-wrapper-tenant-control"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Create and secure an AI agent wrapper using AI Gateway and Zero Trust"],"description":[0,"This tutorial explains how to use Cloudflare AI Gateway and Zero Trust to create a functional and secure website wrapper for an AI agent."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"AI"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Advanced"],"reviewed":[3,"2025-05-02T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"cloudflare-one"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"YNwEWV"],"name":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"url":[0,"/cloudflare-one/"],"group":[0,"Cloudflare One"],"wrap":[0,true],"preview_tryout":[0,true]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare One docs"],"description":[0,"Replace legacy security perimeters with Cloudflare's network to protect your organization"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/cloudflare-one.yaml"],"digest":[0,"3bf10f1fd9b66cb3"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Cloudflare One"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { TabItem, Tabs, Details, Render, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nThis tutorial explains how to use [Cloudflare AI Gateway](/ai-gateway/) and Zero Trust to create a functional and secure website wrapper for an AI agent. Cloudflare Zero Trust administrators can protect access to the wrapper with [Cloudflare Access](/cloudflare-one/access-controls/policies/). Additionally, you can enforce [Gateway policies](/cloudflare-one/traffic-policies/) to control how your users interact with AI agents, including executing AI agents in an isolated browser with [Browser Isolation](/cloudflare-one/remote-browser-isolation/), enforcing [Data Loss Prevention](/cloudflare-one/data-loss-prevention/) profiles to prevent your users from sharing sensitive data, and scanning content to avoid answers from AI agents that violate internal corporate guidelines. Creating an AI agent wrapper is also an effective way to enforce tenant control if you have an enterprise plan for a specific AI provider, such as ChatGPT Enterprise.\n\nThis tutorial uses ChatGPT as an example AI agent.\n\n## Before you begin\n\nMake sure you have:\n\n- A [Cloudflare Zero Trust organization](/cloudflare-one/setup/).\n- An API key for your desired AI provider, such as an [OpenAI API key](https://platform.openai.com/api-keys) for ChatGPT.\n\n## 1. Create an AI gateway\n\nFirst, create an AI gateway to control your AI app.\n\n1. In the [Cloudflare dashboard](https://dash.cloudflare.com/), go to the **AI Gateway** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/ai/ai-gateway\" />\n\n2. Select **Create Gateway**.\n3. Name your gateway.\n4. Select **Create**.\n5. Configure your desired options for the gateway.\n6. [Connect your AI provider](/ai-gateway/get-started/#connect-application) to proxy queries to your AI agent of choice using your AI gateway.\n7. (Optional) Turn on [Authenticated Gateway](/ai-gateway/configuration/authentication/). The Authenticated Gateway feature ensures your AI gateway can only be called securely by enforcing a token in the form of a request header `cf-aig-authorization`.\n   1. Go to **AI** > **AI Gateway**.\n   2. Select your AI gateway, then go to **Settings**.\n   3. Turn on **Authenticated Gateway**, then choose **Confirm**.\n   4. Select **Create authentication token**, then select **Create an AI Gateway authentication token**.\n   5. Configure your token and copy the token value. When creating your Worker, you will need to pass this token when calling your AI gateway.\n\nFor more information, refer to [Getting started with AI Gateway](/ai-gateway/get-started/).\n\n## 2. (Optional) Use Guardrails to block unsafe or inappropriate content\n\n[Guardrails](/ai-gateway/features/guardrails/) is an built-in AI Gateway security feature that allows Cloudflare to identify unsafe or inappropriate content in prompts and responses based on selected categories.\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **AI Gateway** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/ai/ai-gateway\" />\n\n2. Select your AI gateway.\n3. Go to **Guardrails**.\n4. Turn on Guardrails.\n5. Select **Change** to configure the categories you would like to filter for both prompts and responses.\n\n## 3. Build a Worker to serve the wrapper\n\n### 1. Create the Worker\n\nIn order to build the Worker, you will need to choose if you want to build it locally using [Wrangler](/workers/wrangler/install-and-update/) or remotely using the [dashboard](https://dash.cloudflare.com/).\n\n<Tabs> <TabItem label=\"Wrangler\">\n\n1. In a terminal, log in to your Cloudflare account:\n\n   ```bash\n   wrangler login\n   ```\n\n2. Initiate the project locally:\n\n   ```bash\n   mkdir ai-agent-wrapper\n   cd ai-agent-wrapper\n   wrangler init\n   ```\n\n3. Create a Wrangler configuration file:\n\n   ```toml\n   name = \"ai-agent-wrapper\"\n   main = \"src/index.js\"\n   compatibility_date = \"2023-10-30\"\n\n   [vars]\n   # Add any environment variables here\n   ```\n\n4. Add your AI provider's API key as a [secret](/workers/configuration/secrets/):\n\n   ```bash\n   wrangler secret put <OPENAI_API_KEY>\n   ```\n\nYou can now build the Worker using the `index.js` file created by Wrangler.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Workers & Pages** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/workers-and-pages\" />\n\n2. Select **Create**.\n3. In **Workers**, choose the **Hello world** template.\n4. Name your worker, then select **Deploy**.\n5. Select your Worker, then go to the **Settings** tab.\n6. Go to **Variables and Secrets**, then select **Add**.\n7. Choose _Secret_ as the type, name your secret (for example, `OPENAI_API_KEY`), and enter the value of your AI provider's API key in **Value**.\n\nYou can now build the Worker using the online code editor by selecting **Edit code** on your Worker page.\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n### 2.
