{ Render, DashButton, Tabs, TabItem, APIRequest, Details } from \"~/components\";\n\nCloudflare DNS offers a few different [setup options](/dns/zone-setups/). A primary setup (also known as full) is the most common and the only one available for Free or Pro plans. For details, refer to [About](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/). For more introductory context, refer to [Concepts](/dns/concepts/).\n\n## Before you begin\n\nThe sections below offer detailed guidance on the different steps to onboard your domain. Before you begin, make sure that you:\n\n- Already own a domain name (such as `example.com` or `cloudflare.com`).\n\n:::note\nIf you do not already have a [domain name](https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/dns/glossary/what-is-a-domain-name/), get one at-cost through [Cloudflare Registrar](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/domains/register).\n\nAll domains purchased through Cloudflare Registrar automatically use Cloudflare for authoritative DNS, which means you can skip the rest of this tutorial.\n:::\n\n- Have previously created a [Cloudflare account](/fundamentals/account/create-account/).\n- Disabled [DNSSEC](/dns/concepts/#dnssec) at your registrar (where you bought your domain name).\n\n<Render file=\"dnssec-providers\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n<Render file=\"dnssec-enabled-migration\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 1. Add your domain to Cloudflare\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\nIn the Cloudflare dashboard, [add your domain](/fundamentals/manage-domains/add-site/).\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n<APIRequest\n  path=\"/zones\"\n  method=\"POST\"\n  json={{\n    \"name\": \"<YOUR_DOMAIN>\",\n    \"account\": {\n      \"id\": \"<YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID>\"\n    }\n  }}\n/>\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\nIf Cloudflare is unable to identify your domain as a registered domain, make sure you are using an existing [top-level domain](https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/dns/top-level-domain/) (`.com`, `.net`, `.biz`, or others).\n\nCloudflare requires your `apex domain` to be one level below a valid TLD defined in the [Public Suffix List (PSL)](https://github.com/publicsuffix/list/blob/master/public_suffix_list.dat). Enterprise customers can onboard lower-level subdomains using [Subdomain setup](/dns/zone-setups/subdomain-setup/).\n\n## 2. Review your DNS records\n\nWhen you start using Cloudflare's nameservers for authoritative DNS and your zone is in a primary setup (full), Cloudflare will become your primary DNS provider. This means that your DNS records in Cloudflare need to be accurate for your domain to work properly.\n\n<Render file=\"dns-scan-intro\" product=\"dns\" /> <br />\n\n:::note\n\n<Render file=\"dns-scan-note\" product=\"dns\" />\n:::\n\n<Render file=\"dns-scan-procedure\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n<Render file=\"dns-nxdomain-warning\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 3. Change your nameservers\n\n<Render file=\"nameserver-preamble\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n<Render file=\"minimize-downtime-tip\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n### Get nameserver names\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the zone **Overview** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/\" />\n\n2. Locate the nameserver names in **2. Replace with Cloudflare's nameservers**.\n\n   ![Find nameserver names on the Overview page of your domain](~/assets/images/dns/nameserver-names.png)\n\n<br />\n3. Keep this window open while you perform the next step.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n<APIRequest\n  path=\"/zones/{zone_id}\"\n  method=\"GET\"\n/>\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n:::note\n\nCloudflare automatically assigns nameservers to a domain and these assignments cannot be changed. For more details, refer to [Nameserver assignments](/dns/nameservers/nameserver-options/#assignment-method).\n:::\n\n### Update your registrar\n\n1. Log in to the admin account for your domain registrar. If you do not know your provider, use [ICANN Lookup](https://lookup.icann.org/).\n\n:::note\n\nDepending on your use case, you may have to perform this step on the DNS records management of your domain parent zone, or at a domain reseller, instead. Refer to [Nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/#specific-processes) for details.\n:::\n\n2. Remove your existing authoritative nameservers.\n\n3. Add the nameservers provided by Cloudflare. If their names are not **copied exactly**, your DNS will not resolve correctly.\n\n<Details header=\"Provider-specific instructions\">\n<Render file=\"ns-update-providers\" product=\"dns\" />\n</Details>\n\n:::note\n\nTo avoid common issues, refer to our [Nameserver replacement checklist](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/troubleshooting/).\n\n:::\n\n### Verify changes\n\nWait up to 24 hours while your registrar updates your nameservers.\n\nWhen your domain is **Active**:\n\n- You will receive an email from Cloudflare.\n- Your domain will have a [status](/dns/zone-setups/reference/domain-status/) of **Active** on the **Websites** page of your account.\n- Online tools such as [https://www.whatsmydns.net/](https://www.whatsmydns.net/) will show your Cloudflare-assigned nameservers (most of these tools use cached query results, so it may take longer for them to show the updated nameservers).\n- CLI commands will show your Cloudflare-assigned nameservers\n\n```txt\n*macOS/Linux*\n\nwhois <DOMAIN_NAME>\ndig ns <DOMAIN_NAME> @1.1.1.1\ndig ns <DOMAIN_NAME> @8.8.8.8\ndig <DOMAIN_NAME> +trace\n\n*Windows*\n\nnslookup -type=ns <DOMAIN_NAME> 1.1.1.1\nnslookup -type=ns <DOMAIN_NAME> 8.8.8.8\n```\n\n:::note\n\nIf you see unexpected results, refer to our [troubleshooting suggestions](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/troubleshooting/) and check with your domain registrar.\n\n:::\n\n## 4. Re-enable DNSSEC\n\nWhen you updated your nameservers, you should have also disabled DNSSEC at your registrar.\n\nYou should now [enable DNSSEC](/dns/dnssec/) to protect from domain spoofing."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/setup.mdx"],"digest":[0,"74f9cd9dc0b8f007"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-full-to-partial"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert full setup to partial setup"],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1],"label":[0,"Full to partial"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Tabs, TabItem, Render, GlossaryTooltip, DashButton, Details, APIRequest } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially configured a [primary setup (full)](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/), you can later convert your zone to use a CNAME setup (also known as partial setup).\n\nA CNAME setup allows you to use [Cloudflare's reverse proxy](/fundamentals/concepts/how-cloudflare-works/) on individual subdomains while using a different authoritative DNS provider.\n\n## Before you begin\n\nMake sure you consider the following:\n\n- It will not be possible to use Cloudflare's reverse proxy on the zone apex (`example.com`), only on subdomains.\n- On the dashboard, you will only be able to create A, AAAA, and CNAME records, which are the DNS record types that can be [proxied](/dns/proxy-status/).\n- You should plan for SSL/TLS certificates. If you are only using [Universal SSL](/ssl/edge-certificates/universal-ssl/) prior to converting your zone, a certificate will be provisioned for your subdomains only after each of the respective DNS records are proxied. If your domain is sensitive to downtime, instead of using Universal SSL, consider using an [advanced certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/advanced-certificate-manager/) with [delegated DCV](/ssl/edge-certificates/changing-dcv-method/methods/delegated-dcv/#setup).\n\n## 1. Prepare DNS records\n\n1. Export a zone file\n\n   <Render file=\"export-dns-records\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n2. Import the zone file into your new primary DNS provider.\n3. Create or update your records so that you have CNAME records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net` for every hostname you wish to proxy through Cloudflare.\n\n   <Details header=\"Example CNAME record at authoritative DNS provider\">\n\n   The CNAME record for `www.example.com` would be:\n\n   ```txt\n   www.example.com CNAME www.example.com.cdn.cloudflare.net\n   ```\n\n   </Details>\n\n4. Remove any previously existing A, AAAA, or CNAME records referencing the hostnames you want to proxy through Cloudflare. For these hostnames, leave only the records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net`.\n\n5. Confirm you have the correct record for every subdomain that should be proxied through Cloudflare.\n\n## 2. Convert the zone\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\">\n  <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n\t  1. On the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the zone's **Overview** page.\n\t\t2. Select **Convert to CNAME DNS Setup** and then **Convert** to confirm.\n\t\t3. Save the information from the **Verification TXT Record**. If you lose the information, you can also access it on the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page, under **Verification TXT Record**.\n\n\t</TabItem>\n  <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n\t  1. Use the [Edit Zone endpoint](/api/resources/zones/methods/edit/) with `type` set to `partial` to convert the zone type.\n\t\t2. Take note of the value returned under `verification_key` in the API response. This will be used in the next step.\n\n\t</TabItem>\n</Tabs>\n\n## 3. Verify ownership\n\n<Render file=\"partial-setup-verification-record\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 4. Update your nameservers\n\nOnce verification is complete, update the nameservers at your domain registrar to point to your new authoritative DNS provider. Make sure to remove the Cloudflare nameservers."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-full-to-partial.mdx"],"digest":[0,"4eab042eebebe3e7"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-full-to-secondary"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert full setup to secondary setup"],"description":[0,"If you initially configured a full setup you can later convert your zone to use incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary)."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,2],"label":[0,"Full to secondary"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Tabs, TabItem, Render, GlossaryTooltip, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially configured a [primary setup (full)](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/), you can later convert your zone to use [incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary)](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/setup/).\n\n<Render file=\"conversion-subdomain-setup-callout\" product=\"dns\" />\n\nFollow the steps below to achieve this conversion.\n\n## 1. Prepare DNS records\n\n1. [Export a zone file](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/import-and-export/#export-records).\n2. Import the zone file into your new primary DNS provider.\n3. At your Cloudflare zone, use the [Update DNS Settings](/api/resources/dns/subresources/settings/subresources/zone/methods/edit/) endpoint to enable [secondary DNS overrides](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/proxy-traffic/). Set the value for `secondary_overrides` to `true`.\n\n   :::note\n   Enabling secondary DNS overrides is necessary in case you have DNS records that you wish to keep <GlossaryTooltip term=\"proxy status\" link=\"/dns/proxy-status/\">proxied</GlossaryTooltip>.\n   :::\n\n## 2. Prepare the zone transfers\n\n1. Make adjustments to DNSSEC according to your option for [DNSSEC with secondary setup](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/dnssec-for-secondary/).\n2. (Optional) Create a Transaction Signature (TSIG).\n\n   <Render file=\"tsig-definition\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n   <Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n   <Render file=\"tsig-create-dash\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n   </TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n   <Render file=\"tsig-create-api\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n   </TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n3. Create a peer server.\n\n   <Render file=\"create-peer-server\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 3. Convert the zone and initiate zone transfers\n\n1. Use the [Edit Zone endpoint](/api/resources/zones/methods/edit/) with `type` set to `secondary` to convert the zone type. The existing records will remain in place.\n2. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n3. Select **Manage linked peers** under **DNS Zone Transfers**.\n3. Link the peer server you created in the previous steps and select **Save**.\n4. Back to the the [**DNS Settings**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings) page, select **Initiate zone transfer**.\n5. Confirm the DNS records are transferring as expected.\n6. Go to the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page and take note of your new **Cloudflare Nameservers**.\n7. At your domain registrar (or parent zone), [update your nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/) to include the `secondary.cloudflare.com` nameservers."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-full-to-secondary.mdx"],"digest":[0,"219c1a80ba0b87e3"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-partial-to-full"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert partial setup to full setup"],"description":[0,"If you initially set up a partial domain on Cloudflare, you can later migrate it to a full setup."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,4],"label":[0,"Partial to full"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially set up a partial domain on Cloudflare, you can later migrate it to a [primary setup](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/) (also know as full setup).\n\n<Render file=\"conversion-subdomain-setup-callout\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 1. Prepare Cloudflare SSL/TLS\n\nIn the Cloudflare dashboard, either order an [advanced certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/advanced-certificate-manager/manage-certificates/) or [upload a custom SSL certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/custom-certificates/uploading/) for your website or application.\n\nYou should also verify that the [status](/ssl/reference/certificate-statuses/) of your SSL certificate is **Active**.\n\n:::note\n\nIt is possible to use [Universal SSL](/ssl/edge-certificates/universal-ssl/) instead, but you should consider the following:\n\n- Universal certificates can take at least [15 minutes](/ssl/edge-certificates/universal-ssl/enable-universal-ssl/#full-dns-setup) to be issued.\n- You should make sure to add Cloudflare nameservers to your registrar within 72 hours of the conversion process.\n- Universal SSL only supports first-level subdomains. You can use [Advanced certificates](/ssl/edge-certificates/advanced-certificate-manager/) with the [Total TLS](/ssl/edge-certificates/additional-options/total-tls/) option to automatically issue certificates for any proxied hostname.\n- To minimize downtime, it is recommended having a certificate in place beforehand.\n\n:::\n\n## 2. Update settings in authoritative DNS\n\nAt least 24 hours prior to converting your zone, disable DNSSEC at your authoritative DNS provider.\n\n:::note\n\nAs a best practice, you should also delete the previous [zone activation TXT record](/dns/zone-setups/partial-setup/setup/#1-convert-your-zone-and-review-dns-records) at your authoritative DNS provider. To locate this value in the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page and find the **Verification TXT Record**.\n\n:::\n\n## 3. Convert to full setup\n\nIn the Cloudflare dashboard:\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, select your partial zone (CNAME setup) and go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n2. Select **Convert to Primary DNS** (this will not affect how your traffic is proxied).\n3. Import your records into Cloudflare DNS and verify that they have been configured correctly. Usually, you will want to import [unproxied records](/dns/proxy-status/).\n\n## 4. Activate full setup\n\nGet your assigned Cloudflare nameservers from the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page and [update your nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/) at your registrar.\n\n:::caution\n\nIf you are counting on Universal SSL certificates to cover your website or application, make sure to add Cloudflare nameservers to your registrar within 72 hours of the conversion process.\n\n:::\n\nCloudflare recommends that you also [enable DNSSEC](/dns/dnssec/) from the [**DNS Settings**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings) page\n and add the DS record to your registrar.\n\nOnce all the DNS TTLs expire, all your DNS queries will be answered by the Cloudflare global network.\n\nStart proxying additional hostnames by enabling the [proxy status](/dns/proxy-status/) (also known as orange-clouding) for specific DNS records. Previously proxied subdomains will continue to be proxied without any interruption."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-partial-to-full.mdx"],"digest":[0,"f0e21aad278c0ca5"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-partial-to-secondary"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert partial setup to secondary setup"],"description":[0,"If you initially set up a partial zone on Cloudflare, you can later convert it to use a secondary setup."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,5],"label":[0,"Partial to secondary"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Details, Render, TabItem, Tabs, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially set up a [partial zone (CNAME setup)](/dns/zone-setups/partial-setup/) on Cloudflare, you can later convert it to use a [secondary setup](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/).\n\n<Render file=\"conversion-subdomain-setup-callout\" product=\"dns\" />\n\nThis page will guide you through this conversion using [export and import](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/import-and-export/) and API calls.\n\n## Before you begin\n\nMake sure you consider the following:\n\n- Proxying traffic with secondary zones requires a setting that is not turned on by default. Refer to [Secondary DNS override](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/proxy-traffic/) to learn more. The steps below include enabling this setting.\n- There are a few options for [DNSSEC with incoming zone transfers](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/dnssec-for-secondary/). If you want to use DNSSEC, plan for which option you will configure and confirm that your other DNS provider(s) support the setup.\n- You can prepare SSL/TLS in advance by either ordering an [advanced certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/advanced-certificate-manager/manage-certificates/) or [uploading a custom certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/custom-certificates/uploading/). You should confirm that the certificate covers all your proxied hostnames and that the [status of your SSL certificate](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/ssl-tls/edge-certificates) is **Active**.\n\n## 1. Prepare a zone file\n\n1. Export a zone file from the authoritative DNS provider you were using with your CNAME setup (partial).\n2. Edit the zone file to remove any occurrences of the `cdn.cloudflare.net` suffix.\n\n- If the `CNAME` target is only appending the Cloudflare suffix to the same hostname at which it is created, replace it by the records on the Cloudflare partial zone.\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\nOriginal record in authoritative DNS provider:\n\n| Type    | Name              | Content                              |\n| ------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------------ |\n| `CNAME` | `www.example.com` | `www.example.com.cdn.cloudflare.net` |\n\nRecords in the Cloudflare partial zone:\n\n| Type | Name              | Content  |\n| ---- | ----------------- | -------- |\n| `A`  | `www.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n| `A`  | `www.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n\nFinal records adjusted in the zone file:\n\n| Type | Name              | Content  |\n| ---- | ----------------- | -------- |\n| `A`  | `www.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n| `A`  | `www.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n\n</Details>\n\n- If the `CNAME` record points to a different hostname, keep this record but remove the `cdn.cloudflare.net` suffix, and also bring the records from the Cloudflare partial zone.\n\n<Details header=\"Example\">\n\nOriginal record in authoritative DNS provider:\n\n| Type    | Name              | Content                                         |\n| ------- | ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |\n| `CNAME` | `www.example.com` | `other-hostname.example.com.cdn.cloudflare.net` |\n\nRecords in the Cloudflare partial zone (CNAME setup):\n\n| Type | Name                         | Content  |\n| ---- | ---------------------------- | -------- |\n| `A`  | `other-hostname.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n| `A`  | `other-hostname.example.com` | `<IPv4>` |\n\nFinal records adjusted in the zone file:\n\n| Type    | Name                         | Content                      |\n| ------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------- |\n| `CNAME` | `www.example.com`            | `other-hostname.example.com` |\n| `A`     | `other-hostname.example.com` | `<IPv4>`                     |\n| `A`     | `other-hostname.example.com` | `<IPv4>`                     |\n\n</Details>\n\n## 2. Configure the Cloudflare zone\n\n1. Use the [Import DNS Records endpoint](/api/resources/dns/subresources/records/methods/import/) with a properly [formatted zone file](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/import-and-export/#format-your-zone-file) to import the records into your partial zone.\n\n   <Render file=\"zone-file-size-limit\" product=\"dns\" /> Existing and already\n   proxied records will not be overwritten by the import.\n\n2. Use the [Update DNS Settings endpoint](/api/resources/dns/subresources/settings/subresources/zone/methods/edit/) with `secondary_overrides` set to `true`, to enable Secondary DNS Override.\n\n:::caution\n\nThis step is essential so that Cloudflare can keep the proxy status of the records after the conversion.\n:::\n\n3. Use the [Edit Zone endpoint](/api/resources/zones/methods/edit/) with `type` set to `secondary`, to convert the zone type.\n\n   You can verify if it answers as expected by querying the new assigned secondary nameservers. You can find your nameservers on the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page, and they should follow a format like `ns0123.secondary.cloudflare.com`.\n\n```bash\n# Replace ns0123 with your actual Cloudflare nameservers\ndig example.com @ns0123.secondary.cloudflare.com\n```\n\n4. At your registrar, [update your nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/) to point to the Cloudflare nameservers.\n\nOnce the time to live (TTL) of previous `NS` records is expired and this information is evicted from resolvers' cache, your zone will be properly delegated to Cloudflare. In order to update DNS records, you must configure [zone transfers](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/) in the next steps.\n\n## 3. Configure the zone transfers\n\n1. Remove all references to `cdn.cloudflare.net` from your primary DNS provider. You can do this by importing the same zone file you prepared in [Step 1](#1-prepare-a-zone-file) onto your primary zone.\n\n:::caution\n\nIf you keep any DNS records that still refer `cdn.cloudflare.net`, HTTP traffic for the respective hostnames will break.\n:::\n\n2. Enable outgoing zone transfers at your primary provider and create a peer DNS server on your Cloudflare account.\n\n<Render file=\"create-peer-server\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n3. Link your Cloudflare zone to the peer DNS server you just created.\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n2. Under **DNS Zone Transfers**, select **Manage linked peers**.\n3. Choose a value for **Zone refresh**, which controls the number of seconds between zone updates from your primary DNS server.\n   :::caution\n   Cloudflare will not use the REFRESH value inside the SOA record that is served by your primary provider. Instead the value of zone refresh configured for your secondary zone on Cloudflare will be used to determine the interval after which the SOA serial of the primary zone will be checked for changes.\n   :::\n4. Select the peer server you previously created. If needed, you can link more than one peer server to a zone.\n5. Select **Save** to confirm.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nUse the [Update Secondary Zone Configuration endpoint](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/peers/methods/update/) to link your Cloudflare zone to the peer DNS server.\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n4. On the [**DNS Settings**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings) page, confirm the linked peer is listed under **DNS Zone Transfers**, and select **Initiate zone transfer**. Alternatively, you can use the [Force AXFR endpoint](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/force_axfr/methods/create/)."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-partial-to-secondary.mdx"],"digest":[0,"ed34f7f1e70713d3"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-secondary-to-full"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert secondary setup to full setup"],"description":[0,"If you initially set up incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary), you can later convert your zone to use a full setup."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,7],"label":[0,"Secondary to full"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially set up [incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary)](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/setup/), you can later convert your zone to use a [primary setup](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/) (also know as full setup).\n\n<Render file=\"conversion-subdomain-setup-callout\" product=\"dns\" />\n\nFollow the steps below to achieve this conversion.\n\n## 1. Stop transferring the zone\n\n<Render file=\"secondary-conversion-unlink\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 2. Prepare for the conversion\n\n1. Plan for [DNSSEC settings](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/dnssec-for-secondary/). If you were previously using [Pre-signed DNSSEC](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/dnssec-for-secondary/#set-up-pre-signed-dnssec), consider disabling DNSSEC before starting the conversion.\n2. Make sure the [proxy statuses](/dns/proxy-status/) of your DNS records are consistently set:\n   - If you have [Secondary DNS override](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/proxy-traffic/), confirm each record has the appropriate setting (**Proxied** or **DNS only**).\n   - If [Secondary DNS override](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/proxy-traffic/) is disabled, make sure all of your DNS records are listed as **DNS only**.\n\n3. (Optional) For consistency, use the [Update DNS Settings](/api/resources/dns/subresources/settings/subresources/zone/methods/edit/) endpoint to specify SOA record fields according to your needs. Once Cloudflare automatically generates an SOA record for your zone on primary setup (full), the field overrides will be considered.\n\n## 3. Convert your zone\n\n1. Use the [Edit Zone endpoint](/api/resources/zones/methods/edit/) with `type` set to `full` to convert the zone type. Existing DNS records will not be affected.\n2. Go to the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page and take note of your new **Cloudflare Nameservers**.\n3. At your domain registrar (or parent zone), [update your nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/). Replace the nameservers ending in `secondary.cloudflare.com` by the ones ending in `ns.cloudflare.com`.\n\n   :::note\n   If Cloudflare will be your only primary DNS provider, remove any other nameservers as well.\n   :::\n\n4. Delete the previous SOA record to make sure Cloudflare generates a new one.\n5. (Optional) If Cloudflare was previously not signing your records and you wish to use DNSSEC, follow the steps to [Enable DNSSEC](/dns/dnssec/#enable-dnssec)."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-secondary-to-full.mdx"],"digest":[0,"4ca2384f20017447"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-secondary-to-partial"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Convert secondary setup to partial setup"],"description":[0,"If you initially set up incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary), you can later convert your zone to use a partial setup."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,8],"label":[0,"Secondary to partial"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { GlossaryTooltip, Details, Render } from \"~/components\";\n\nIf you initially set up [incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as secondary)](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/setup/), you can later convert your zone to use a <GlossaryTooltip term=\"CNAME setup\">CNAME setup (partial)</GlossaryTooltip>.\n\n<Render file=\"conversion-subdomain-setup-callout\" product=\"dns\" />\n\nFollow the steps below to achieve this conversion.\n\n## 1. Stop transferring the zone\n\n<Render file=\"secondary-conversion-unlink\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 2. Configure your authoritative DNS provider\n\n1. (Optional) If you are also migrating to a new authoritative DNS provider, export a zone file from the previous provider and import it into the new one.\n2. At your authoritative DNS provider, create `CNAME` records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net` for every hostname you wish to proxy through Cloudflare.\n\n   <Details header=\"Example CNAME record at authoritative DNS provider\">\n\n   The `CNAME` record for `www.example.com` would be:\n\n   ```txt\n   www.example.com CNAME www.example.com.cdn.cloudflare.net\n   ```\n\n   </Details>\n\n3. At your authoritative DNS provider, remove any previously existing `A`, `AAAA`, or `CNAME` records referencing the hostnames you want to proxy through Cloudflare. For these hostnames, leave only the records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net`.\n\n## 3. Convert your Cloudflare zone\n\n1. Back at your Cloudflare zone, confirm that you have all the `A`, `AAAA`, or `CNAME` [DNS records](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/create-dns-records/) needed for the hostnames you pointed to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net` in the previous step. You can also delete any DNS records that have a different type, as they will no longer resolve once you convert your zone to a CNAME setup (partial).\n2. Use the [Edit Zone endpoint](/api/resources/zones/methods/edit/) with `type` set to `partial` to convert the zone type. Existing DNS records will not be affected.\n3. On the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page, get the **Verification TXT Record** and add it at your authoritative DNS provider.\n\n   <Details header=\"Example verification record\">\n\n   A verification record for `sub.example.com` might be:\n\n   | Type | Name                                | Content             |\n   | ---- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------- |\n   | TXT  | `cloudflare-verify.sub.example.com` | 966215192-518620144 |\n\n   If your authoritative DNS provider automatically appends DNS record `name` fields with your domain, make sure to only insert `cloudflare-verify` as the record name. Otherwise, it may result in an incorrect record name, such as `cloudflare-verify.sub.example.com.sub.example.com`.\n\n   After creating the record, you can use this [Dig Web Interface link](https://digwebinterface.com/?type=TXT&ns=auth&nameservers=) to search (`dig`) for `cloudflare-verify.<YOUR DOMAIN>` and validate if it is working.\n\n   </Details>\n\n   :::note\n   The verification record must remain in place for as long as you want your CNAME setup (partial) to be active on Cloudflare.\n   :::\n\n## 4. Update nameservers\n\nAt your domain registrar (or parent zone), [update the nameservers](/dns/nameservers/update-nameservers/). In a CNAME setup (partial), only the nameservers of your external DNS provider should be listed.\n\n    - Remove any `secondary.cloudflare.com` nameservers if you used to have them.\n    - If you are also migrating to a new authoritative DNS provider, add your new nameservers."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/conversions/convert-secondary-to-partial.mdx"],"digest":[0,"9f5fec25f601f6e3"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/partial-setup/setup"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Setup"],"description":[0,"A CNAME setup (also known as partial) allows you to use Cloudflare's reverse proxy while maintaining your primary and authoritative DNS provider."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Set up a partial zone (CNAME setup)"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Details, Render, GlossaryTooltip, Steps, Tabs, TabItem, APIRequest } from \"~/components\";\n\n<Render file=\"partial-setup-definition\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n:::note\nA CNAME setup (partial) is only available to customers on a Business or Enterprise plan.\nPartial setups are not supported on Cloudflare Registrar domains.\n:::\n\n---\n\n## Before you begin\n\n<Steps>\n\n1. Create a Cloudflare account and add your domain.\n2. Choose **Business** or **Enterprise** as your plan.\n3. If you are onboarding a new domain to Cloudflare, ignore the instructions to change your nameservers.\n4. (Recommended) Plan for SSL/TLS certificates:\n\n   If you are only using [Universal SSL](/ssl/edge-certificates/universal-ssl/) prior to converting your zone, a certificate will be provisioned for your subdomains only after each of the respective DNS records ([step 3](#3-add-dns-records) below) are [proxied](/dns/proxy-status/). Refer to [Enable Universal SSL](/ssl/edge-certificates/universal-ssl/enable-universal-ssl/#partial-dns-setup) for details.\n\n   If your domain is sensitive to downtime, instead of using Universal SSL, consider using an [advanced certificate](/ssl/edge-certificates/advanced-certificate-manager/) with [delegated DCV](/ssl/edge-certificates/changing-dcv-method/methods/delegated-dcv/#setup).\n\n</Steps>\n\n## 1. Convert your zone and review DNS records\n\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n:::caution[Make sure you have the correct plan]\nMake sure your zone is on the Business or Enterprise plan. If you have Free or Pro, the options mentioned below will not be displayed.\n:::\n\n<Steps>\n1. On the **Overview** page, select **Convert to CNAME DNS Setup**.\n2. Select **Convert** to confirm.\n3. Save the information from the **Verification TXT Record**. If you lose the information, you can also access it on the [**DNS Records**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/records) page, under **Verification TXT Record**.\n4. Make sure that you have all the DNS records (A, AAAA, or CNAME) for subdomains that you want to proxy through Cloudflare.\n\n</Steps>\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nIf you are adding a zone for the first time via API you can add it directly with a `type` of `partial`, without converting it.\n\n<APIRequest\n  path=\"/zones\"\n  method=\"POST\"\n  json={{\n    \"name\": \"example.com\",\n    \"account\": {\n      \"id\": \"YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID\"\n    },\n    \"type\": \"partial\"\n  }}\n/>\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 2. Verify ownership for your domain\n\n<Render file=\"partial-setup-verification-record\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 3. Add DNS records\n\n<Steps>\n\n1. At your authoritative DNS provider:\n   1. Create CNAME records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net` for every hostname you wish to proxy through Cloudflare.\n\n   <Details header=\"Example CNAME record at authoritative DNS provider\">\n\n   The CNAME record for `www.example.com` would be:\n\n   ```txt\n   www.example.com CNAME www.example.com.cdn.cloudflare.net\n   ```\n\n   </Details>\n2. Remove any previously existing A, AAAA, or CNAME records referencing the hostnames you want to proxy through Cloudflare. For these hostnames, leave only the records pointing to `{your-hostname}.cdn.cloudflare.net`.\n\n2. Repeat this process for each subdomain that should be proxied to Cloudflare.\n\n</Steps>\n\n---\n\n## Other record types\n\nIf you are preparing a conversion from CNAME setup (partial) to primary setup (full), or if you have a more specific use case, you can use the [Create DNS Record](/api/resources/dns/subresources/records/methods/create/) API endpoint to create DNS records of any supported type."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/partial-setup/setup.mdx"],"digest":[0,"b473a44c78691dc2"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"queues/tutorials/handle-rate-limits"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Handle rate limits of external APIs"],"description":[0,"Example of how to use Queues to handle rate limits of external APIs."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Cloudflare Queues - Queues & Rate Limits"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1002],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"TypeScript"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Beginner"],"reviewed":[3,"2024-09-25T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"queues"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"EtjQvV"],"name":[0,"Queues"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Queues"],"url":[0,"/queues/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Storage"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Queues docs"],"description":[0,"Reliably send and receive messages without the egress fees"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/queues.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b7e5012d0738ccc7"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"summary":[0,"Example of how to use Queues to handle rate limits of external APIs."],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"],[0,"Queues"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, PackageManagers, WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nThis tutorial explains how to use Queues to handle rate limits of external APIs by building an application that sends email notifications using [Resend](https://www.resend.com/). However, you can use this pattern to handle rate limits of any external API.\n\nResend is a service that allows you to send emails from your application via an API. Resend has a default [rate limit](https://resend.com/docs/api-reference/introduction#rate-limit) of two requests per second. You will use Queues to handle the rate limit of Resend.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n<Render file=\"prereqs\" product=\"workers\" />\n\n4. Sign up for [Resend](https://resend.com/) and generate an API key by following the guide on the [Resend documentation](https://resend.com/docs/dashboard/api-keys/introduction).\n\n5. Additionally, you will need access to Cloudflare Queues.\n\n<Render file=\"enable-queues\" product=\"queues\" />\n\n## 1. Create a new Workers application\n\nTo get started, create a Worker application using the [`create-cloudflare` CLI](https://github.com/cloudflare/workers-sdk/tree/main/packages/create-cloudflare). Open a terminal window and run the following command:\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"create\"\n\tpkg=\"cloudflare@latest\"\n\targs={\"resend-rate-limit-queue\"}\n/>\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"c3-post-run-steps\"\n\tproduct=\"workers\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tcategory: \"hello-world\",\n\t\ttype: \"Worker only\",\n\t\tlang: \"TypeScript\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\nThen, go to your newly created directory:\n\n```sh frame=\"none\"\ncd resend-rate-limit-queue\n```\n\n## 2. Set up a Queue\n\nYou need to create a Queue and a binding to your Worker. Run the following command to create a Queue named `rate-limit-queue`:\n\n```sh title=\"Create a Queue\"\nnpx wrangler queues create rate-limit-queue\n```\n\n```sh output\nCreating queue rate-limit-queue.\nCreated queue rate-limit-queue.\n```\n\n### Add Queue bindings to your [Wrangler configuration file](/workers/wrangler/configuration/)\n\nIn your Wrangler file, add the following:\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"queues\": {\n\t\t\"producers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"binding\": \"EMAIL_QUEUE\",\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"rate-limit-queue\"\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t],\n\t\t\"consumers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"rate-limit-queue\",\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_size\": 2,\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_timeout\": 10,\n\t\t\t\t\"max_retries\": 3\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t]\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nIt is important to include the `max_batch_size` of two to the consumer queue is important because the Resend API has a default rate limit of two requests per second. This batch size allows the queue to process the message in the batch size of two. If the batch size is less than two, the queue will wait for 10 seconds to collect the next message. If no more messages are available, the queue will process the message in the batch. For more information, refer to the [Batching, Retries and Delays documentation](/queues/configuration/batching-retries)\n\nYour final Wrangler file should look similar to the example below.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"$schema\": \"./node_modules/wrangler/config-schema.json\",\n\t\"name\": \"resend-rate-limit-queue\",\n\t\"main\": \"src/index.ts\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"compatibility_flags\": [\n\t\t\"nodejs_compat\"\n\t],\n\t\"queues\": {\n\t\t\"producers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"binding\": \"EMAIL_QUEUE\",\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"rate-limit-queue\"\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t],\n\t\t\"consumers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"rate-limit-queue\",\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_size\": 2,\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_timeout\": 10,\n\t\t\t\t\"max_retries\": 3\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t]\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n## 3. Add bindings to environment\n\nAdd the bindings to the environment interface in `worker-configuration.d.ts`, so TypeScript correctly types the bindings. The queue is typed as `Queue<Message>`, where `Message` is defined in the following step.\n\n```ts title=\"worker-configuration.d.ts\"\ninterface Env {\n\tEMAIL_QUEUE: Queue<Message>;\n}\n```\n\n## 4. Send message to the queue\n\nThe application will send a message to the queue when the Worker receives a request. For simplicity, you will send the email address as a message to the queue. A new message will be sent to the queue with a delay of one second.\n\n```ts title=\"src/index.ts\"\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tawait env.EMAIL_QUEUE.send(\n\t\t\t\t{ email: await req.text() },\n\t\t\t\t{ delaySeconds: 1 },\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Error!\", { status: 500 });\n\t\t}\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;\n```\n\nThis will accept requests to any subpath and forwards the request's body. It expects that the request body to contain only an email. In production, you should check that the request was a `POST` request. You should also avoid sending such sensitive information (email) directly to the queue. Instead, you can send a message to the queue that contains a unique identifier for the user. Then, your consumer queue can use the unique identifier to look up the email address in a database and use that to send the email.\n\n## 5. Process the messages in the queue\n\nAfter the message is sent to the queue, it will be processed by the consumer Worker. The consumer Worker will process the message and send the email.\n\nSince you have not configured Resend yet, you will log the message to the console. After you configure Resend, you will use it to send the email.\n\nAdd the `queue()` handler as shown below:\n\n```ts title=\"src/index.ts\" ins={1-3,17-28}\ninterface Message {\n\temail: string;\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tawait env.EMAIL_QUEUE.send(\n\t\t\t\t{ email: await req.text() },\n\t\t\t\t{ delaySeconds: 1 },\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Error!\", { status: 500 });\n\t\t}\n\t},\n\tasync queue(batch, env, ctx): Promise<void> {\n\t\tfor (const message of batch.messages) {\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\tconsole.log(message.body.email);\n\t\t\t\t// After configuring Resend, you can send email\n\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\t\tconsole.error(e);\n\t\t\t\tmessage.retry({ delaySeconds: 5 });\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env, Message>;\n```\n\nThe above `queue()` handler will log the email address to the console and send the email. It will also retry the message if sending the email fails. The `delaySeconds` is set to five seconds to avoid sending the email too quickly.\n\nTo test the application, run the following command:\n\n```sh title=\"Start the development server\"\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nUse the following cURL command to send a request to the application:\n\n```sh title=\"Test with a cURL request\"\ncurl -X POST -d \"test@example.com\" http://localhost:8787/\n```\n\n```sh output\n[wrangler:inf] POST / 200 OK (2ms)\nQueueMessage {\n  attempts: 1,\n  body: { email: 'test@example.com' },\n  timestamp: 2024-09-12T13:48:07.236Z,\n  id: '72a25ff18dd441f5acb6086b9ce87c8c'\n}\n```\n\n## 6. Set up Resend\n\nTo call the Resend API, you need to configure the Resend API key. Create a `.dev.vars` file in the root of your project and add the following:\n\n```txt title=\".dev.vars\"\nRESEND_API_KEY='your-resend-api-key'\n```\n\nReplace `your-resend-api-key` with your actual Resend API key.\n\nNext, update the `Env` interface in `worker-configuration.d.ts` to include the `RESEND_API_KEY` variable.\n\n```ts title=\"worker-configuration.d.ts\" ins={3}\ninterface Env {\n\tEMAIL_QUEUE: Queue<Message>;\n\tRESEND_API_KEY: string;\n}\n```\n\nLastly, install the [`resend` package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/resend) using the following command:\n\n<PackageManagers pkg=\"resend\" />\n\nYou can now use the `RESEND_API_KEY` variable in your code.\n\n## 7. Send email with Resend\n\nIn your `src/index.ts` file, import the Resend package and update the `queue()` handler to send the email.\n\n```ts title=\"src/index.ts\" ins={1,21,26-40} del={24,41}\nimport { Resend } from \"resend\";\n\ninterface Message {\n\temail: string;\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tawait env.EMAIL_QUEUE.send(\n\t\t\t\t{ email: await req.text() },\n\t\t\t\t{ delaySeconds: 1 },\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Error!\", { status: 500 });\n\t\t}\n\t},\n\tasync queue(batch, env, ctx): Promise<void> {\n\t\t// Initialize Resend\n\t\tconst resend = new Resend(env.RESEND_API_KEY);\n\t\tfor (const message of batch.messages) {\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\tconsole.log(message.body.email);\n\t\t\t\t// send email\n\t\t\t\tconst sendEmail = await resend.emails.send({\n\t\t\t\t\tfrom: \"onboarding@resend.dev\",\n\t\t\t\t\tto: [message.body.email],\n\t\t\t\t\tsubject: \"Hello World\",\n\t\t\t\t\thtml: \"<strong>Sending an email from Worker!</strong>\",\n\t\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\t\t// check if the email failed\n\t\t\t\tif (sendEmail.error) {\n\t\t\t\t\tconsole.error(sendEmail.error);\n\t\t\t\t\tmessage.retry({ delaySeconds: 5 });\n\t\t\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t\t\t// if success, ack the message\n\t\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\t\tconsole.error(e);\n\t\t\t\tmessage.retry({ delaySeconds: 5 });\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env, Message>;\n```\n\nThe `queue()` handler will now send the email using the Resend API. It also checks if sending the email failed and will retry the message.\n\nThe final script is included below:\n\n```ts title=\"src/index.ts\"\nimport { Resend } from \"resend\";\n\ninterface Message {\n\temail: string;\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tawait env.EMAIL_QUEUE.send(\n\t\t\t\t{ email: await req.text() },\n\t\t\t\t{ delaySeconds: 1 },\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"Error!\", { status: 500 });\n\t\t}\n\t},\n\tasync queue(batch, env, ctx): Promise<void> {\n\t\t// Initialize Resend\n\t\tconst resend = new Resend(env.RESEND_API_KEY);\n\t\tfor (const message of batch.messages) {\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\t// send email\n\t\t\t\tconst sendEmail = await resend.emails.send({\n\t\t\t\t\tfrom: \"onboarding@resend.dev\",\n\t\t\t\t\tto: [message.body.email],\n\t\t\t\t\tsubject: \"Hello World\",\n\t\t\t\t\thtml: \"<strong>Sending an email from Worker!</strong>\",\n\t\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\t\t// check if the email failed\n\t\t\t\tif (sendEmail.error) {\n\t\t\t\t\tconsole.error(sendEmail.error);\n\t\t\t\t\tmessage.retry({ delaySeconds: 5 });\n\t\t\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t\t\t// if success, ack the message\n\t\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t} catch (e) {\n\t\t\t\tconsole.error(e);\n\t\t\t\tmessage.retry({ delaySeconds: 5 });\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env, Message>;\n```\n\nTo test the application, start the development server using the following command:\n\n```sh title=\"Start the development server\"\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nUse the following cURL command to send a request to the application:\n\n```sh title=\"Test with a cURL request\"\ncurl -X POST -d \"delivered@resend.dev\" http://localhost:8787/\n```\n\nOn the Resend dashboard, you should see that the email was sent to the provided email address.\n\n## 8. Deploy your Worker\n\nTo deploy your Worker, run the following command:\n\n```sh title=\"Deploy your Worker\"\nnpx wrangler deploy\n```\n\nLastly, add the Resend API key using the following command:\n\n```sh title=\"Add the Resend API key\"\nnpx wrangler secret put RESEND_API_KEY\n```\n\nEnter the value of your API key. Your API key will get added to your project. You can now use the `RESEND_API_KEY` variable in your code.\n\nYou have successfully created a Worker which can send emails using the Resend API respecting rate limits.\n\nTo test your Worker, you could use the following cURL request. Replace `<YOUR_WORKER_URL>` with the URL of your deployed Worker.\n\n```bash title=\"Test with a cURL request\"\ncurl -X POST -d \"delivered@resend.dev\" <YOUR_WORKER_URL>\n```\n\nRefer to the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/harshil1712/queues-rate-limit) for the complete code for this tutorial. If you are using [Hono](https://hono.dev/), you can refer to the [Hono example](https://github.com/harshil1712/resend-rate-limit-demo).\n\n## Related resources\n\n- [How Queues works](/queues/reference/how-queues-works/)\n- [Queues Batching and Retries](/queues/configuration/batching-retries/)\n- [Resend](https://resend.com/docs/)"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/queues/tutorials/handle-rate-limits/index.mdx"],"digest":[0,"13169f30d36cb0d7"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"queues/tutorials/web-crawler-with-browser-rendering"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Build a web crawler with Queues and Browser Rendering"],"description":[0,"Example of how to use Queues and Browser Rendering to power a web crawler."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Cloudflare Queues - Queues & Browser Rendering"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1002],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"TypeScript"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Intermediate"],"reviewed":[3,"2024-08-06T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"queues"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"browser-rendering"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"queues"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"EtjQvV"],"name":[0,"Queues"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Queues"],"url":[0,"/queues/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"Storage"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Queues docs"],"description":[0,"Reliably send and receive messages without the egress fees"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/queues.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b7e5012d0738ccc7"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"summary":[0,"Example of how to use Queues and Browser Rendering to power a web crawler."],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"],[0,"Queues"],[0,"Browser Rendering"],[0,"Queues"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, PackageManagers, WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nThis tutorial explains how to build and deploy a web crawler with Queues, [Browser Rendering](/browser-rendering/), and [Puppeteer](/browser-rendering/puppeteer/).\n\nPuppeteer is a high-level library used to automate interactions with Chrome/Chromium browsers. On each submitted page, the crawler will find the number of links to `cloudflare.com` and take a screenshot of the site, saving results to [Workers KV](/kv/).\n\nYou can use Puppeteer to request all images on a page, save the colors used on a site, and more.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n<Render file=\"prereqs\" product=\"workers\" />\n\n## 1. Create new Workers application\n\nTo get started, create a Worker application using the [`create-cloudflare` CLI](https://github.com/cloudflare/workers-sdk/tree/main/packages/create-cloudflare). Open a terminal window and run the following command:\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"create\"\n\tpkg=\"cloudflare@latest\"\n\targs={\"queues-web-crawler\"}\n/>\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"c3-post-run-steps\"\n\tproduct=\"workers\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tcategory: \"hello-world\",\n\t\ttype: \"Worker only\",\n\t\tlang: \"TypeScript\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\nThen, move into your newly created directory:\n\n```sh\ncd queues-web-crawler\n```\n\n## 2. Create KV namespace\n\nWe need to create a KV store. This can be done through the Cloudflare dashboard or the Wrangler CLI. For this tutorial, we will use the Wrangler CLI.\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"exec\"\n\tpkg=\"wrangler\"\n\targs=\"kv namespace create crawler_links\"\n/>\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"exec\"\n\tpkg=\"wrangler\"\n\targs=\"kv namespace create crawler_screenshots\"\n/>\n\n```sh output\n🌀 Creating namespace with title \"web-crawler-crawler-links\"\n✨ Success!\nAdd the following to your configuration file in your kv_namespaces array:\n[[kv_namespaces]]\nbinding = \"crawler_links\"\nid = \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n\n🌀 Creating namespace with title \"web-crawler-crawler-screenshots\"\n✨ Success!\nAdd the following to your configuration file in your kv_namespaces array:\n[[kv_namespaces]]\nbinding = \"crawler_screenshots\"\nid = \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n```\n\n### Add KV bindings to the [Wrangler configuration file](/workers/wrangler/configuration/)\n\nThen, in your Wrangler file, add the following with the values generated in the terminal:\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"kv_namespaces\": [\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV\",\n\t\t\t\"id\": \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n\t\t},\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_LINKS_KV\",\n\t\t\t\"id\": \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n\t\t}\n\t]\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n## 3. Set up Browser Rendering\n\nNow, you need to set up your Worker for Browser Rendering.\n\nIn your current directory, install Cloudflare's [fork of Puppeteer](/browser-rendering/puppeteer/) and also [robots-parser](https://www.npmjs.com/package/robots-parser):\n\n<PackageManagers pkg=\"@cloudflare/puppeteer\" dev />\n\n<PackageManagers pkg=\"robots-parser\" />\n\nThen, add a Browser Rendering binding. Adding a Browser Rendering binding gives the Worker access to a headless Chromium instance you will control with Puppeteer.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"browser\": {\n\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_BROWSER\"\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n## 4. Set up a Queue\n\nNow, we need to set up the Queue.\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"exec\"\n\tpkg=\"wrangler\"\n\targs=\"queues create queues-web-crawler\"\n/>\n\n```txt title=\"Output\"\nCreating queue queues-web-crawler.\nCreated queue queues-web-crawler.\n```\n\n### Add Queue bindings to Wrangler configuration\n\nThen, in your Wrangler file, add the following:\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"queues\": {\n\t\t\"consumers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"queues-web-crawler\",\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_timeout\": 60\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t],\n\t\t\"producers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"queues-web-crawler\",\n\t\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_QUEUE\"\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t]\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nAdding the `max_batch_timeout` of 60 seconds to the consumer queue is important because Browser Rendering has a limit of two new browsers per minute per account. This timeout waits up to a minute before collecting queue messages into a batch. The Worker will then remain under this browser invocation limit.\n\nYour final Wrangler file should look similar to the one below.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"$schema\": \"./node_modules/wrangler/config-schema.json\",\n\t\"name\": \"web-crawler\",\n\t\"main\": \"src/index.ts\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"compatibility_flags\": [\n\t\t\"nodejs_compat\"\n\t],\n\t\"kv_namespaces\": [\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV\",\n\t\t\t\"id\": \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n\t\t},\n\t\t{\n\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_LINKS_KV\",\n\t\t\t\"id\": \"<GENERATED_NAMESPACE_ID>\"\n\t\t}\n\t],\n\t\"browser\": {\n\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_BROWSER\"\n\t},\n\t\"queues\": {\n\t\t\"consumers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"queues-web-crawler\",\n\t\t\t\t\"max_batch_timeout\": 60\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t],\n\t\t\"producers\": [\n\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\"queue\": \"queues-web-crawler\",\n\t\t\t\t\"binding\": \"CRAWLER_QUEUE\"\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t]\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n## 5. Add bindings to environment\n\nAdd the bindings to the environment interface in `src/index.ts`, so TypeScript correctly types the bindings. The queue is typed as `Queue<Message>`, where `Message` is defined in the following step.\n\n```ts\nimport type { BrowserWorker } from \"@cloudflare/puppeteer\";\n\nexport interface Env {\n\tCRAWLER_QUEUE: Queue<Message>;\n\tCRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV: KVNamespace;\n\tCRAWLER_LINKS_KV: KVNamespace;\n\tCRAWLER_BROWSER: BrowserWorker;\n}\n```\n\n## 6. Submit links to crawl\n\nAdd a `fetch()` handler to the Worker to submit links to crawl.\n\n```ts\ntype Message = {\n\turl: string;\n};\n\nexport interface Env {\n\tCRAWLER_QUEUE: Queue<Message>;\n\t// ... etc.\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\tawait env.CRAWLER_QUEUE.send({ url: await req.text() });\n\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;\n```\n\nThis will accept requests to any subpath and forwards the request's body to be crawled. It expects that the request body only contains a URL. In production, you should check that the request was a `POST` request and contains a well-formed URL in its body. This has been omitted for simplicity.\n\n## 7. Crawl with Puppeteer\n\nAdd a `queue()` handler to the Worker to process the links you send.\n\n```ts\nimport puppeteer from \"@cloudflare/puppeteer\";\nimport robotsParser from \"robots-parser\";\n\nasync queue(batch, env, ctx): Promise<void> {\n  let browser: puppeteer.Browser | null = null;\n  try {\n    browser = await puppeteer.launch(env.CRAWLER_BROWSER);\n  } catch {\n    batch.retryAll();\n\treturn;\n  }\n\n  for (const message of batch.messages) {\n    const { url } = message.body;\n\n    let isAllowed = true;\n    try {\n      const robotsTextPath = new URL(url).origin + \"/robots.txt\";\n      const response = await fetch(robotsTextPath);\n\n      const robots = robotsParser(robotsTextPath, await response.text());\n      isAllowed = robots.isAllowed(url) ?? true; // respect robots.txt!\n    } catch {}\n\n    if (!isAllowed) {\n      message.ack();\n      continue;\n    }\n\n\t// TODO: crawl!\n    message.ack();\n  }\n\n  await browser.close();\n},\n```\n\nThis is a skeleton for the crawler. It launches the Puppeteer browser and iterates through the Queue's received messages. It fetches the site's `robots.txt` and uses `robots-parser` to check that this site allows crawling. If crawling is not allowed, the message is `ack`'ed, removing it from the Queue. If crawling is allowed, you can continue to crawl the site.\n\nThe `puppeteer.launch()` is wrapped in a `try...catch` to allow the whole batch to be retried if the browser launch fails. The browser launch may fail due to going over the limit for number of browsers per account.\n\n```ts\ntype Result = {\n\tnumCloudflareLinks: number;\n\tscreenshot: ArrayBuffer;\n};\n\nconst crawlPage = async (url: string): Promise<Result> => {\n\tconst page = await (browser as puppeteer.Browser).newPage();\n\n\tawait page.goto(url, {\n\t\twaitUntil: \"load\",\n\t});\n\n\tconst numCloudflareLinks = await page.$$eval(\"a\", (links) => {\n\t\tlinks = links.filter((link) => {\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\treturn new URL(link.href).hostname.includes(\"cloudflare.com\");\n\t\t\t} catch {\n\t\t\t\treturn false;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t});\n\t\treturn links.length;\n\t});\n\n\tawait page.setViewport({\n\t\twidth: 1920,\n\t\theight: 1080,\n\t\tdeviceScaleFactor: 1,\n\t});\n\n\treturn {\n\t\tnumCloudflareLinks,\n\t\tscreenshot: ((await page.screenshot({ fullPage: true })) as Buffer).buffer,\n\t};\n};\n```\n\nThis helper function opens a new page in Puppeteer and navigates to the provided URL. `numCloudflareLinks` uses Puppeteer's `$$eval` (equivalent to `document.querySelectorAll`) to find the number of links to a `cloudflare.com` page. Checking if the link's `href` is to a `cloudflare.com` page is wrapped in a `try...catch` to handle cases where `href`s may not be URLs.\n\nThen, the function sets the browser viewport size and takes a screenshot of the full page. The screenshot is returned as a `Buffer` so it can be converted to an `ArrayBuffer` and written to KV.\n\nTo enable recursively crawling links, add a snippet after checking the number of Cloudflare links to send messages recursively from the queue consumer to the queue itself. Recursing too deep, as is possible with crawling, will cause a Durable Object `Subrequest depth limit exceeded.` error. If one occurs, it is caught, but the links are not retried.\n\n```ts null {3-14}\n// const numCloudflareLinks = await page.$$eval(\"a\", (links) => { ...\n\nawait page.$$eval(\"a\", async (links) => {\n\tconst urls: MessageSendRequest<Message>[] = links.map((link) => {\n\t\treturn {\n\t\t\tbody: {\n\t\t\t\turl: link.href,\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t};\n\t});\n\ttry {\n\t\tawait env.CRAWLER_QUEUE.sendBatch(urls);\n\t} catch {} // do nothing, likely hit subrequest limit\n});\n\n// await page.setViewport({ ...\n```\n\nThen, in the `queue` handler, call `crawlPage` on the URL.\n\n```ts null {8-23}\n// in the `queue` handler:\n// ...\nif (!isAllowed) {\n\tmessage.ack();\n\tcontinue;\n}\n\ntry {\n\tconst { numCloudflareLinks, screenshot } = await crawlPage(url);\n\tconst timestamp = new Date().getTime();\n\tconst resultKey = `${encodeURIComponent(url)}-${timestamp}`;\n\tawait env.CRAWLER_LINKS_KV.put(resultKey, numCloudflareLinks.toString(), {\n\t\tmetadata: { date: timestamp },\n\t});\n\tawait env.CRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV.put(resultKey, screenshot, {\n\t\tmetadata: { date: timestamp },\n\t});\n\tmessage.ack();\n} catch {\n\tmessage.retry();\n}\n\n// ...\n```\n\nThis snippet saves the results from `crawlPage` into the appropriate KV namespaces. If an unexpected error occurred, the URL will be retried and resent to the queue again.\n\nSaving the timestamp of the crawl in KV helps you avoid crawling too frequently.\n\nAdd a snippet before checking `robots.txt` to check KV for a crawl within the last hour. This lists all KV keys beginning with the same URL (crawls of the same page), and check if any crawls have been done within the last hour. If any crawls have been done within the last hour, the message is `ack`'ed and not retried.\n\n```ts null {12-23}\ntype KeyMetadata = {\n  date: number;\n};\n\n// in the `queue` handler:\n// ...\nfor (const message of batch.messages) {\n  const sameUrlCrawls = await env.CRAWLER_LINKS_KV.list({\n    prefix: `${encodeURIComponent(url)}`,\n  });\n\n  let shouldSkip = false;\n  for (const key of sameUrlCrawls.keys) {\n    if (timestamp - (key.metadata as KeyMetadata)?.date < 60 * 60 * 1000) {\n      // if crawled in last hour, skip\n      message.ack();\n      shouldSkip = true;\n      break;\n    }\n  }\n  if (shouldSkip) {\n    continue;\n  }\n\n  let isAllowed = true;\n  // ...\n```\n\nThe final script is included below.\n\n```ts\nimport puppeteer, { BrowserWorker } from \"@cloudflare/puppeteer\";\nimport robotsParser from \"robots-parser\";\n\ntype Message = {\n\turl: string;\n};\n\nexport interface Env {\n\tCRAWLER_QUEUE: Queue<Message>;\n\tCRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV: KVNamespace;\n\tCRAWLER_LINKS_KV: KVNamespace;\n\tCRAWLER_BROWSER: BrowserWorker;\n}\n\ntype Result = {\n\tnumCloudflareLinks: number;\n\tscreenshot: ArrayBuffer;\n};\n\ntype KeyMetadata = {\n\tdate: number;\n};\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(req, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {\n\t\t// util endpoint for testing purposes\n\t\tawait env.CRAWLER_QUEUE.send({ url: await req.text() });\n\t\treturn new Response(\"Success!\");\n\t},\n\tasync queue(batch, env, ctx): Promise<void> {\n\t\tconst crawlPage = async (url: string): Promise<Result> => {\n\t\t\tconst page = await (browser as puppeteer.Browser).newPage();\n\n\t\t\tawait page.goto(url, {\n\t\t\t\twaitUntil: \"load\",\n\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\tconst numCloudflareLinks = await page.$$eval(\"a\", (links) => {\n\t\t\t\tlinks = links.filter((link) => {\n\t\t\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\t\t\treturn new URL(link.href).hostname.includes(\"cloudflare.com\");\n\t\t\t\t\t} catch {\n\t\t\t\t\t\treturn false;\n\t\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\t});\n\t\t\t\treturn links.length;\n\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\t// to crawl recursively - uncomment this!\n\t\t\t/*await page.$$eval(\"a\", async (links) => {\n        const urls: MessageSendRequest<Message>[] = links.map((link) => {\n          return {\n            body: {\n              url: link.href,\n            },\n          };\n        });\n        try {\n          await env.CRAWLER_QUEUE.sendBatch(urls);\n        } catch {} // do nothing, might've hit subrequest limit\n      });*/\n\n\t\t\tawait page.setViewport({\n\t\t\t\twidth: 1920,\n\t\t\t\theight: 1080,\n\t\t\t\tdeviceScaleFactor: 1,\n\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\treturn {\n\t\t\t\tnumCloudflareLinks,\n\t\t\t\tscreenshot: ((await page.screenshot({ fullPage: true })) as Buffer)\n\t\t\t\t\t.buffer,\n\t\t\t};\n\t\t};\n\n\t\tlet browser: puppeteer.Browser | null = null;\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tbrowser = await puppeteer.launch(env.CRAWLER_BROWSER);\n\t\t} catch {\n\t\t\tbatch.retryAll();\n\t\t\treturn;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tfor (const message of batch.messages) {\n\t\t\tconst { url } = message.body;\n\t\t\tconst timestamp = new Date().getTime();\n\t\t\tconst resultKey = `${encodeURIComponent(url)}-${timestamp}`;\n\n\t\t\tconst sameUrlCrawls = await env.CRAWLER_LINKS_KV.list({\n\t\t\t\tprefix: `${encodeURIComponent(url)}`,\n\t\t\t});\n\n\t\t\tlet shouldSkip = false;\n\t\t\tfor (const key of sameUrlCrawls.keys) {\n\t\t\t\tif (timestamp - (key.metadata as KeyMetadata)?.date < 60 * 60 * 1000) {\n\t\t\t\t\t// if crawled in last hour, skip\n\t\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t\t\tshouldSkip = true;\n\t\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\tif (shouldSkip) {\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\tlet isAllowed = true;\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\tconst robotsTextPath = new URL(url).origin + \"/robots.txt\";\n\t\t\t\tconst response = await fetch(robotsTextPath);\n\n\t\t\t\tconst robots = robotsParser(robotsTextPath, await response.text());\n\t\t\t\tisAllowed = robots.isAllowed(url) ?? true; // respect robots.txt!\n\t\t\t} catch {}\n\n\t\t\tif (!isAllowed) {\n\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\ttry {\n\t\t\t\tconst { numCloudflareLinks, screenshot } = await crawlPage(url);\n\t\t\t\tawait env.CRAWLER_LINKS_KV.put(\n\t\t\t\t\tresultKey,\n\t\t\t\t\tnumCloudflareLinks.toString(),\n\t\t\t\t\t{ metadata: { date: timestamp } },\n\t\t\t\t);\n\t\t\t\tawait env.CRAWLER_SCREENSHOTS_KV.put(resultKey, screenshot, {\n\t\t\t\t\tmetadata: { date: timestamp },\n\t\t\t\t});\n\t\t\t\tmessage.ack();\n\t\t\t} catch {\n\t\t\t\tmessage.retry();\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tawait browser.close();\n\t},\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env, Message>;\n```\n\n## 8. Deploy your Worker\n\nTo deploy your Worker, run the following command:\n\n<PackageManagers type=\"exec\" pkg=\"wrangler\" args=\"deploy\" />\n\nYou have successfully created a Worker which can submit URLs to a queue for crawling and save results to Workers KV.\n\nTo test your Worker, you could use the following cURL request to take a screenshot of this documentation page.\n\n```bash title=\"Test with a cURL request\"\ncurl <YOUR_WORKER_URL> \\\n  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n  -d 'https://developers.cloudflare.com/queues/tutorials/web-crawler-with-browser-rendering/'\n\n```\n\nRefer to the [GitHub repository for the complete tutorial](https://github.com/cloudflare/queues-web-crawler), including a front end deployed with Pages to submit URLs and view crawler results.\n\n## Related resources\n\n- [How Queues works](/queues/reference/how-queues-works/)\n- [Queues Batching and Retries](/queues/configuration/batching-retries/)\n- [Browser Rendering](/browser-rendering/)\n- [Puppeteer Examples](https://github.com/puppeteer/puppeteer/tree/main/examples)"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/queues/tutorials/web-crawler-with-browser-rendering/index.mdx"],"digest":[0,"46fe32e5f701d8f1"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"reference-architecture/diagrams/ai/ai-composable"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Composable AI architecture"],"description":[0,"The architecture diagram illustrates how AI applications can be built end-to-end on Cloudflare, or single services can be integrated with external infrastructure and services."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1],"label":[0,"Composable AI
