A [Cloudflare account](https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up) with Workers and Workers AI enabled.\n2. Your `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` and `CLOUDFLARE_AUTH_TOKEN`.\n   - You can generate an API token in your Cloudflare dashboard under API Tokens.\n3. Node.js installed for working with Cloudflare Workers (optional but recommended).\n\n## 1. Agree to Meta's license\n\nThe first time you use the [Llama 3.2 11B Vision Instruct](/workers-ai/models/llama-3.2-11b-vision-instruct) model, you need to agree to Meta's License and Acceptable Use Policy.\n\n```bash title=\"curl\"\ncurl https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts/$CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID/ai/run/@cf/meta/llama-3.2-11b-vision-instruct \\\n  -X POST \\\n  -H \"Authorization: Bearer $CLOUDFLARE_AUTH_TOKEN\" \\\n  -d '{ \"prompt\": \"agree\" }'\n```\n\nReplace `$CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` and `$CLOUDFLARE_AUTH_TOKEN` with your actual account ID and token.\n\n## 2. Set up your Cloudflare Worker\n\n1. Create a Worker Project\n   You will create a new Worker project using the `create-cloudflare` CLI (`C3`). This tool simplifies setting up and deploying new applications to Cloudflare.\n\n   Run the following command in your terminal:\n\n<PackageManagers\n\ttype=\"create\"\n\tpkg=\"cloudflare@latest\"\n\targs={\"llama-vision-tutorial\"}\n/>\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"c3-post-run-steps\"\n\tproduct=\"workers\"\n\tparams={{\n\t\tcategory: \"hello-world\",\n\t\ttype: \"Worker only\",\n\t\tlang: \"JavaScript\",\n\t}}\n/>\n\nAfter completing the setup, a new directory called `llama-vision-tutorial` will be created.\n\n2. Navigate to your application directory\n   Change into the project directory:\n\n   ```bash\n   cd llama-vision-tutorial\n   ```\n\n3. Project structure\n   Your `llama-vision-tutorial` directory will include:\n   - A \"Hello World\" Worker at `src/index.ts`.\n   - A `wrangler.json` configuration file for managing deployment settings.\n\n## 3. Write the Worker code\n\nEdit the `src/index.ts` (or `index.js` if you are not using TypeScript) file and replace the content with the following code:\n\n```javascript\nexport interface Env {\n  AI: Ai;\n}\n\nexport default {\n  async fetch(request, env): Promise<Response> {\n    const messages = [\n      { role: \"system\", content: \"You are a helpful assistant.\" },\n      { role: \"user\", content: \"Describe the image I'm providing.\" },\n    ];\n\n    // Replace this with your image data encoded as base64 or a URL\n    const imageBase64 = \"data:image/png;base64,IMAGE_DATA_HERE\";\n\n    const response = await env.AI.run(\"@cf/meta/llama-3.2-11b-vision-instruct\", {\n      messages,\n      image: imageBase64,\n    });\n\n    return Response.json(response);\n  },\n} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;\n```\n\n## 4. Bind Workers AI to your Worker\n\n1. Open the [Wrangler configuration file](/workers/wrangler/configuration/) and add the following configuration:\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"env\": {},\n\t\"ai\": {\n\t\t\"binding\": \"AI\"\n\t}\n}\n```\n</WranglerConfig>\n\n2. Save the file.\n\n## 5. Deploy the Worker\n\nRun the following command to deploy your Worker:\n\n```bash\nwrangler deploy\n```\n\n## 6. Test Your Worker\n\n1. After deployment, you will receive a unique URL for your Worker (e.g., `https://llama-vision-tutorial.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev`).\n2. Use a tool like `curl` or Postman to send a request to your Worker:\n\n```bash\ncurl -X POST https://llama-vision-tutorial.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev \\\n  -d '{ \"image\": \"BASE64_ENCODED_IMAGE\" }'\n```\n\nReplace `BASE64_ENCODED_IMAGE` with an actual base64-encoded image string.\n\n## 7. Verify the response\n\nThe response will include the output from the model, such as a description or answer to your prompt based on the image provided.\n\nExample response:\n\n```json\n{\n\t\"result\": \"This is a golden retriever sitting in a grassy park.\"\n}\n```"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/workers-ai/guides/tutorials/llama-vision-tutorial.mdx"],"digest":[0,"646c4e6fd05a8bfd"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers-ai/guides/tutorials/using-bigquery-with-workers-ai"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Using BigQuery with Workers AI"],"description":[0,"Learn how to ingest data stored outside of Cloudflare as an input to Workers AI models."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,2],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"tags":[1,[[0,"AI"],[0,"JavaScript"]]],"difficulty":[0,"Beginner"],"reviewed":[3,"2024-10-21T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers-ai"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers-ai"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"FTasgK"],"name":[0,"Workers AI"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Workers AI"],"url":[0,"/workers-ai/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"additional_groups":[1,[[0,"AI"]]],"tags":[1,[[0,"AI"]]]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Workers AI docs"],"description":[0,"Run AI models in Workers, Pages, or via API"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/workers-ai.yaml"],"digest":[0,"5d9fe2244888b108"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"],[0,"Workers AI"],[0,"Workers AI"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nThe easiest way to get started with [Workers AI](/workers-ai/) is to try it out in the [Multi-modal Playground](https://multi-modal.ai.cloudflare.com/) and the [LLM playground](https://playground.ai.cloudflare.com/). If you decide that you want to integrate your code with Workers AI, you may then decide to use its [REST API endpoints](/workers-ai/get-started/rest-api/) or a [Worker binding](/workers-ai/configuration/bindings/).\n\nBut what about the data? What if you want these models to ingest data that is stored outside Cloudflare?\n\nIn this tutorial, you will learn how to bring data from Google BigQuery to a Cloudflare Worker so that it can be used as input for Workers AI models.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nYou will need:\n\n- A [Cloudflare Worker](/workers/) project running a [Hello World script](/workers/get-started/guide/).\n- A Google Cloud Platform [service account](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create#iam-service-accounts-create-console) with an [associated key](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/keys-create-delete#iam-service-account-keys-create-console) file downloaded that has read access to BigQuery.\n- Access to a BigQuery table with some test data that allows you to create a [BigQuery Job Query](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs/query). For this tutorial it is recommended you that you create your own table as [sampled tables](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/public-data#sample_tables), unless cloned to your own GCP namespace, won't allow you to run job queries against them. For this example, the [Hacker News Corpus](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/hacker-news/hacker-news-corpus) was used under its MIT licence.\n\n## 1. Set up your Cloudflare Worker\n\nTo ingest the data into Cloudflare and feed it into Workers AI, you will be using a [Cloudflare Worker](/workers/). If you have not created one yet, please review our [tutorial on how to get started](/workers/get-started/).\n\nAfter following the steps to create a Worker, you should have the following code in your new Worker project:\n\n```javascript\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\treturn new Response(\"Hello World!\");\n\t},\n};\n```\n\nIf the Worker project has successfully been created, you should also be able to run `npx wrangler dev` in a console to run the Worker locally:\n\n```sh\n[wrangler:inf] Ready on http://localhost:8787\n```\n\nOpen a browser tab at `http://localhost:8787/` to see your deployed Worker. Please note that the port `8787` may be a different one in your case.\n\nYou should be seeing `Hello World!` in your browser:\n\n```sh\nHello World!\n```\n\nIf you run into any issues during this step, please review the [Worker's Get Started Guide](/workers/get-started/guide/).\n\n## 2. Import GCP Service key into the Worker as Secrets\n\nNow that you have verified that the Worker has been created successfully, you will need to reference the Google Cloud Platform service key created in the [Prerequisites](#prerequisites) section of this tutorial.\n\nYour downloaded key JSON file from Google Cloud Platform should have the following format:\n\n```json\n{\n\t\"type\": \"service_account\",\n\t\"project_id\": \"<your_project_id>\",\n\t\"private_key_id\": \"<your_private_key_id>\",\n\t\"private_key\": \"<your_private_key>\",\n\t\"client_email\": \"<your_service_account_id>@<your_project_id>.iam.gserviceaccount.com\",\n\t\"client_id\": \"<your_oauth2_client_id>\",\n\t\"auth_uri\": \"https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth\",\n\t\"token_uri\": \"https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token\",\n\t\"auth_provider_x509_cert_url\": \"https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/certs\",\n\t\"client_x509_cert_url\": \"https://www.googleapis.com/robot/v1/metadata/x509/<your_service_account_id>%40<your_project_id>.iam.gserviceaccount.com\",\n\t\"universe_domain\": \"googleapis.com\"\n}\n```\n\nFor this tutorial, you will only need the values of the following fields: `client_email`, `private_key`, `private_key_id`, and `project_id`.\n\nInstead of storing this information in plain text in the Worker, you will use [Secrets](/workers/configuration/secrets/) to make sure its unencrypted content is only accessible via the Worker itself.\n\nImport those three values from the JSON file into Secrets, starting with the field from the JSON key file called `client_email`, which we will now call `BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL` (you can use another variable name):\n\n```sh\nnpx wrangler secret put BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL\n```\n\nYou will be asked to enter a secret value, which will be the value of the field `client_email` in the JSON key file.\n\n:::note\n\nDo not include any double quotes in the secret that you store, as it will already be interpreted as a string.\n\n:::\n\nIf the secret was uploaded successfully, the following message will be displayed:\n\n```sh\n✨ Success! Uploaded secret BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL\n```\n\nNow import the secrets for the three remaining fields; `private_key`, `private_key_id`, and `project_id` as `BQ_PRIVATE_KEY`, `BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID`, and `BQ_PROJECT_ID` respectively:\n\n```sh\nnpx wrangler secret put BQ_PRIVATE_KEY\n```\n\n```sh\nnpx wrangler secret put BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID\n```\n\n```sh\nnpx wrangler secret put BQ_PROJECT_ID\n```\n\nAt this point, you have successfully imported three fields from the JSON key file downloaded from Google Cloud Platform into Cloudflare Secrets to be used in a Worker.\n\n[Secrets](/workers/configuration/secrets/) are only made available to Workers once they are deployed. To make them available during development, [create a `.dev.vars`](/workers/configuration/secrets/#local-development-with-secrets) file to locally store these credentials and reference them as environment variables.\n\nYour `dev.vars` file should look like the following:\n\n```\nBQ_CLIENT_EMAIL=\"<your_service_account_id>@<your_project_id>.iam.gserviceaccount.com\"\nBQ_CLIENT_KEY=\"-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----<content_of_your_private_key>-----END PRIVATE KEY-----\\n\"\nBQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID=\"<your_private_key_id>\"\nBQ_PROJECT_ID=\"<your_project_id>\"\n```\n\nMake sure to include `.dev.vars` in your project `.gitignore` file to prevent your credentials being uploaded to a repository when using version control.\n\nCheck the secrets are loaded correctly in `src/index.js` by logging their values into a console output, as follows:\n\n```javascript\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\tconsole.log(\"BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL: \", env.BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL);\n\t\tconsole.log(\"BQ_PRIVATE_KEY: \", env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY);\n\t\tconsole.log(\"BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID: \", env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID);\n\t\tconsole.log(\"BQ_PROJECT_ID: \", env.BQ_PROJECT_ID);\n\t\treturn new Response(\"Hello World!\");\n\t},\n};\n```\n\nRestart the Worker and run `npx wrangler dev`. You should see that the server now mentions the newly added variables:\n\n```\nUsing vars defined in .dev.vars\nYour worker has access to the following bindings:\n- Vars:\n  - BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL: \"(hidden)\"\n  - BQ_PRIVATE_KEY: \"(hidden)\"\n  - BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID: \"(hidden)\"\n  - BQ_PROJECT_ID: \"(hidden)\"\n[wrangler:inf] Ready on http://localhost:8787\n```\n\nIf you open `http://localhost:8787` in your browser, you should see the values of the variables show up in your console where the `npx wrangler dev` command is running, while still seeing only the `Hello World!` text in the browser window.\n\nYou now have access to the GCP credentials from a Worker. Next, you will install a library to help with the creation of the JSON Web Token needed to interact with GCP's API.\n\n## 3. Install library to handle JWT operations\n\nTo interact with BigQuery's REST API, you will need to generate a [JSON Web Token](https://jwt.io/introduction) to authenticate your requests using the credentials that you have loaded into Worker secrets in the previous step.\n\nFor this tutorial, you will be using the [jose](https://www.npmjs.com/package/jose?activeTab=readme) library for JWT-related operations. Install it by running the following command in a console:\n\n```sh\nnpm i jose\n```\n\nTo verify that the installation succeeded, you can run `npm list`, which lists all the installed packages, to check if the `jose` dependency has been added:\n\n```sh\n<project_name>@0.0.0\n/<path_to_your_project>/<project_name>\n├── @cloudflare/vitest-pool-workers@0.4.29\n├── jose@5.9.2\n├── vitest@1.5.0\n└── wrangler@3.75.0\n```\n\n## 4. Generate JSON web token\n\nNow that you have installed the `jose` library, it is time to import it and add a function to your code that generates a signed JSON Web Token (JWT):\n\n```javascript\nimport * as jose from 'jose';\n...\nconst generateBQJWT = async (aCryptoKey, env) => {\nconst algorithm = \"RS256\";\nconst audience = \"https://bigquery.googleapis.com/\";\nconst expiryAt = (new Date().valueOf() / 1000);\n\tconst privateKey = await jose.importPKCS8(env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY, algorithm);\n\n\t// Generate signed JSON Web Token (JWT)\n\treturn new jose.SignJWT()\n    \t.setProtectedHeader({\n        \ttyp: 'JWT',\n        \talg: algorithm,\n        \tkid: env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID\n    \t})\n    \t.setIssuer(env.BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL)\n    \t.setSubject(env.BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL)\n    \t.setAudience(audience)\n    \t.setExpirationTime(expiryAt)\n    \t.setIssuedAt()\n    \t.sign(privateKey)\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n       ...\n// Create JWT to authenticate the BigQuery API call\n    \tlet bqJWT;\n    \ttry {\n        \tbqJWT = await generateBQJWT(env);\n    \t} catch (e) {\n        \treturn new Response('An error has occurred while generating the JWT', { status: 500 })\n    \t}\n\t},\n       ...\n};\n\n```\n\nNow that you have created a JWT, it is time to do an API call to BigQuery to fetch some data.\n\n## 5. Make authenticated requests to Google BigQuery\n\nWith the JWT token created in the previous step, issue an API request to BigQuery's API to retrieve data from a table.\n\nYou will now query the table that you created in BigQuery earlier in this tutorial. This example uses a sampled version of the [Hacker News Corpus](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/hacker-news/hacker-news-corpus) that was used under its MIT licence and uploaded to BigQuery.\n\n```javascript\nconst queryBQ = async (bqJWT, path) => {\n\tconst bqEndpoint = `https://bigquery.googleapis.com${path}`\n\t// In this example, text is a field in the BigQuery table that is being queried (hn.news_sampled)\n\tconst query = 'SELECT text FROM hn.news_sampled LIMIT 3';\n\tconst response = await fetch(bqEndpoint, {\n    \tmethod: \"POST\",\n    \tbody: JSON.stringify({\n        \t\"query\": query\n    \t}),\n    \theaders: {\n        \tAuthorization: `Bearer ${bqJWT}`\n    \t}\n\t})\n\treturn response.json()\n}\n...\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\t...\n    \t\tlet ticketInfo;\n    \t\ttry {\n    \t\tticketInfo = await queryBQ(bqJWT);\n    \t} catch (e) {\n        \treturn new Response('An error has occurred while querying BQ', { status: 500 });\n    \t}\n\t...\n\t},\n};\n```\n\nHaving the raw row data from BigQuery means that you can now format it in a JSON-like style next.\n\n## 6. Format results from the query\n\nNow that you have retrieved the data from BigQuery, your BigQuery API response should look something like this:\n\n```json\n{\n\t...\n\t\"schema\": {\n    \t\"fields\": [\n        \t{\n            \t\"name\": \"title\",\n            \t\"type\": \"STRING\",\n            \t\"mode\": \"NULLABLE\"\n        \t},\n        \t{\n            \t\"name\": \"text\",\n            \t\"type\": \"STRING\",\n            \t\"mode\": \"NULLABLE\"\n        \t}\n    \t]\n\t},\n\t...\n\t\"rows\": [\n    \t{\n        \t\"f\": [\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t},\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t}\n        \t]\n    \t},\n    \t{\n        \t\"f\": [\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t},\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t}\n        \t]\n    \t},\n    \t{\n        \t\"f\": [\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t},\n            \t{\n                \t\"v\": \"<some_value>\"\n            \t}\n        \t]\n    \t}\n\t],\n\t...\n}\n```\n\nThis format may be difficult to read and work with when iterating through results. So you will now implement a function that maps the schema into each individual value, and the resulting output will be easier to read, as shown below. Each row corresponds to an object within an array.\n\n```javascript\n[\n\t{\n\t\ttitle: \"<some_value>\",\n\t\ttext: \"<some_value>\",\n\t},\n\t{\n\t\ttitle: \"<some_value>\",\n\t\ttext: \"<some_value>\",\n\t},\n\t{\n\t\ttitle: \"<some_value>\",\n\t\ttext: \"<some_value>\",\n\t},\n];\n```\n\nCreate a `formatRows` function that takes a number of rows and fields returned from the BigQuery response body and returns an array of results as objects with named fields.\n\n```javascript\nconst formatRows = (rowsWithoutFieldNames, fields) => {\n\t// Index to fieldName\n\tconst fieldsByIndex = new Map();\n\n\t// Load all fields by name and have their index in the array result as their key\n\tfields.forEach((field, index) => {\n    \tfieldsByIndex.set(index, field.name)\n\t})\n\n\t// Iterate through rows\n\tconst rowsWithFieldNames = rowsWithoutFieldNames.map(row => {\n    \t// Per each row represented by an array f, iterate through the unnamed values and find their field names by searching them in the fieldsByIndex.\n    \tlet newRow = {}\n    \trow.f.forEach((field, index) => {\n        \tconst fieldName = fieldsByIndex.get(index);\n        \tif (fieldName) {\n\t\t// For every field in a row, add them to newRow\n            \tnewRow = ({ ...newRow, [fieldName]: field.v });\n        \t}\n    \t})\n    \treturn newRow\n\t})\n\n\treturn rowsWithFieldNames\n}\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\t...\n    \t// Transform output format into array of objects with named fields\n    \tlet formattedResults;\n\n    \tif ('rows' in ticketInfo) {\n        \tformattedResults = formatRows(ticketInfo.rows, ticketInfo.schema.fields);\n        \tconsole.log(formattedResults)\n    \t} else if ('error' in ticketInfo) {\n        \treturn new Response(ticketInfo.error.message, { status: 500 })\n    \t}\n\t...\n\t},\n};\n```\n\n## 7. Feed data into Workers AI\n\nNow that you have converted the response from the BigQuery API into an array of results, generate some tags and attach an associated sentiment score using an LLM via [Workers AI](/workers-ai/):\n\n```javascript\nconst generateTags = (data, env) => {\n\treturn env.AI.run(\"@cf/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct\", {\n    \tprompt: `Create three one-word tags for the following text. return only these three tags separated by a comma. don't return text that is not a category.Lowercase only. ${JSON.stringify(data)}`,\n\t});\n}\n\nconst generateSentimentScore = (data, env) => {\n\treturn env.AI.run(\"@cf/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct\", {\n    \tprompt: `return a float number between 0 and 1 measuring the sentiment of the following text. 0 being negative and 1 positive. return only the number, no text. ${JSON.stringify(data)}`,\n\t});\n}\n\n// Iterates through values, sends them to an AI handler and encapsulates all responses into a single Promise\nconst getAIGeneratedContent = (data, env, aiHandler) => {\n\tlet results = data?.map(dataPoint => {\n    \treturn aiHandler(dataPoint, env)\n\t})\n\treturn Promise.all(results)\n}\n...\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\t...\nlet summaries, sentimentScores;\n    \ttry {\n        \tsummaries = await getAIGeneratedContent(formattedResults, env, generateTags);\n        \tsentimentScores = await getAIGeneratedContent(formattedResults, env, generateSentimentScore)\n    \t} catch {\n        \treturn new Response('There was an error while generating the text summaries or sentiment scores')\n    \t}\n},\n\nformattedResults = formattedResults?.map((formattedResult, i) => {\n        \tif (sentimentScores[i].response && summaries[i].response) {\n            \treturn {\n                \t...formattedResult,\n                \t'sentiment': parseFloat(sentimentScores[i].response).toFixed(2),\n                \t'tags': summaries[i].response.split(',').map((result) => result.trim())\n            \t}\n        \t}\n    \t}\n};\n\n```\n\nUncomment the following lines from the Wrangler file in your project:\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"ai\": {\n\t\t\"binding\": \"AI\"\n\t}\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nRestart the Worker that is running locally, and after doing so, go to your application endpoint:\n\n```sh\ncurl http://localhost:8787\n```\n\nIt is likely that you will be asked to log in to your Cloudflare account and grant temporary access to Wrangler (the Cloudflare CLI) to use your account when using Worker AI.\n\nOnce you access `http://localhost:8787` you should see an output similar to the following:\n\n```sh\n{\n  \"data\": [\n\t{\n  \t\"text\": \"You can see a clear spike in submissions right around US Thanksgiving.\",\n  \t\"sentiment\": \"0.61\",\n  \t\"tags\": [\n    \t\"trends\",\n    \t\"submissions\",\n    \t\"thanksgiving\"\n  \t]\n\t},\n\t{\n  \t\"text\": \"I didn't test the changes before I published them.  I basically did development on the running server. In fact for about 30 seconds the comments page was broken due to a bug.\",\n  \t\"sentiment\": \"0.35\",\n  \t\"tags\": [\n    \t\"software\",\n    \t\"deployment\",\n    \t\"error\"\n  \t]\n\t},\n\t{\n  \t\"text\": \"I second that. As I recall, it's a very enjoyable 700-page brain dump by someone who's really into his subject. The writing has a personal voice; there are lots of asides, dry wit, and typos that suggest restrained editing. The discussion is intelligent and often theoretical (and Bartle is not scared to use mathematical metaphors), but the tone is not academic.\",\n  \t\"sentiment\": \"0.86\",\n  \t\"tags\": [\n    \t\"review\",\n    \t\"game\",\n    \t\"design\"\n  \t]\n\t}\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nThe actual values and fields will mostly depend on the query made in Step 5 that is then fed into the LLM.\n\n## Final result\n\nAll the code shown in the different steps is combined into the following code in `src/index.js`:\n\n```javascript\nimport * as jose from \"jose\";\n\nconst generateBQJWT = async (env) => {\n\tconst algorithm = \"RS256\";\n\tconst audience = \"https://bigquery.googleapis.com/\";\n\tconst expiryAt = new Date().valueOf() / 1000;\n\tconst privateKey = await jose.importPKCS8(env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY, algorithm);\n\n\t// Generate signed JSON Web Token (JWT)\n\treturn new jose.SignJWT()\n\t\t.setProtectedHeader({\n\t\t\ttyp: \"JWT\",\n\t\t\talg: algorithm,\n\t\t\tkid: env.BQ_PRIVATE_KEY_ID,\n\t\t})\n\t\t.setIssuer(env.BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL)\n\t\t.setSubject(env.BQ_CLIENT_EMAIL)\n\t\t.setAudience(audience)\n\t\t.setExpirationTime(expiryAt)\n\t\t.setIssuedAt()\n\t\t.sign(privateKey);\n};\n\nconst queryBQ = async (bgJWT, path) => {\n\tconst bqEndpoint = `https://bigquery.googleapis.com${path}`;\n\tconst query = \"SELECT text FROM hn.news_sampled LIMIT 3\";\n\tconst response = await fetch(bqEndpoint, {\n\t\tmethod: \"POST\",\n\t\tbody: JSON.stringify({\n\t\t\tquery: query,\n\t\t}),\n\t\theaders: {\n\t\t\tAuthorization: `Bearer ${bgJWT}`,\n\t\t},\n\t});\n\treturn response.json();\n};\n\nconst formatRows = (rowsWithoutFieldNames, fields) => {\n\t// Index to fieldName\n\tconst fieldsByIndex = new Map();\n\n\tfields.forEach((field, index) => {\n\t\tfieldsByIndex.set(index, field.name);\n\t});\n\n\tconst rowsWithFieldNames = rowsWithoutFieldNames.map((row) => {\n\t\t// Map rows into an array of objects with field names\n\t\tlet newRow = {};\n\t\trow.f.forEach((field, index) => {\n\t\t\tconst fieldName = fieldsByIndex.get(index);\n\t\t\tif (fieldName) {\n\t\t\t\tnewRow = { ...newRow, [fieldName]: field.v };\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t});\n\t\treturn newRow;\n\t});\n\n\treturn rowsWithFieldNames;\n};\n\nconst generateTags = (data, env) => {\n\treturn env.AI.run(\"@cf/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct\", {\n\t\tprompt: `Create three one-word tags for the following text. return only these three tags separated by a comma. don't return text that is not a category.Lowercase only. ${JSON.stringify(data)}`,\n\t});\n};\n\nconst generateSentimentScore = (data, env) => {\n\treturn env.AI.run(\"@cf/meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct\", {\n\t\tprompt: `return a float number between 0 and 1 measuring the sentiment of the following text. 0 being negative and 1 positive. return only the number, no text. ${JSON.stringify(data)}`,\n\t});\n};\n\nconst getAIGeneratedContent = (data, env, aiHandler) => {\n\tlet results = data?.map((dataPoint) => {\n\t\treturn aiHandler(dataPoint, env);\n\t});\n\treturn Promise.all(results);\n};\n\nexport default {\n\tasync fetch(request, env, ctx) {\n\t\t// Create JWT to authenticate the BigQuery API call\n\t\tlet bqJWT;\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tbqJWT = await generateBQJWT(env);\n\t\t} catch (error) {\n\t\t\tconsole.log(error);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"An error has occurred while generating the JWT\", {\n\t\t\t\tstatus: 500,\n\t\t\t});\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Fetch results from BigQuery\n\t\tlet ticketInfo;\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tticketInfo = await queryBQ(\n\t\t\t\tbqJWT,\n\t\t\t\t`/bigquery/v2/projects/${env.BQ_PROJECT_ID}/queries`,\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t} catch (error) {\n\t\t\tconsole.log(error);\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\"An error has occurred while querying BQ\", {\n\t\t\t\tstatus: 500,\n\t\t\t});\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Transform output format into array of objects with named fields\n\t\tlet formattedResults;\n\t\tif (\"rows\" in ticketInfo) {\n\t\t\tformattedResults = formatRows(ticketInfo.rows, ticketInfo.schema.fields);\n\t\t} else if (\"error\" in ticketInfo) {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(ticketInfo.error.message, { status: 500 });\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Generate AI summaries and sentiment scores\n\t\tlet summaries, sentimentScores;\n\t\ttry {\n\t\t\tsummaries = await getAIGeneratedContent(\n\t\t\t\tformattedResults,\n\t\t\t\tenv,\n\t\t\t\tgenerateTags,\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t\tsentimentScores = await getAIGeneratedContent(\n\t\t\t\tformattedResults,\n\t\t\t\tenv,\n\t\t\t\tgenerateSentimentScore,\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t} catch {\n\t\t\treturn new Response(\n\t\t\t\t\"There was an error while generating the text summaries or sentiment scores\",\n\t\t\t);\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Add AI summaries and sentiment scores to previous results\n\t\tformattedResults = formattedResults?.map((formattedResult, i) => {\n\t\t\tif (sentimentScores[i].response && summaries[i].response) {\n\t\t\t\treturn {\n\t\t\t\t\t...formattedResult,\n\t\t\t\t\tsentiment: parseFloat(sentimentScores[i].response).toFixed(2),\n\t\t\t\t\ttags: summaries[i].response.split(\",\").map((result) => result.trim()),\n\t\t\t\t};\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t});\n\n\t\tconst response = { data: formattedResults };\n\n\t\treturn new Response(JSON.stringify(response), {\n\t\t\theaders: { \"Content-Type\": \"application/json\" },\n\t\t});\n\t},\n};\n```\n\nIf you wish to deploy this Worker, you can do so by running `npx wrangler deploy`:\n\n```sh\nTotal Upload: <size_of_your_worker> KiB / gzip: <compressed_size_of_your_worker> KiB\nUploaded <name_of_your_worker> (x sec)\nDeployed <name_of_your_worker> triggers (x sec)\n  https://<your_public_worker_endpoint>\nCurrent Version ID: <worker_script_version_id>\n```\n\nThis will create a public endpoint that you can use to access the Worker globally. Please keep this in mind when using production data, and make sure to include additional access controls in place.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nIn this tutorial, you have learnt how to integrate Google BigQuery and Cloudflare Workers by creating a GCP service account key and storing part of it as Worker secrets. This was later imported in the code, and by using the `jose` npm library, you created a JSON Web Token to authenticate the API query to BigQuery.\n\nOnce you obtained the results, you formatted them to pass to generative AI models via Workers AI to generate tags and to perform sentiment analysis on the extracted data.\n\n## Next Steps\n\nIf, instead of displaying the results of ingesting the data to the AI model in a browser, your workflow requires fetching and store data (for example in [R2](/r2/) or [D1](/d1/)) on regular intervals, you may want to consider adding a [scheduled handler](/workers/runtime-apis/handlers/scheduled/) for this Worker. This enables you to trigger the Worker with a predefined cadence via a [Cron Trigger](/workers/configuration/cron-triggers/). Consider reviewing the Reference Architecture Diagrams on [Ingesting BigQuery Data into Workers AI](/reference-architecture/diagrams/ai/bigquery-workers-ai/).\n\nA use case to ingest data from other sources, like you did in this tutorial, is to create a RAG system. If this sounds relevant to you, please check out the [Build a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) AI tutorial](/workers-ai/guides/tutorials/build-a-retrieval-augmented-generation-ai/).\n\nTo learn more about what other AI models you can use at Cloudflare, please visit the [Workers AI](/workers-ai) section of our docs."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/workers-ai/guides/tutorials/using-bigquery-with-workers-ai.mdx"],"digest":[0,"1fa6ce649e98873e"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers/static-assets/migration-guides/netlify-to-workers"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Migrate from Netlify to Workers"],"description":[0,"Migrate your Netlify application to Cloudflare Workers. You should already have an existing project deployed on Netlified that you would like to host on Workers."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"difficulty":[0,"Beginner"],"reviewed":[3,"2025-05-16T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"zderhn"],"name":[0,"Workers"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Workers"],"url":[0,"/workers/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"preview_tryout":[0,true]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Workers docs"],"description":[0,"Build, deploy, and scale serverless applications globally with low latency and minimal configuration"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/workers.yaml"],"digest":[0,"f00ae79b1cae79ce"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nIn this tutorial, you will learn how to migrate your Netlify application to Cloudflare Workers.\n\nYou should already have an existing project deployed on Netlify that you would like to host on Cloudflare Workers. Netlify specific features are not supported by Cloudflare Workers. Review the [Workers compatibility matrix](/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/migrate-from-pages/#compatibility-matrix) for more information on what is supported.\n\n## Frameworks\n\nSome frameworks like Next.js, Astro with on demand rendering, and others have specific guides for migrating to Cloudflare Workers. Refer to our [framework guides](/workers/framework-guides/) for more information. If your framework has a **Deploy an existing project on Workers** guide, follow that guide for specific instructions. Otherwise, continue with the steps below.\n\n## Find your build command and build directory\n\nTo move your application to Cloudflare Workers, you will need to know your build command and build directory. Cloudflare Workers will use this information to build and deploy your application. We will cover how to find these values in the Netlify Dashboard below.\n\nIn your Netlify Dashboard, find the project you want to migrate to Workers. Go to the **Project configuration** menu for your specific project, then go into the **Build & deploy** menu item. You will find a **Build settings** card that includes the **Build command** and **Publish directory** fields. Save these for deploying to Cloudflare Workers. In the below image, the **Build Command** is `npm run build`, and the **Output Directory** is `.next`.\n\n![Finding the Build Command and publish Directory fields](~/assets/images/workers/migrations/netlify-build-command.png)\n\n## Create a wrangler file\n\nIn the root of your project, create a `wrangler.jsonc` or `wrangler.toml` file (`wrangler.jsonc` is recommended). What goes in the file depends on what type of application you are deploying: an application powered by [Static Site Generation (SSG)](/workers/static-assets/routing/static-site-generation/), or a [Single Page Application (SPA)](/workers/static-assets/routing/single-page-application/).\n\nFor each case, be sure to update the `<your-project-name>` value with the name of your project and `<your-build-directory>` value with the build directory from Netlify.\n\nFor a **static site**, you will need to add the following to your wrangler file.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"name\": \"<your-project-name>\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"assets\": {\n\t\t\"directory\": \"<your-build-directory>\",\n\t},\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nFor a **Single Page Application**, you will need to add the following to your Wrangler configuration file, which includes the `not_found_handling` field.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"name\": \"<your-project-name>\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"assets\": {\n\t\t\"directory\": \"<your-build-directory>\",\n\t\t\"not_found_handling\": \"single-page-application\",\n\t},\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nSome frameworks provide specific guides for migrating to Cloudflare Workers. Please refer to our [framework guides](/workers/framework-guides/) for more information. If your framework includes a “Deploy an existing project on Workers” guide, follow it for detailed instructions.\n\n## Create a new Workers project\n\nYour application has the proper configuration to be built and deployed to Cloudflare Workers.\n\nThe [Connect a new Worker](/workers/ci-cd/builds/#connect-a-new-worker) guide will instruct you how to connect your GitHub project to Cloudflare Workers. In the configuration step, ensure your build command is the same as the command you found on Netlify. Also, the deploy command should be the default `npx wrangler deploy`.\n\n## Add a custom domain\n\nWorkers Custom Domains only supports domains that are configured as zones on your account. A zone refers to a domain (such as example.com) that Cloudflare manages for you, including its DNS and traffic.\n\nFollow these instructions for [adding a custom domain to your Workers project](/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains/#add-a-custom-domain). You will also find additional information on creating a zone for your domain.\n\n## Delete your Netlify app\n\nOnce your custom domain is set up and sending requests to Cloudflare Workers, you can safely delete your Netlify application.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\nFor additional migration instructions, review the [Cloudflare Pages to Workers migration guide](/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/migrate-from-pages/). While not Netlify specific, it does cover some additional steps that may be helpful."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/netlify-to-workers.mdx"],"digest":[0,"6585e5bc0655633a"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers/static-assets/migration-guides/vercel-to-workers"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Migrate from Vercel to Workers"],"description":[0,"Migrate your Vercel application to Cloudflare Workers. You should already have an existing project deployed on Vercel that you would like to host on Workers."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"difficulty":[0,"Beginner"],"reviewed":[3,"2025-04-25T00:00:00.000Z"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"workers"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"zderhn"],"name":[0,"Workers"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"Workers"],"url":[0,"/workers/"],"group":[0,"Developer platform"],"preview_tryout":[0,true]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare Workers docs"],"description":[0,"Build, deploy, and scale serverless applications globally with low latency and minimal configuration"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/workers.yaml"],"digest":[0,"f00ae79b1cae79ce"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Workers"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { WranglerConfig } from \"~/components\";\n\nIn this tutorial, you will learn how to migrate your Vercel application to Cloudflare Workers.\n\nYou should already have an existing project deployed on Vercel that you would like to host on Cloudflare Workers. Vercel specific features are not supported by Cloudflare Workers. Review the [Workers compatibility matrix](/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/migrate-from-pages/#compatibility-matrix) for more information on what is supported.\n\n## Frameworks\n\nSome frameworks like Next.js, Astro with on demand rendering, and others have specific guides for migrating to Cloudflare Workers. Refer to our [framework guides](/workers/framework-guides/) for more information. If your framework has a **Deploy an existing project on Workers** guide, follow that guide for specific instructions. Otherwise, continue with the steps below.\n\n## Find your build command and build directory\n\nTo move your application to Cloudflare Workers, you will need to know your build command and build directory. Cloudflare Workers will use this information to build and deploy your application. We'll cover how to find these values in the Vercel Dashboard below.\n\nIn your Vercel Dashboard, find the project you want to migrate to Workers. Go to the **Settings** tab for your specific project and find the **Build & Development settings** panel. You will find the **Build Command** and **Output Directory** fields there. If you are using a framework, these values may not be filled in but will show the defaults used by the framework. Save these for deploying to Cloudflare Workers. In the below image, the **Build Command** is `npm run build`, and the **Output Directory** is `dist`.\n\n![Finding the Build Command and Output Directory fields](~/assets/images/workers/migrations/vercel-deploy-1.png)\n\n## Create a wrangler file\n\nIn the root of your project, create a `wrangler.jsonc` or `wrangler.toml` file (`wrangler.jsonc` is recommended). What goes in the file depends on what type of application you are deploying: static or single-page application.\n\nFor each case, be sure to update the `<your-project-name>` value with the name of your project and `<your-build-directory>` value with the build directory from Vercel. Be sure to set the right pathing, for example `./dist` if the build directory is `dist` or `./build` if your build directory is `build`.\n\nFor a **static site**, you will need to add the following to your wrangler file.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"name\": \"<your-project-name>\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"assets\": {\n\t\t\"directory\": \"<your-build-directory>\",\n\t},\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nFor a **single page application**, you will need to add the following to your wrangler file, which includes the `not_found_handling` field.\n\n<WranglerConfig>\n\n```jsonc\n{\n\t\"name\": \"<your-project-name>\",\n\t\"compatibility_date\": \"$today\",\n\t\"assets\": {\n\t\t\"directory\": \"<your-build-directory>\",\n\t\t\"not_found_handling\": \"single-page-application\",\n\t},\n}\n```\n\n</WranglerConfig>\n\nSome frameworks provide specific guides for migrating to Cloudflare Workers. Please refer to our [framework guides](/workers/framework-guides/) for more information. If your framework includes a “Deploy an existing project on Workers” guide, follow it for detailed instructions.\n\n## Create a new Workers project\n\nYour application has the proper configuration to be built and deployed to Cloudflare Workers.\n\nThe [Connect a new Worker](/workers/ci-cd/builds/#connect-a-new-worker) guide will instruct you how to connect your GitHub project to Cloudflare Workers. In the configuration step, ensure your build command is the same as the command you found on Vercel. Also, the deploy command should be the default `npx wrangler deploy`.\n\n## Add a custom domain\n\nWorkers Custom Domains only supports domains that are configured as zones on your account. A zone refers to a domain (such as example.com) that Cloudflare manages for you, including its DNS and traffic.\n\nFollow these instructions for [adding a custom domain to your Workers project](/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains/#add-a-custom-domain). You will also find additional information on creating a zone for your domain.\n\n## Delete your Vercel app\n\nOnce your custom domain is set up and sending requests to Cloudflare Workers, you can safely delete your Vercel application.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\nFor additional migration instructions, review the [Cloudflare Pages to Workers migration guide](/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/migrate-from-pages/). While not Vercel specific, it does cover some additional steps that may be helpful."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/workers/static-assets/migration-guides/vercel-to-workers.mdx"],"digest":[0,"944c0636f92c7f94"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/dnssec-for-primary"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Set up DNSSEC with Cloudflare as Primary"],"description":[0,"With outgoing zone transfers, you keep Cloudflare as your primary DNS provider and use one or more secondary providers for increased availability and fault tolerance."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Set up multi-signer DNSSEC with outgoing zone transfers"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,3],"label":[0,"DNSSEC options"],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application performance"]}],"meta":[0,{"title":[0,"Cloudflare DNS docs"],"description":[0,"Deliver excellent performance and reliability to your domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Example, APIRequest } from \"~/components\";\n\nWith [outgoing zone transfers](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/), you keep Cloudflare as your primary DNS provider and use one or more secondary providers for increased availability and fault tolerance.\n\nIf you want to use DNSSEC with outgoing zone transfers, you should configure [multi-signer DNSSEC](/dns/dnssec/multi-signer-dnssec/). After setting up [Cloudflare as primary](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/setup/), follow the steps below to enable DNSSEC.\n\n## Before you begin\n\nNote that:\n\n- This process requires that your other DNS provider(s) also support multi-signer DNSSEC.\n- Although you can complete a few steps via the dashboard, currently the whole process can only be completed using the API.\n- Enabling **DNSSEC** and **Multi-signer DNSSEC** in [**DNS Settings**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings) only replaces the first step below. You still have to follow the rest of this tutorial to complete the setup.\n\n## Steps\n\n1. Use the [Edit DNSSEC Status endpoint](/api/resources/dns/subresources/dnssec/methods/edit/) to enable DNSSEC and activate multi-signer DNSSEC for your zone. This is done by setting `status` to `active` and `dnssec_multi_signer` to `true`, as in the following example.\n\n<APIRequest\n\tpath=\"/zones/{zone_id}/dnssec\"\n\tmethod=\"PATCH\"\n\tjson={{\n\t\tstatus: \"active\",\n\t\tdnssec_multi_signer: true,\n\t}}\n/>\n\n2. Add the ZSK(s) of your external provider(s) to Cloudflare by creating a DNSKEY record on your zone.\n\n```bash\ncurl 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/dns_records' \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Email: <EMAIL>\" \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Key: <API_KEY>\" \\\n--header \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n--data '{\n  \"type\": \"DNSKEY\",\n  \"name\": \"<ZONE_NAME>\",\n  \"data\": {\n    \"flags\": 256,\n    \"protocol\": 3,\n    \"algorithm\": 13,\n    \"public_key\": \"<PUBLIC_KEY>\"\n  },\n  \"ttl\": 3600\n}'\n```\n\n3. Once the DNSKEY record is transferred out from Cloudflare to your secondary provider, get Cloudflare's ZSK and manually add it to the DNSKEY record.\n\n   Currently, the ZSK is not automatically transferred out. You can use either the API or a query from one of the assigned Cloudflare nameservers to obtain it.\n\n<Example>\n\nAPI example:\n\n```bash\ncurl 'https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/dnssec/zsk' \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Email: <EMAIL>\" \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Key: <API_KEY>\"\n```\n\nCommand line query example:\n\n```sh\n$ dig <ZONE_NAME> dnskey @<CLOUDFLARE_NAMESERVER> +noall +answer | grep 256\n```\n\n</Example>\n\n4. Add DS records to your registrar, one for each provider. You can see your Cloudflare DS record on the [**DNS Settings**](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings) page, under **DS Record**.\n\nThe nameserver settings at your registrar should include the nameservers of all providers you will be using for your multi-signer DNSSEC setup."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/dnssec-for-primary.mdx"],"digest":[0,"314a951185944d20"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/setup"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Setup"],"description":[0,"With outgoing zone transfers, you can keep Cloudflare as your primary DNS provider and use one or more secondary providers for increased availability."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Set up outgoing zone transfers (Cloudflare as Primary)"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,2],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, TabItem, Tabs, APIRequest, DashButton } from \"~/components\";\n\nWith [outgoing zone transfers](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/), you can keep Cloudflare as your primary DNS provider and use one or more secondary providers for increased availability and fault tolerance.\n\n## Before you begin\n\nMake sure your account team has enabled your zone for outgoing zone transfers.\n\nConsider the [expected behaviors](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/transfer-criteria/) for different record types, and review your [existing DNS records](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/create-dns-records/) to make sure all of them have the desired **Proxy status**.\n\nIf using the API, you may also want to [locate your Zone and Account IDs](/fundamentals/account/find-account-and-zone-ids/).\n\n---\n\n## 1. Create TSIG (optional)\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-definition\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-create-dash\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-create-api\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 2. Create Peer DNS Server (optional)\n\nYou only need to create a peer DNS server if you want:\n\n- Your secondary nameservers to receive **NOTIFYs** for changes to your Cloudflare DNS records.\n- A **TSIG** to sign zone transfer requests and **NOTIFYs**.\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\nTo create a peer using the dashboard:\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the account **Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/configurations\" />\n\n2. Go to **DNS Settings**.\n3. Under **DNS Zone Transfers**, for **Peer DNS servers**, select **Create**.\n4. Enter the following information, paying particular attention to:\n   - **IP**: If configured, specifies where Cloudflare sends NOTIFY requests to.\n   - **Port**: Specifies the IP Port for the NOTIFY IP.\n   - **Enable incremental (IXFR) zone transfers**: Does not apply when you are using Cloudflare as your primary DNS provider (Cloudflare zones always accept IXFR requests).\n   - **Link an existing TSIG**: If desired, link the TSIG you [previously created](#1-create-tsig-optional).\n5. Select **Create**.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nTo create a peer DNS server using the API, send a [POST](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/peers/methods/create/) request.\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 3. Link peer to primary zone (optional)\n\nIf you previously [created a peer DNS server](#2-create-peer-dns-server-optional), you should link it to your primary zone.\n\n:::note\n\n<Render file=\"linked-peers-limit\" product=\"dns\" />\n:::\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\nTo link a primary zone to a peer using the dashboard:\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n2. For **DNS Zone Transfers**, select **Manage linked peers**.\n3. Select a peer.\n4. Select **Save**.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nTo link a primary zone to a peer using the API, send a [POST](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/incoming/methods/create/) request with the ID of the peer you [previously created](#2-create-peer-dns-server-optional).\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n:::caution[Multiple peers and TSIG]\nIf you link more than one peer to a zone and at least one of them has TSIG configured, all peers are expected to also use the same TSIG.\n:::\n\n## 4. Update your secondary DNS provider\n\nYour secondary DNS provider should send zone transfer requests (via AXFR or IXFR) to [this IP](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/access-control-lists/cloudflare-ip-addresses/#transfer-ip) on port 53 and from the IP address specified in your [peer configuration](#2-create-peer-dns-server-optional).\n\nIt should also have updated [Access Control Lists (ACLs)](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/access-control-lists/cloudflare-ip-addresses/#allow-range) to prevent NOTIFY messages sent from Cloudflare IP ranges from being blocked.\n\n## 5. Add secondary nameservers within Cloudflare\n\nUsing the information from your secondary DNS provider, [create NS records](/dns/manage-dns-records/how-to/create-dns-records/#create-dns-records) on your zone apex listing your secondary nameservers.\n\nBy default, Cloudflare ignores NS records added to the zone apex. To modify this behavior, enable [multi-provider DNS](/dns/nameservers/nameserver-options/#multi-provider-dns).\n\n:::note\nIf your account [zone defaults](/dns/additional-options/dns-zone-defaults/) are already defined to have **Multi-provider DNS** enabled, this step may not be necessary.\n:::\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n2. Enable **Multi-provider DNS**.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nSend the following `PATCH` request replacing the placeholders with your zone ID and authentication information:\n\n<APIRequest\n\tpath=\"/zones/{zone_id}/dns_settings\"\n\tmethod=\"PATCH\"\n\tjson={{\n\t\tmulti_provider: true,\n\t}}\n/>\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n:::note\nIn case you want to keep Cloudflare as the only authoritative DNS provider, do not enable multi-provider DNS. In this way, your secondary DNS is kept hidden and up-to-date with the Cloudflare primary, as a backup option for disaster recovery scenarios.\n:::\n\n## 6. Enable outgoing zone transfers\n\nWhen you enable outgoing zone transfers, this will send a DNS NOTIFY message to your secondary DNS provider.\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **DNS Settings** page.\n\n   <DashButton url=\"/?to=/:account/:zone/dns/settings\" />\n\n2. For **Outgoing Zone Transfers**, switch the toggle to **On**.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nTo enable outgoing zone transfers using the API, send a [POST](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/outgoing/methods/enable/) request.\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 7. Add secondary nameservers to registrar\n\nAt your registrar, add the nameservers of your secondary DNS provider."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-primary/setup.mdx"],"digest":[0,"2977ce519624b941"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/setup"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Setup"],"description":[0,"With incoming zone transfers, you can keep your primary DNS provider and use Cloudflare as a secondary DNS provider."],"editUrl":[0,true],"head":[1,[[0,{"tag":[0,"title"],"content":[0,"Set up incoming zone transfers (Cloudflare as Secondary)"]}]]],"template":[0,"doc"],"sidebar":[0,{"order":[0,1],"hidden":[0,false],"attrs":[0,{}]}],"pagefind":[0,true],"draft":[0,false],"pcx_content_type":[0,"tutorial"],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"nvjPKg"],"name":[0,"DNS"],"entry":[0,{"title":[0,"DNS"],"url":[0,"/dns/"],"group":[0,"Application domain"],"author":[0,"@cloudflare"]}]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/directory/dns.yaml"],"digest":[0,"b1cf79eab13ece0f"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"feedback":[0,true],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, TabItem, Tabs } from \"~/components\";\n\nWith [incoming zone transfers](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/), you can keep your primary DNS provider and use Cloudflare as a secondary DNS provider.\n\n<Render file=\"secondary-dns-override\" product=\"dns\" /> <br />\n\n## Before you begin\n\n- You should already have a registered domain, set up with your primary DNS provider.\n- Review the available options and plan for how you will use [DNSSEC with Cloudflare as secondary](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/dnssec-for-secondary/).\n- Make sure you have completed the following tasks at your primary DNS provider and at Cloudflare.\n\n### At your primary DNS provider\n\nYour primary DNS provider should allow traffic from the IP address and port specified in your [peer server configuration](#2-create-peer-server).\n\nIt should also have updated [Access Control Lists (ACLs)](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/access-control-lists/cloudflare-ip-addresses/#cloudflare-as-secondary) to prevent zone transfers from being blocked.\n\nWe strongly recommend configuring [DNS NOTIFY](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1996) at your primary DNS provider to ensure your secondary zone on Cloudflare is updated with the most recent changes as quickly as possible. In order to do so, set up [Cloudflare NOTIFY IPs](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/access-control-lists/cloudflare-ip-addresses/#notify-ips) at your primary DNS provider.\n\nYou will also need the following information from your Primary DNS provider:\n\n- **Primary IP address**: The IP address that Cloudflare sends zone transfer requests to (via AXFR or IXFR).\n- **Zone transfer type**: Will zone transfers be full (AXFR) or incremental (IXFR)?\n- **TSIG name** (optional): A descriptive name of the TSIG following domain name syntax ([RFC 8945 section 4.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8945#section-4.2)). <Render file=\"tsig-name-match\" product=\"dns\" />\n- **TSIG secret** (optional): The secret string used to authenticate zone transfers.\n- **TSIG algorithm** (optional): The algorithm used to authenticate zone transfers.\n\n### At Cloudflare\n\nMake sure your account team has enabled your zone for Secondary DNS.\n\nGet the following values from your Cloudflare account:\n\n- [Account ID](/fundamentals/account/find-account-and-zone-ids/)\n- [Zone ID](/fundamentals/account/find-account-and-zone-ids/)\n- [Nameserver names](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/setup/#get-nameserver-names), which should have **secondary** in the name.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Create TSIG (optional)\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-definition\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-create-dash\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\n<Render file=\"tsig-create-api\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 2. Create Peer Server\n\n<Render file=\"create-peer-server\" product=\"dns\" />\n\n## 3. Create the Secondary Zone\n\n<Tabs syncKey=\"dashPlusAPI\"> <TabItem label=\"Dashboard\">\n\nTo create a secondary zone using the dashboard:\n\n1. Log in to the [Cloudflare dashboard](https://dash.cloudflare.com/login) and select your account.\n2. Select **Onboard a domain**.\n3. Enter your zone name and choose **Secondary DNS** (if this option is not available, contact your account team).\n4. Select **Continue**.\n5. Select your plan type.\n6. Choose a value for **Zone refresh**, which controls the number of seconds between zone updates from your primary DNS server.\n   :::caution\n   Cloudflare will not use the REFRESH value inside the SOA record that is served by your primary provider. Instead the value of zone refresh configured for your secondary zone on Cloudflare will be used to determine the interval after which the SOA serial of the primary zone will be checked for changes.\n   :::\n7. Select the peer server you [previously created](#2-create-peer-server). If needed, you can link more than one peer server to a zone.\n   :::note\n   <Render file=\"linked-peers-limit\" product=\"dns\" />\n   :::\n8. Select **Continue**.\n9. Review the list of transferred records and select **Continue**.\n   :::note\n   If no records appear, you may have misconfigured the TSIG or the IP address of the peer server or the [Access Control List](/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/access-control-lists/cloudflare-ip-addresses/#cloudflare-as-secondary) was improperly configured at your primary DNS provider.\n   :::\n10. Select **Initiate zone transfer**.\n\n</TabItem> <TabItem label=\"API\">\n\nTo create a secondary zone using the API, send a [POST](/api/resources/dns/subresources/zone_transfers/subresources/incoming/methods/create/) request with the `type` parameter set to `\"secondary\"`.\n\n</TabItem> </Tabs>\n\n## 4. Update registrar\n\nAt your registrar, add the secondary nameservers [specified in the Cloudflare dashboard](/dns/zone-setups/full-setup/setup/#get-nameserver-names). Do not remove your primary DNS provider's nameservers.\n\nWhen you have added the Cloudflare nameservers, go into your new secondary zone and select **Done, check nameservers**.\n\n## 5. Create notifications (optional)\n\nTo increase the reliability of your incoming zone transfers, [set up notifications](/notifications/get-started/#create-a-notification) to be notified when your primaries are failing, when records are updated, [and more](/notifications/notification-available/#dns).\n\n## 6. Proxy traffic through Cloudflare (optional)\n\n<Render file=\"secondary-dns-override\" product=\"dns\" />"],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/dns/zone-setups/zone-transfers/cloudflare-as-secondary/setup.mdx"],"digest":[0,"51e11d618e0233a2"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"workers-ai/guides/tutorials/image-generation-playground/image-generator-flux-newmodels"],"data":[0,{"title":[0,"Add
