for Platforms"]]]}],"body":[0,"import { Render, APIRequest } from \"~/components\";\n\n<Render file=\"csr-definition\" product=\"ssl\" />\n\nOnce the CSR has been generated, provide it to your customer. Your customer will then pass it along to their preferred CA to obtain a certificate and return it to you. After you receive the certificate, you should upload it to Cloudflare and reference the unique CSR ID that was provided to you during CSR creation.\n\n<Render\n\tfile=\"ssl-for-saas-plan-limitation\"\n\tproduct=\"cloudflare-for-platforms\"\n/>\n\n---\n\n## Generate the private key and CSR\n\n### 1. Build the CSR payload\n\nAll fields except for organizational_unit and key_type are required. If you do not specify a `key_type`, the default of `rsa2048` (RSA 2048 bit) will be used; the other option is `p256v1` (NIST P-256).\n\nCommon names are restricted to 64 characters and subject alternative names (SANs) are limited to 255 characters, [per RFC 5280](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280). You must specify at least one SAN, and the list of SANs should include the common name.\n\n```bash\nrequest_body=$(< <(cat <<EOF\n{\n  \"country\": \"US\",\n  \"state\": \"MA\",\n  \"locality\": \"Boston\",\n  \"organization\": \"City of Boston\",\n  \"organizational_unit\": \"Championship Parade Detail\",\n  \"common_name\": \"app.example.com\",\n  \"sans\": [\n    \"app.example.com\",\n    \"www.example.com\",\n    \"blog.example.com\",\n    \"example.com\"\n  ],\n  \"key_type\": \"p256v1\"\n}\nEOF\n))\n```\n\n### 2. Generate a CSR\n\nNow, you want to generate a CSR that you can provide to your customer.\n\n```bash\ncurl https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/custom_csrs \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Email: <EMAIL>\" \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Key: <API_KEY>\" \\\n--header \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n--data \"$request_body\"\n\n# Response:\n{\n  \"result\": {\n    \"id\": \"7b163417-1d2b-4c84-a38a-2fb7a0cd7752\",\n    \"country\": \"US\",\n    \"state\": \"MA\",\n    \"locality\": \"Boston\",\n    \"organization\": \"City of Boston\",\n    \"organizational_unit\": \"Championship Parade Detail\",\n    \"common_name\": \"app.example.com\",\n    \"sans\": [\n      \"app.example.com\",\n      \"www.example.com\",\n      \"blog.example.com\",\n      \"example.com\",\n    ],\n    \"key_type\": \"p256v1\",\n    \"csr\": \"-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----\\nMIIBSzCB8gIBADBiMQswaQYDVQQGEwJVUzELMAkGA1UECBMCTUExDzANBgNVBAcT\\nBkJvc3RvbjEaMBgGA1UEChMRQ2l0eSBvZiBDaGFtcGlvbnMxGTAXBgNVBAMTEGNz\\nci1wcm9kLnRscy5mdW4wWTATBgcqhkjOPQIBBggqhkjOPQMBBwNCAaTKf70NYlwr\\n20P6P8xj8/4mTN5q28dbZR/gM3u4m/RPs24+PxAfMZCNvkVKAPVWYfUAadZI4Ha/\\ndxLh5Q6X5bhIoC4wLAYJKoZIhvcNAQkOMR8wHTAbBqNVHREEFDASghBjc3ItcHJv\\nZC50bHMuZnVuMAoGCCqGSM49BAMCA0gAMEUCIQDgtFUZav466SbT2FGBsIBlahDI\\nVkg4y+u+V/K5DlY1+gIgQ9xLfUSKnSnJYbM9TwWr4Z964+lBtB9af4O5pp7/PSA=\\n-----END CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----\\n\"\n  },\n  \"success\": true\n```\n\nReplace the `\\n` characters with actual newlines before passing to your customer. This can be accomplished by piping the output of the prior call to a tool like jq and perl, such as:\n\n```bash\ncurl https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/custom_csrs \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Email: <EMAIL>\" \\\n--header \"X-Auth-Key: <API_KEY>\" \\\n--header \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n--data \"$request_body\" | jq .result.csr | perl -npe s'/\\\\n/\\n/g; s/\"//g' > csr.txt\n```\n\n### 3. Customer obtains certificate\n\nYour customer will take the provided CSR and work with their CA to obtain a signed, publicly trusted certificate.\n\n### 4. Upload the certificate\n\nUpload the certificate and reference the ID that was provided when you generated the CSR.\n\nYou should replace newlines in the certificate with literal `\\n` characters, as illustrated above in the custom certificate upload example. After doing so, build the request body and provide the ID that was returned in a previous step.\n\nCloudflare only accepts publicly trusted certificates. If you attempt to upload a self-signed certificate, it will be rejected.\n\n```bash\n$ MYCERT=\"$(cat app_example_com.pem|perl -pe 's/\\r?\\n/\\\\n/'|sed -e 's/..$//')\"\n\n$ request_body=$(< <(cat <<EOF\n{\n  \"hostname\": \"app.example.com\",\n  \"ssl\": {\n    \"custom_csr_id\": \"7b163417-1d2b-4c84-a38a-2fb7a0cd7752\",\n    \"custom_certificate\": \"$MYCERT\"\n  }\n}\nEOF\n))\n```\n\nWith the request body built, [create the custom hostname](/api/resources/custom_hostnames/methods/create/) with the supplied custom certificate. If you intend to use the certificate with multiple hostnames, make multiple API calls replacing the `hostname` field.\n\n---\n\n## Other actions\n\n### List all CSRs\n\nYou can request the (paginated) collection of all previously generated custom CSRs by making a `GET` request to `https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/custom_csrs`.\n\n### Delete a CSR\n\nDelete one or more of the CSRs to delete the underlying private key by making a `DELETE` request to `https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/{zone_id}/custom_csrs/{csr_id}`.\n\nYou may delete a CSR provided there are no custom certificates using the private key that was generated for the CSR. If you attempt to delete a CSR whose private key is still in use, you will receive an error."],"filePath":[0,"src/content/docs/cloudflare-for-platforms/cloudflare-for-saas/security/certificate-management/custom-certificates/certificate-signing-requests.mdx"],"digest":[0,"8e00772ceff8d1ee"],"deferredRender":[0,true],"collection":[0,"docs"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"app-sec-get-started"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"1a426a3ae597ae3935eb97b5f97f106f"],"url":[0,"app-sec-get-started"],"title":[0,"Application Security - Get started guide"],"description":[0,"In this video, learn how to get immediate protection against the most common attacks."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"transcript":[0,"\nWEBVTT\n\n1\n00:00:00.080 --> 00:00:04.680\nCloudflare Application Security Dashboard \nallows you to manage application security\n\n2\n00:00:04.720 --> 00:00:08.800\nfeatures that protect your domains and \napplications from various web attacks and\n\n3\n00:00:08.800 --> 00:00:13.440\nthreats. In this video, \nyou'll learn how to get immediate protection\n\n4\n00:00:13.440 --> 00:00:15.400\nagainst the most common attacks.\n\n5\n00:00:16.040 --> 00:00:20.080\nUse analytics to monitor and investigate \ntraffic patterns and events.\n\n6\n00:00:20.640 --> 00:00:22.200\nHandle false positives.\n\n7\n00:00:22.960 --> 00:00:24.960\nCreate custom rules with filters.\n\n8\n00:00:25.520 --> 00:00:29.000\nDepending on your plan, \nsome functionalities may or may not be\n\n9\n00:00:29.000 --> 00:00:33.440\navailable to you. For more information, \nvisit our developer documentation.\n\n10\n00:00:36.760 --> 00:00:40.080\nBefore you can start reviewing analytics or \nfine tuning rules,\n\n11\n00:00:40.080 --> 00:00:44.240\nyou need to set up and enable rules that will \ninspect and mitigate incoming threats.\n\n12\n00:00:44.800 --> 00:00:47.720\nLet's start by setting up Cloudflare's \nmanaged rule set.\n\n13\n00:00:47.760 --> 00:00:51.840\nThis rule set contains multiple rules \ndesigned to detect known vulnerabilities and\n\n14\n00:00:51.840 --> 00:00:53.200\ncommon attack vectors.\n\n15\n00:00:53.640 --> 00:00:58.400\nIt's updated weekly and also includes \nemergency patches for zero day threats.\n\n16\n00:00:58.840 --> 00:01:02.480\nIf you're on a free plan, \nthe free managed rule set is already deployed\n\n17\n00:01:02.480 --> 00:01:05.220\nby default, so you don't have to do anything \nfor this step.\n\n18\n00:01:05.580 --> 00:01:10.580\nFirst, log in to the Cloudflare dashboard, \nselect your account and domain and go to\n\n19\n00:01:10.620 --> 00:01:15.900\nSecurity Settings. Second, \nin the Web Application Exploits category,\n\n20\n00:01:15.900 --> 00:01:18.620\nlocate the Cloudflare managed rule set and \nturn it on.\n\n21\n00:01:18.940 --> 00:01:22.780\nBy enabling this rule set, \nyou immediately get broad protection with low\n\n22\n00:01:22.820 --> 00:01:25.940\nfalse positives. Once you've enabled this \nrule set,\n\n23\n00:01:26.020 --> 00:01:29.940\nyou can go to the analytics page to start \nunderstanding how your rules are working.\n\n24\n00:01:30.500 --> 00:01:33.100\nKeep in mind the system needs time to collect \ndata,\n\n25\n00:01:33.100 --> 00:01:37.220\nso if you just enable the rule sets, \nallow some time for incoming traffic to be\n\n26\n00:01:37.220 --> 00:01:38.540\nanalyzed by Cloudflare.\n\n27\n00:01:41.380 --> 00:01:43.220\nLet's check out analytics page.\n\n28\n00:01:43.340 --> 00:01:45.460\nThere are two different views in analytics.\n\n29\n00:01:45.860 --> 00:01:49.660\nThe traffic tab shows all incoming HTTP \nrequests to your domain,\n\n30\n00:01:49.660 --> 00:01:53.100\nincluding ones that aren't mitigated by any \nsecurity rules.\n\n31\n00:01:53.420 --> 00:01:57.940\nThe events tab only shows requests that \ntriggered a Cloudflare Security action,\n\n32\n00:01:57.940 --> 00:02:00.220\nsuch as block, challenge, \nor lock.\n\n33\n00:02:00.260 --> 00:02:01.900\nLet's start with the traffic tab.\n\n34\n00:02:02.180 --> 00:02:06.840\nHere you can identify patterns of traffic \nthrough filters like request properties.\n\n35\n00:02:06.880 --> 00:02:10.600\nPath source, IP action taken, \nor rule ID.\n\n36\n00:02:10.840 --> 00:02:12.600\nLet's take a look at an example.\n\n37\n00:02:13.280 --> 00:02:17.200\nSay that you only want to see only post \nrequests for the login path that contain\n\n38\n00:02:17.200 --> 00:02:25.240\nleaked credentials. First click Add filter \nand enter path equals login.\n\n39\n00:02:26.720 --> 00:02:35.200\nClick apply. Then add the following \nadditional filters HTTP method equals post\n\n40\n00:02:35.800 --> 00:02:40.560\nleaked credentials. Scan results equals \nusername and password leaked.\n\n41\n00:02:40.960 --> 00:02:44.000\nHover on served by origin, \nthen click filter.\n\n42\n00:02:44.280 --> 00:02:47.680\nNow that you're filtering by requests that \nhave actually reached origin with leaked\n\n43\n00:02:47.680 --> 00:02:52.040\ncredentials, you notice that these requests \nall come from the same IP address.\n\n44\n00:02:52.680 --> 00:02:56.600\nThe IP address is displayed in the source IP \ncolumn in sample logs.\n\n45\n00:02:56.920 --> 00:03:00.240\nAlso available in top statistics source IPS.\n\n46\n00:03:01.120 --> 00:03:03.080\nLet's add that IP to the filters.\n\n47\n00:03:03.440 --> 00:03:06.120\nSource IP equals to the IP we see here.\n\n48\n00:03:06.820 --> 00:03:10.540\nNot only can you use filters to analyze \nspecific kinds of requests,\n\n49\n00:03:10.540 --> 00:03:14.940\nyou can also use filters as a starting point \nfor creating your own security rules.\n\n50\n00:03:15.260 --> 00:03:18.020\nLet's create a rule using the filters we just \napplied.\n\n51\n00:03:18.020 --> 00:03:22.420\nThis rule will represent a security challenge \nto post requests that contain leaked\n\n52\n00:03:22.420 --> 00:03:25.100\ncredentials coming from the IP address we \nspecified.\n\n53\n00:03:25.500 --> 00:03:27.660\nClick Create Custom Security Rule.\n\n54\n00:03:27.980 --> 00:03:29.740\nA preview side panel will appear.\n\n55\n00:03:30.260 --> 00:03:33.060\nThis preview shows you the beginning of the \nrule you're building.\n\n56\n00:03:33.460 --> 00:03:37.500\nWe still need to decide what action happens \nwhen requests match this rule.\n\n57\n00:03:37.500 --> 00:03:39.780\nSo let's select Configure Rule action.\n\n58\n00:03:39.940 --> 00:03:43.780\nNow you've been brought to the Rule Builder \npage under the Security Rule section.\n\n59\n00:03:46.900 --> 00:03:49.020\nFirst let's give a rule a name.\n\n60\n00:03:49.460 --> 00:03:53.780\nNext you'll see that the rule expression \ncontains the filters you just applied in the\n\n61\n00:03:53.780 --> 00:03:58.620\nanalytics page. The rule expression specifies \nthe conditions that must be met for the rule\n\n62\n00:03:58.620 --> 00:04:03.580\nto run. You can build a rule expression by \neither using the expression builder or by\n\n63\n00:04:03.580 --> 00:04:05.380\nmanually writing the rule expression.\n\n64\n00:04:05.420 --> 00:04:09.270\nNow let's select the action we want this rule \nto perform on matching requests.\n\n65\n00:04:09.630 --> 00:04:13.350\nEach of the challenge actions use \nCloudflare's Challenge platform to verify\n\n66\n00:04:13.350 --> 00:04:15.710\nwhether a visitor to a domain is a real \nhuman,\n\n67\n00:04:15.710 --> 00:04:18.070\nand not a bot or automated script.\n\n68\n00:04:18.350 --> 00:04:22.670\nAn interactive challenge presents visitors \nwith a challenge they need to solve for the\n\n69\n00:04:22.670 --> 00:04:24.110\nrequest to be successful.\n\n70\n00:04:24.150 --> 00:04:28.950\nA JavaScript challenge asks the visitor's web \nbrowser to solve a JavaScript based challenge\n\n71\n00:04:28.950 --> 00:04:32.270\nin the background. Unlike interactive \nchallenges,\n\n72\n00:04:32.270 --> 00:04:35.630\nthis type of challenge does not require \ninteractions from a visitor.\n\n73\n00:04:35.870 --> 00:04:39.870\nA managed challenge allows Cloudflare to \ndynamically choose the appropriate type of\n\n74\n00:04:39.870 --> 00:04:43.510\nchallenge to present to a visitor, \nbased on the characteristics of their\n\n75\n00:04:43.510 --> 00:04:48.830\nrequests. Aside from the challenge actions, \nwe can choose block to completely block all\n\n76\n00:04:48.830 --> 00:04:54.270\nrequests that match this rule or skip to skip \nother rules from a selection of options.\n\n77\n00:04:54.470 --> 00:04:58.390\nEnterprise accounts also have the option to \nlog requests that match a rule.\n\n78\n00:04:58.870 --> 00:05:03.390\nThese logged requests can then be found in \nthe Security Analytics under the events tab.\n\n79\n00:05:03.470 --> 00:05:07.790\nIn this example, we're going to choose \nManaged Challenge as the action to take on\n\n80\n00:05:07.790 --> 00:05:09.270\nrequests that match this rule.\n\n81\n00:05:09.430 --> 00:05:13.930\nLastly, we can use a Select Order dropdown to \nselect whether to place this rule at the\n\n82\n00:05:13.930 --> 00:05:16.250\nbeginning or end of the execution order.\n\n83\n00:05:16.250 --> 00:05:19.810\nRules that match a request are executed in \nthe way they're placed in,\n\n84\n00:05:19.810 --> 00:05:24.450\nstarting from one. If multiple rules match \nwith the request and a preceding rule is a\n\n85\n00:05:24.450 --> 00:05:28.850\nterminating action, such as a block, \nno subsequent rules will be performed.\n\n86\n00:05:29.250 --> 00:05:33.130\nIf you want to place this rule in a specific \nplace of the execution order,\n\n87\n00:05:33.130 --> 00:05:35.570\nwe can edit this after we deploy the rule.\n\n88\n00:05:35.890 --> 00:05:38.450\nNow that we've finished building our rule, \nselect deploy.\n\n89\n00:05:38.730 --> 00:05:42.530\nNow you're at the Security Rules Overview \npage where you manage and create custom\n\n90\n00:05:42.530 --> 00:05:45.650\nsecurity rules. And here's the rule we just \ncreated.\n\n91\n00:05:46.210 --> 00:05:50.970\nIf you ever want to edit a rule you created \nor move its position in the execution order,\n\n92\n00:05:51.130 --> 00:05:54.530\njust press the three dots to the right of the \nrule to find these actions.\n\n93\n00:05:57.610 --> 00:06:02.170\nOccasionally, legitimate requests may also \nget blocked by rule from a managed rule set\n\n94\n00:06:02.170 --> 00:06:04.290\nthat's created and maintained by Cloudflare.\n\n95\n00:06:04.690 --> 00:06:06.570\nThese are known as false positives.\n\n96\n00:06:06.850 --> 00:06:10.250\nLet's head back to the analytics page and go \nto the events tab.\n\n97\n00:06:11.850 --> 00:06:16.550\nFilter by action block and surface managed \nrules.\n\n98\n00:06:16.950 --> 00:06:19.150\nYou can adjust the time frame if necessary.\n\n99\n00:06:19.550 --> 00:06:23.990\nSo in previous 24 hours, \nif you find legitimate traffic being blocked\n\n100\n00:06:23.990 --> 00:06:27.110\nby managed rules, don't just disable the \nentire rule set.\n\n101\n00:06:27.110 --> 00:06:31.310\nFirst, check for common properties between \nblock requests that should be allowed,\n\n102\n00:06:31.310 --> 00:06:33.710\nsuch as the same path like login.\n\n103\n00:06:33.750 --> 00:06:38.750\nThen expand the log details for any of these \nblock requests you'd like to follow and copy\n\n104\n00:06:38.750 --> 00:06:42.630\nthe rule ID. Take note of the managed Rule \nset name to allow these requests.\n\n105\n00:06:42.630 --> 00:06:46.990\nTo bypass this rule, you can either add an \nexception to skip the rule for request to a\n\n106\n00:06:46.990 --> 00:06:51.310\nspecific path, or you can configure an \noverride to disable the rule.\n\n107\n00:06:51.430 --> 00:06:56.430\nLet's see how you would create an exception \nfor specific path in security rules.\n\n108\n00:06:56.430 --> 00:06:59.350\nSelect create rule. Manage rules.\n\n109\n00:07:00.950 --> 00:07:09.830\nEnter a name for the exception in field we \nenter URI path in operator equals in value\n\n110\n00:07:10.070 --> 00:07:15.850\nlogin. Then select Skip specific rules from a \nmanaged rule set and then choose Select\n\n111\n00:07:15.850 --> 00:07:19.690\nRuleset for the managed rule you previously \nidentified.\n\n112\n00:07:19.690 --> 00:07:24.850\nChoose select rules. Search for the rule you \nwant to skip using the rule ID and select it\n\n113\n00:07:24.850 --> 00:07:28.330\nusing the checkbox. Select next.\n\n114\n00:07:28.970 --> 00:07:32.970\nReview your configuration in rules being \nskipped and select deploy.\n\n115\n00:07:34.290 --> 00:07:37.610\nNow let's look at configuring a rule override \nfor that specific rule.\n\n116\n00:07:38.010 --> 00:07:41.450\nThe override will change the rule for all \nincoming requests.\n\n117\n00:07:42.450 --> 00:07:46.170\nSelect the rule name to open the sidebar, \nthen select view.\n\n118\n00:07:46.170 --> 00:07:50.490\nIn Security rules, select browse rules.\n\n119\n00:07:51.490 --> 00:07:54.890\nSearch for the rule you want to skip using \nthe rule ID you copied.\n\n120\n00:07:55.450 --> 00:07:59.530\nTo disable the rule for all requests, \nset the status to off.\n\n121\n00:08:00.130 --> 00:08:02.330\nSelect next and then save.\n\n122\n00:08:02.690 --> 00:08:06.370\nThis keeps your overall protection in place \nwhile allowing valid traffic through for\n\n123\n00:08:06.370 --> 00:08:11.090\nknown cases. You can now set up application \nsecurity features to protect your domains and\n\n124\n00:08:11.090 --> 00:08:15.890\napplications. For more information, \nplease refer to our developer documentation.\n"],"pcx_content_type":[0,"video"],"thumbnail":[0,{"url":[0,"https://pub-d9bf66e086fb4b639107aa52105b49dd.r2.dev/appsec-get-started-guide.png"]}],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"DNS"]]]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/stream/app-sec-get-started/index.yaml"],"digest":[0,"6d381390b85fcf20"],"collection":[0,"stream"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"china-network-inside-china"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"b7933a5b3636ca29f834128ca92665b3"],"url":[0,"china-network-inside-china"],"title":[0,"China network - How to speed up your web traffic inside mainland China"],"description":[0,"In this video, Jess Liu walks us through the main features of Cloudflares China Network. They cover how the China Network works, including integrated caching, in-country China name servers, and compliance with ICP regulations. They also briefly discuss Cloudflare's CDN Global Acceleration (formerly China Express), an option for accelerating dynamic content that cannot be cached."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"china-network"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"transcript":[0,"WEBVTT\n\n1\n00:00:03.650 --> 00:00:08.210\nDo you have users in mainland China who are \nexperiencing high latency accessing your\n\n2\n00:00:08.210 --> 00:00:12.860\nservices? Cloudflare China network solution \ncan help you solve this easily.\n\n3\n00:00:13.310 --> 00:00:18.530\nHello, I'm Jess from Cloudflare here to teach \nyou about our China network products.\n\n4\n00:00:18.530 --> 00:00:23.780\nSometimes when the internet users in mainland \nChina visit your website on Cloudflare global\n\n5\n00:00:23.780 --> 00:00:27.620\nnetwork. They are connected to data centers \noutside of mainland China,\n\n6\n00:00:27.620 --> 00:00:32.210\nand these longer distances will lead to high \nlatency and low availability,\n\n7\n00:00:32.210 --> 00:00:35.240\ncreating a slow and poor user experience.\n\n8\n00:00:35.510 --> 00:00:40.160\nWe can speed up these web services easily \nwith Cloudflare China network solution,\n\n9\n00:00:40.160 --> 00:00:46.530\nwith availability improving from 25% to 100% \nand response time speeding up from 300\n\n10\n00:00:46.530 --> 00:00:49.040\nmilliseconds to just 50 milliseconds.\n\n11\n00:00:49.040 --> 00:00:55.160\nHow first Cloudflare China network provides \ncaching in mainland China through our partner\n\n12\n00:00:55.190 --> 00:00:58.640\nJD cloud, with data centers in 34 cities.\n\n13\n00:00:58.640 --> 00:01:03.530\nThis means for your users in mainland China, \ntheir requests will go to the nearest data\n\n14\n00:01:03.560 --> 00:01:08.570\ncenter inside of mainland China instead of \nany external Cloudflare data center.\n\n15\n00:01:08.600 --> 00:01:11.870\nShorter distance means faster content \ndelivery.\n\n16\n00:01:11.930 --> 00:01:15.980\nThese data centers are fully integrated with \nCloudflare global network.\n\n17\n00:01:16.010 --> 00:01:22.050\nYou'll have access to the same configuration \ntools for cache WAF origin rules and other\n\n18\n00:01:22.050 --> 00:01:24.450\nsettings, all within a single dashboard.\n\n19\n00:01:24.960 --> 00:01:27.330\nThe same goes for name servers in Mainland \nChina.\n\n20\n00:01:27.360 --> 00:01:30.840\nIf the majority of your users are located \nwithin Mainland China,\n\n21\n00:01:30.840 --> 00:01:35.910\nwe offer the option of an Inchina name server \nthat will help speed up DNS resolution.\n\n22\n00:01:36.120 --> 00:01:41.070\nSecond, the China network is designed to \nfollow mainland China's ICP regulations.\n\n23\n00:01:41.070 --> 00:01:46.020\nThis means we can guarantee your web service \nwill always remain accessible to users in\n\n24\n00:01:46.050 --> 00:01:51.240\nmainland China, without interruptions from \nlocal internet service providers or ISPs.\n\n25\n00:01:51.270 --> 00:01:55.500\nIf you're not sure how ICP works, \nour China based partners can help you with\n\n26\n00:01:55.500 --> 00:01:57.840\nthe ICP filing and maintenance.\n\n27\n00:01:57.870 --> 00:02:03.210\nThird, for dynamic content that cannot be \ncached in the data centers within mainland\n\n28\n00:02:03.210 --> 00:02:09.300\nChina, such as API calls or web apps, \nusers may still experience increased latency\n\n29\n00:02:09.300 --> 00:02:12.540\nif your origin is located outside of mainland \nChina.\n\n30\n00:02:12.540 --> 00:02:16.590\nSo for these cases, we offer the option of \nChina Express,\n\n31\n00:02:16.590 --> 00:02:21.870\nwhich will accelerate both DNS resolution and \ntraffic for origin content.\n\n32\n00:02:21.900 --> 00:02:24.840\nWatch our video on China Express to learn \nmore.\n\n33\n00:02:24.870 --> 00:02:29.490\nThus, the China network solutions sound like \nsomething that will help you reach out to our\n\n34\n00:02:29.490 --> 00:02:30.870\nsales team to learn more.\n"],"chapters":[0,{"Introduction":[0,"0s"],"How does it work?":[0,"50s"],"ICP Regulations":[0,"1m35s"],"China an API token"],"description":[0,"In this video, learn the difference between account and user API tokens how to create one."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"fundamentals"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"transcript":[0,"WEBVTT\n\n1\n00:00:00.160 --> 00:00:04.600\nAn API token allows secure, \nfine grained access to specific resources\n\n2\n00:00:04.600 --> 00:00:07.080\nwithout exposing your full account \ncredentials.\n\n3\n00:00:07.080 --> 00:00:10.080\nThey have a limited lifespan and expire \nautomatically.\n\n4\n00:00:10.640 --> 00:00:12.760\nThis reduces risk if they are compromised.\n\n5\n00:00:12.760 --> 00:00:17.280\nFor example, if you want to grant someone \naccess to specific apps or data.\n\n6\n00:00:17.440 --> 00:00:19.440\nYou can create an API token for them.\n\n7\n00:00:19.760 --> 00:00:23.600\nThere are two types of tokens user token and \naccount token.\n\n8\n00:00:23.600 --> 00:00:27.080\nUser tokens are directly tied to an \nindividual and their account.\n\n9\n00:00:27.280 --> 00:00:31.240\nWhile account tokens are typically tied to \nservices which can help prevent an\n\n10\n00:00:31.240 --> 00:00:32.640\ninterruption in service.\n\n11\n00:00:32.680 --> 00:00:34.840\nIf an employee leaves their organization.\n\n12\n00:00:34.960 --> 00:00:39.080\nIn this video, we'll walk through how to \ncreate an API token in the Cloudflare\n\n13\n00:00:39.080 --> 00:00:43.080\ndashboard. Before you begin, \nmake sure you know your account and zone IDs.\n\n14\n00:00:43.080 --> 00:00:46.400\nSo let's do that. First, \nthe account ID identifies your Cloudflare\n\n15\n00:00:46.400 --> 00:00:50.840\naccount, while the zone ID identifies a \nspecific domain you've added to Cloudflare.\n\n16\n00:00:51.000 --> 00:00:55.080\nFrom the accounts page, \nlocate your account at the end of the account\n\n17\n00:00:55.080 --> 00:00:58.240\nrow, open the menu and select Copy Account \nID.\n\n18\n00:00:58.560 --> 00:01:01.200\nIf you only have one account, \nit looks a little bit different.\n\n19\n00:01:01.400 --> 00:01:05.860\nLogin and go to the account home page next to \nyour account name,\n\n20\n00:01:05.860 --> 00:01:08.220\nselect the menu button from the drop down.\n\n21\n00:01:08.220 --> 00:01:13.340\nChoose Copy Account ID to find your zone ID, \nlog in and go to the accounts page.\n\n22\n00:01:13.900 --> 00:01:17.180\nSelect your account, then go to the overview \npage for your domain.\n\n23\n00:01:18.060 --> 00:01:20.580\nScroll to the API section near the bottom.\n\n24\n00:01:20.980 --> 00:01:24.460\nHere you'll see the zone ID and an option to \nclick to copy.\n\n25\n00:01:25.300 --> 00:01:28.500\nThis section also lists your account ID for \nconvenience.\n\n26\n00:01:28.940 --> 00:01:32.620\nNow we can create an API token to create a \ntoken.\n\n27\n00:01:32.660 --> 00:01:34.420\nStart from the Cloudflare dashboard.\n\n28\n00:01:34.620 --> 00:01:38.220\nFor a user token, go to profile API tokens.\n\n29\n00:01:39.220 --> 00:01:43.580\nFor an account token, \ngo to Manage Account account API tokens.\n\n30\n00:01:43.900 --> 00:01:46.620\nYou will only see this option if you are a \nSuperadmin.\n\n31\n00:01:46.620 --> 00:01:51.780\nSelect Create token. You can choose from \npredefined templates or build a custom token.\n\n32\n00:01:52.180 --> 00:01:55.660\nFor example, let's use the Edit Zone DNS \ntemplate.\n\n33\n00:01:55.660 --> 00:02:00.260\nGive your token a descriptive name such as \nDNS updates for example.com.\n\n34\n00:02:00.860 --> 00:02:04.500\nThe template will fulfill permissions, \nbut you can adjust them as needed.\n\n35\n00:02:04.700 --> 00:02:06.620\nNext assign permissions.\n\n36\n00:02:06.900 --> 00:02:12.190\nPermissions are organized by account, \nuser or zone and usually offer either read\n\n37\n00:02:12.190 --> 00:02:13.790\naccess or edit access.\n\n38\n00:02:13.830 --> 00:02:17.590\nEdit allows full control, \ncreate, read, update,\n\n39\n00:02:17.590 --> 00:02:21.030\ndelete, and list, while read provides viewing \nrights only.\n\n40\n00:02:21.510 --> 00:02:24.510\nThen select the resources the token applies \nto.\n\n41\n00:02:24.790 --> 00:02:30.510\nFor instance, granting zone DNS read access \nfor example.com will let the token view DNS\n\n42\n00:02:30.510 --> 00:02:32.070\nrecords for that zone only.\n\n43\n00:02:32.590 --> 00:02:36.070\nIf the token is used on a different zone, \nit will return an error.\n\n44\n00:02:36.550 --> 00:02:41.550\nOptionally, you can add restrictions such as \nfiltering by client IP or setting a time to\n\n45\n00:02:41.590 --> 00:02:43.110\nlive value for the token.\n\n46\n00:02:43.350 --> 00:02:46.190\nClick continue to summary to review your \nselections.\n\n47\n00:02:46.430 --> 00:02:49.310\nIf everything looks correct, \nchoose Create Token.\n\n48\n00:02:49.350 --> 00:02:52.150\nThe dashboard will now display your new token \nsecret.\n\n49\n00:02:52.310 --> 00:02:54.910\nCopy it immediately to a secure location.\n\n50\n00:02:54.950 --> 00:02:56.990\nThe secret is shown only once.\n\n51\n00:02:57.150 --> 00:03:00.230\nAnyone with a token can perform the actions \nyou've authorized,\n\n52\n00:03:00.230 --> 00:03:01.710\nso treat it like a password.\n\n53\n00:03:02.030 --> 00:03:06.550\nWith that, you've successfully created an API \ntoken and can begin using it with a\n\n54\n00:03:06.550 --> 00:03:11.070\nCloudflare API. For more information, \nvisit our developer documentation.\n"],"pcx_content_type":[0,"video"],"thumbnail":[0,{"url":[0,"https://pub-d9bf66e086fb4b639107aa52105b49dd.r2.dev/API%20token.png"]}],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Cloudflare Fundamentals"]]]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/stream/create-api-tokens/index.yaml"],"digest":[0,"d5c953439ba9959b"],"collection":[0,"stream"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"app-sec-dashboard"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"81d50c9845612128e65bf6d04bcf9e3a"],"url":[0,"app-sec-dashboard"],"title":[0,"Application Security - Dashboard walkthrough"],"description":[0,"In this video, learn how to navigate the Cloudflare Application Security dashboard and how to use each page to monitor, investigate, and manage security protections."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"dns"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"transcript":[0,"\nWEBVTT\n\n1\n00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:04.560\nCloudflare Application Security dashboard for \nindividual domains allows you to manage\n\n2\n00:00:04.560 --> 00:00:08.800\napplication security features that protect \nthe domain from various web attacks and\n\n3\n00:00:08.800 --> 00:00:11.000\nthreats. I'll walk you through the interface,\n\n4\n00:00:11.000 --> 00:00:15.360\nshow you what goes under them and how to use \neach page to monitor,\n\n5\n00:00:15.360 --> 00:00:18.200\ninvestigate, and manage your security \nprotections.\n\n6\n00:00:18.240 --> 00:00:22.800\nFirst, select the domain you would like to \nmanage your application security on.\n\n7\n00:00:23.400 --> 00:00:27.880\nOnce you have selected a domain, \nopen up the Application Security dashboard by\n\n8\n00:00:27.880 --> 00:00:31.240\nclicking security on the navigation bar here.\n\n9\n00:00:31.240 --> 00:00:32.800\nNow on the overview page.\n\n10\n00:00:33.120 --> 00:00:37.600\nThe Security Overview page gives you a high \nlevel summary of your security posture for\n\n11\n00:00:37.600 --> 00:00:42.760\nthis domain. You'll find alerts for \nMisconfigurations detected threats and\n\n12\n00:00:42.760 --> 00:00:46.200\nsuggested actions on how to improve your \nsecurity posture.\n\n13\n00:00:46.560 --> 00:00:50.640\nThis is a good place to start and review what \nneeds attention across your environment.\n\n14\n00:00:50.680 --> 00:00:56.200\nUnder analytics, you can analyze security \ndata across two different views traffic and\n\n15\n00:00:56.200 --> 00:01:01.360\nevents. Use this page to understand what kind \nof traffic your domain is receiving and how\n\n16\n00:01:01.400 --> 00:01:05.580\nCloudflare is securing your domain based on \nthe detection tools that are enabled.\n\n17\n00:01:06.020 --> 00:01:11.100\nThis page also serves as a starting point to \nidentify how you may want to configure your\n\n18\n00:01:11.100 --> 00:01:15.300\napplication security settings based on the \npatterns of traffic you're receiving.\n\n19\n00:01:15.340 --> 00:01:19.300\nThe traffic tab shows data on incoming HTTP \nrequests to your domain,\n\n20\n00:01:19.300 --> 00:01:23.220\nincluding ones that have not been affected by \nany security tools.\n\n21\n00:01:23.260 --> 00:01:27.480\nThe events tab shows data on security actions \nthat have been performed by Cloudflare\n\n22\n00:01:27.480 --> 00:01:29.700\nSecurity tools on incoming requests.\n\n23\n00:01:29.860 --> 00:01:35.180\nUnder web assets, you can manage and monitor \nthe security of various types of web related\n\n24\n00:01:35.180 --> 00:01:40.860\nassets. For example, use the endpoints tab to \nget recommendations and insights about your\n\n25\n00:01:40.860 --> 00:01:45.540\nendpoint usage. Use the Schema Validation tab \nto protect your origin from invalid API\n\n26\n00:01:45.540 --> 00:01:47.260\nrequests and malicious payloads.\n\n27\n00:01:47.500 --> 00:01:52.100\nSchema validation works by validating \nrequests against your own API schema.\n\n28\n00:01:52.140 --> 00:01:56.540\nThe Client Side Resources tab is used to \nmonitor resources such as scripts,\n\n29\n00:01:56.540 --> 00:02:01.380\nconnections, and cookies that are running on \nyour visitor's web browsers for your domain.\n\n30\n00:02:01.380 --> 00:02:05.090\nIf you notice unexpected scripts or \nconnections on the dashboard.\n\n31\n00:02:05.130 --> 00:02:07.730\nCheck them here for signs of malicious \nactivity.\n\n32\n00:02:07.770 --> 00:02:11.610\nSecurity rules is where you manage and define \nwhat security actions are.\n\n33\n00:02:11.610 --> 00:02:13.450\nPerform on incoming requests.\n\n34\n00:02:13.490 --> 00:02:15.290\nUnder the Security Rules tab.\n\n35\n00:02:15.330 --> 00:02:18.770\nYou can manage and create different types of \nrules to secure your domain.\n\n36\n00:02:18.810 --> 00:02:23.650\nYou can create your own custom security rules \nfrom scratch or use predefined templates to\n\n37\n00:02:23.690 --> 00:02:26.930\nhelp you get started. Under the DDoS \nprotection tab,\n\n38\n00:02:26.930 --> 00:02:30.890\nyou'll find a DDoS rulesets that mitigate \nDDoS attacks on your domain.\n\n39\n00:02:30.930 --> 00:02:35.050\nThe DDoS Attack Protection rule sets are \nautomatically managed and updated by\n\n40\n00:02:35.050 --> 00:02:39.650\nCloudflare, so you only need to use this tab \nif you want to create an override rule that\n\n41\n00:02:39.650 --> 00:02:42.410\nchanges how Cloudflare DDoS protection \nfunctions.\n\n42\n00:02:42.450 --> 00:02:46.770\nIn settings, you can configure Cloudflare \ndetection tools and your domain security\n\n43\n00:02:46.770 --> 00:02:52.090\nposture. Security settings and detection \ntools are categorized by the type of threat\n\n44\n00:02:52.130 --> 00:02:53.690\nthey detect and mitigate.\n\n45\n00:02:53.850 --> 00:02:57.570\nUse the filters on this page to find \ndetection tools and settings that are\n\n46\n00:02:57.570 --> 00:02:59.490\nrelevant to your security needs.\n\n47\n00:02:59.690 --> 00:03:01.290\nThank you for choosing Cloudflare.\n\n48\n00:03:01.330 --> 00:03:04.930\nFor more information, \nplease refer to our developer CDN global acceleration for Mainland China"],"description":[0,"In this video, Jess Liu discusses Cloudflares CDN Global Acceleration (formerly China Express), including solutions for high latency on dynamic content, accelerating API calls, accessing Cloudflare One services like WARP and Magic WAN from within mainland China, and securely connecting private enterprise networks."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"china-network"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"transcript":[0,"\nWEBVTT\n\n1\n00:00:03.620 --> 00:00:08.780\nIn the last video, we discussed that if your \norigin is outside of mainland China and has\n\n2\n00:00:08.780 --> 00:00:11.630\ndynamic content that cannot be cashed within \nChina,\n\n3\n00:00:11.660 --> 00:00:14.930\nyour users may still encounter increased \nlatency,\n\n4\n00:00:14.960 --> 00:00:17.240\nleading to poor user experience.\n\n5\n00:00:17.270 --> 00:00:22.430\nIn this video, we'll go over three main use \ncases that we can help address dynamic\n\n6\n00:00:22.430 --> 00:00:24.500\ncontent outside of mainland China.\n\n7\n00:00:24.530 --> 00:00:28.430\nAccess to global services and private network \nconnectivity.\n\n8\n00:00:28.430 --> 00:00:32.450\nSo depending on your use case, \nyou can choose what best suits your needs.\n\n9\n00:00:32.480 --> 00:00:37.340\nHello, I'm Jez from Cloudflare here to teach \nyou about our Cloudflare solutions for\n\n10\n00:00:37.370 --> 00:00:42.530\nmainland China. If your origin is outside of \nmainland China and has dynamic content that\n\n11\n00:00:42.530 --> 00:00:46.550\ncannot be cached within China, \nCN Global Acceleration,\n\n12\n00:00:46.550 --> 00:00:51.080\nformerly known as China Express, \ncan help you with this by leveraging\n\n13\n00:00:51.080 --> 00:00:56.060\ndedicated private tunnels established through \nour trusted local partners including JD\n\n14\n00:00:56.060 --> 00:01:01.430\ncloud, CME and CBC tech, \nwe ensure seamless and efficient content\n\n15\n00:01:01.430 --> 00:01:03.770\ndelivery. Here's how it works.\n\n16\n00:01:03.770 --> 00:01:08.210\nTraffic received from our China Network data \ncenters is routed to our premium data\n\n17\n00:01:08.280 --> 00:01:12.420\ncenters, which have global acceleration \nprivileges that are designed to handle\n\n18\n00:01:12.420 --> 00:01:14.190\ndynamic traffic with ease.\n\n19\n00:01:14.520 --> 00:01:18.060\nThis includes typical use cases like web API \ncalls,\n\n20\n00:01:18.060 --> 00:01:23.400\nas well as more complex scenarios such as \ndynamic traffic management or multi origin\n\n21\n00:01:23.400 --> 00:01:28.650\nconfigurations. For example, \ncustomers using Cloudflare SaaS features like\n\n22\n00:01:28.650 --> 00:01:34.050\nR2 for asset storage can integrate these \ncapabilities into our solutions seamlessly.\n\n23\n00:01:34.080 --> 00:01:38.880\nOnce processed, the traffic is transferred \nthrough a partner's dedicated private tunnel\n\n24\n00:01:38.910 --> 00:01:43.710\nto the global public internet, \nensuring high performance and reliability.\n\n25\n00:01:43.860 --> 00:01:48.690\nBesides accelerating dynamic content, \nyou can also reliably access Cloudflare One\n\n26\n00:01:48.690 --> 00:01:52.230\nservices like Warp, Magic WAN, \nand travel SIM cards.\n\n27\n00:01:52.230 --> 00:01:56.700\nWith Warp Global acceleration, \nWarp client can be extended to China for your\n\n28\n00:01:56.700 --> 00:02:01.770\nusers under Pre-signed agreements to access \nthe same Zero Trust Network access services,\n\n29\n00:02:01.770 --> 00:02:06.930\njust as the rest of your global users, \nwhile experiencing stable connectivity\n\n30\n00:02:06.930 --> 00:02:11.040\nanywhere within China, \nno matter if you're at home or a coffee shop.\n\n31\n00:02:11.070 --> 00:02:15.730\nOnce you receive our dedicated China IPS \nthrough Cloudflare and have Warp setup up,\n\n32\n00:02:15.730 --> 00:02:18.130\nall you need to do is override the following.\n\n33\n00:02:18.160 --> 00:02:20.110\nWarp endpoints for each device.\n\n34\n00:02:20.140 --> 00:02:24.130\nWarp endpoint API endpoint and endpoint.\n\n35\n00:02:24.460 --> 00:02:29.260\nVerify the colocation center is your desired \nlocation under Warp preferences.\n\n36\n00:02:30.370 --> 00:02:33.460\nAnd now you should be able to access global \nservices.\n\n37\n00:02:34.480 --> 00:02:38.740\nAs for users who are temporarily in China, \nwe can offer travel SIM cards,\n\n38\n00:02:38.740 --> 00:02:42.610\nwhich connect to our Warp client for a \nseamless and secure mobile acceleration\n\n39\n00:02:42.610 --> 00:02:47.170\nabroad. By inserting the SIM card or applying \nthe eSIM to an employee's phone,\n\n40\n00:02:47.170 --> 00:02:50.200\ncorporate devices, whether it be laptops or \ntablets,\n\n41\n00:02:50.230 --> 00:02:54.400\ncan then connect to their organization's Warp \nclient through mobile hotspot.\n\n42\n00:02:54.430 --> 00:02:58.810\nLastly, if your goal is to scale and \naccelerate private enterprise networks from\n\n43\n00:02:58.810 --> 00:03:02.410\nChina, Magic WAN Global Acceleration can help \nyou with that.\n\n44\n00:03:02.410 --> 00:03:05.170\nYou can securely connect to any traffic \nsource,\n\n45\n00:03:05.170 --> 00:03:08.830\nsuch as offices and data centers to \nCloudflare network,\n\n46\n00:03:08.830 --> 00:03:12.850\nwhile configuring routing policies tailored \nto your corporate networking needs from\n\n47\n00:03:12.850 --> 00:03:18.250\nChina. To extend Magic WAN into China, \nwe provide a customer premises equipment or a\n\n48\n00:03:18.280 --> 00:03:22.900\nCPE device which will be shipped to your \nChina based location once the CPE is\n\n49\n00:03:22.920 --> 00:03:25.470\nconnected to the internet in your physical \nlocation.\n\n50\n00:03:25.470 --> 00:03:29.910\nTraffic from users who are using the same \ninternet in that location will be routed\n\n51\n00:03:29.910 --> 00:03:34.710\nthrough our dedicated private tunnel to any \nresource or service to make the process\n\n52\n00:03:34.710 --> 00:03:37.830\neffortless. Our team will help you in \nconfiguring the CPE.\n\n53\n00:03:38.070 --> 00:03:43.650\nTo sum up. Our solutions are designed to meet \nyour specific needs and can be selected a la\n\n54\n00:03:43.650 --> 00:03:46.950\ncarte if you need to optimize dynamic data \ndelivery,\n\n55\n00:03:46.950 --> 00:03:52.380\nwhether it's API calls or to integrations or \nspecial use cases within a China network\n\n56\n00:03:52.380 --> 00:03:56.130\nzone. Cdn global acceleration is the ideal \nchoice.\n\n57\n00:03:56.160 --> 00:04:01.170\nLooking to connect to global services across \nChina with company enforced policies?\n\n58\n00:04:01.170 --> 00:04:07.050\nChoose Warp Global Acceleration for managing \nEnterprise networking or accessing global\n\n59\n00:04:07.050 --> 00:04:09.420\nservices from your China based offices.\n\n60\n00:04:09.450 --> 00:04:12.960\nMagic WAN global acceleration is your go to \nsolution.\n\n61\n00:04:13.830 --> 00:04:17.340\nThanks for watching. You can reach out to our \nsales team to learn more.\n"],"chapters":[0,{"Introduction and overview of Cloudflare solutions for China":[0,"0s"],"Dynamic content outside of Mainland China":[0,"38s"],"Access to global services":[0,"01m43s"],"Private network connectivity":[0,"02m54s"],"China Network use case and solution overview":[0,"03m43s"]}],"pcx_content_type":[0,"video"],"thumbnail":[0,{"url":[0,"https://pub-d9bf66e086fb4b639107aa52105b49dd.r2.dev/2-China-Express-thumbnail.png"]}],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"China Network"]]]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/stream/china-network-acceleration/index.yaml"],"digest":[0,"6e469ae3153c4299"],"collection":[0,"stream"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"life-of-a-request-3"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"789b42ba4403b32caf54d09a5e5987fd"],"url":[0,"life-of-a-request-3"],"title":[0,"Life of a Request: The Finish Line - Serving the Content"],"description":[0,"Once a request is routed to the nearest Cloudflare data center and then intelligently sped along its way, it moves to the final leg of the journey and how we serve content faster than ever."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"fundamentals"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"pcx_content_type":[0,"video"],"thumbnail":[0,{"url":[0,"https://pub-d9bf66e086fb4b639107aa52105b49dd.r2.dev/lor-3-the-finish-line.png"]}],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Cloudflare Fundamentals"]]]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/stream/life-of-a-request-3/index.yaml"],"digest":[0,"caa952fef3d19faa"],"collection":[0,"stream"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"life-of-a-request-4"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"091109ce8199b511d2f840cfd3a6dae2"],"url":[0,"life-of-a-request-4"],"title":[0,"Life of a Request: The Control Tower - Steering the Life of a Request"],"description":[0,"The Life of a Request is automatic, but you are the conductor. From DNS Resolution to Origin Delivery, watch how to use Cloudflare rules and Workers to intercept, modify, and route traffic at every stage. Turn a standard lifecycle into a fully programmable workflow tailored to your specific business needs."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"fundamentals"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"pcx_content_type":[0,"video"],"thumbnail":[0,{"url":[0,"https://imagedelivery.net/xDOJvHcv1KwTQn6S-BGFIw/28ece432-2dcd-419b-891b-43bf9db22e00/public"]}],"productTitles":[1,[[0,"Cloudflare Fundamentals"]]]}],"filePath":[0,"src/content/stream/life-of-a-request-4/index.yaml"],"digest":[0,"52bf2de2839268e7"],"collection":[0,"stream"]}],[0,{"id":[0,"load-balancing-setup"],"data":[0,{"id":[0,"ce39fdf599aed3661f60e8f780761808"],"url":[0,"load-balancing"],"title":[0,"How to set up a load balancer"],"description":[0,"Unexpected traffic spikes can crash your website, but Cloudflare Load Balancing helps keep your apps online and fast. We'll also explain the difference between public and private load balancers and guide you through using session affinity, fallback pools, and proxy modes."],"products":[1,[[0,{"id":[0,"load-balancing"],"collection":[0,"directory"]}]]],"chapters":[0,{"Introduction to Load Balancing":[0,"0s"],"Step 1 - Select Hostname and Basic
